### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"List any six commonly used plastic packaging materials and their primary food application areas."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** – Carbonated soft drink bottles, water bottles, ovenable trays.
2. **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** – Milk jugs, juice bottles, grocery bags.
3. **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)** – Bread bags, squeeze bottles, frozen vegetable bags.
4. **PP (Polypropylene)** – Yogurt cups, ready-meal trays, bottle caps.
5. **PS (Polystyrene)** – Foam meat trays, vending cups, yogurt containers.
6. **EVOH (Ethylene-Vinyl Alcohol)** – Oxygen-barrier layer in ketchup bottles, vacuum pouches.
7. **PA (Nylon-6)** – Vacuum bags, retort pouches, cheese packaging.

*Marking split:* ½ mark per correct pair (material + application) × 6 = 3 marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Explain the term 'Overall Migration Limit' as applicable to food contact materials."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** is the maximum permitted quantity of non-volatile substances that can transfer from a packaging material into a food simulant under defined test conditions (10 days at 40 °C, or equivalent accelerated conditions as per EU Regulation 10/2011 and FSSAI 2020 amendment).

- **Value:** **10 mg of total migrant per dm² of food-contact surface area.**
- **Purpose:** Ensures the packaging does not transfer excessive chemicals to the food.
- **Test:** Performed using food simulants — **distilled water** (aqueous), **3% acetic acid** (acidic), **10% ethanol** (alcoholic), and **olive oil or iso-octane** (fatty foods).
- **Unit:** mg/dm² (mass per contact area).

*Marking split:* 1 mark definition, 1 mark value + unit, 1 mark purpose/food simulants.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Instruction (KTU 2024):** *Answer any one full question from Module 2. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Set A]***

**(a) Classify food packaging materials in detail with suitable examples for each class. Discuss the advantages and limitations of glass as a food packaging material.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification (4 marks):**

Food packaging materials are classified into four broad classes:

- **(i) Traditional / Rigid Materials** (1 mark)
  - Glass, metals, paper, wood.
- **(ii) Plastic / Polymeric Materials** (1 mark)
  - Single-polymer films (PET, HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS, EVOH, PA, PVC).
  - Multilayer laminates (Tetra Pak, retort pouches, metallized BOPP).
- **(iii) Bio-based / Sustainable** (1 mark)
  - PLA, PHB, PHA, starch blends, edible films (chitosan, alginate, whey).
- **(iv) Active & Intelligent / Composite** (1 mark)
  - Oxygen scavengers, antimicrobial films, TTI labels, bag-in-box systems.

**2. Glass — Advantages (2 marks):**

- **Impermeable** to gases, vapours, aromas, and microorganisms (total barrier).
- **Inert** — no chemical interaction with food.
- **Transparent** — allows product visibility.
- **Sterilizable** — withstands autoclaving, retorting.
- **Recyclable** infinitely without loss of quality (closed-loop).
- **Odourless and tasteless** — does not impart flavour.

**3. Glass — Limitations (1 mark):**

- **Brittle** — fracture under mechanical/thermal shock.
- **Heavy** (density **2.5 g/cm³**) → high transport cost.
- **Energy-intensive** to manufacture (≈ 1500 °C).
- **Slow line speeds** in bottling due to brittleness.

**(b) A 30 µm thick HDPE film is used to package a desiccant-dried product. Calculate the water-vapour transmission rate (WVTR) per day per m². Given D = 1.2 × 10⁻¹² m²/s, S = 0.08 mol/(m³·Pa), Δp = 2400 Pa at 25 °C.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Permeability:**

$$P = D \cdot S = (1.2 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.08 = 9.6 \times 10^{-14} \text{ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Permeation Flux Equation:**

$$J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{-14} \times 2400}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$J = \frac{2.304 \times 10^{-10}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.68 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/(m²·s)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to g/m²·day (M_w of water = 18 g/mol):**

$$J_{WVTR} = 7.68 \times 10^{-6} \times 18 \times 86400$$

$$J_{WVTR} = 7.68 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5552 \times 10^{6} = 11.94 \text{ g/(m²·day)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result: WVTR ≈ 11.94 g/m²·day** — HDPE provides excellent moisture barrier, suitable for **dried product packaging**.

*Marking split:* Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2 = 3 marks, Step 3 = 1 mark, correct answer with units = 1 mark.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Set B]***

**(a) Discuss in detail the various properties of metals (tinplate, aluminum, TFS) used in food packaging. What are the typical lacquer types used internally and why?** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Tinplate (2 marks):**

- **Composition:** Low-carbon steel (≈ 99%) electrolytically coated with **tin (0.5–2.5 µm per side)**.
- **Properties:** Strength of steel + corrosion resistance of tin.
- **Tin coating weight:** standard grades **E1 (1.0 g/m²) to E4 (4.0 g/m²)** for food contact.
- **Application:** Cans for fruits, vegetables, fish, beverages, aerosols.
- **Critical property:** Lacquer compatibility (epoxy phenolic).

**2. Aluminum (2 marks):**

- **Used as:** Rigid cans (2-piece Drawn-and-Ironed), trays, foils (6–30 µm).
- **Properties:** Lightweight (density **2.7 g/cm³**), excellent barrier, formable, reflective.
- **Alloy:** AA 3003, AA 5182 commonly for cans.
- **Lacquer needed** to prevent acid attack (e.g., in fruit juice cans).
- **Recyclable** — saves 95% energy vs. primary production.

**3. TFS / ECCS — Tin-Free Steel (1 mark):**

- **Electrolytic Chromium Coated Steel:** Chromium + chromium oxide layer (≈ 0.01–0.05 µm).
- **Cheaper than tinplate** but **not solderable** → used in **cemented side-seam cans** (e.g., beer cans).
- **Must be lacquered** — no inherent corrosion resistance.

**4. Internal Lacquers (2 marks):**

- **Epoxy phenolic** (e.g., BPA-epoxy): Most common; provides acid resistance, prevents iron dissolution; **withstands 121 °C retort**.
- **Vinyl organosol**: High acid resistance (tomato products, pickles).
- **Phenolic**: Used for **meat, fish, soups** (sulphur-resistant version called **C-enamel**).
- **Epoxy ester**: For **beverage can interiors** (modern non-BPA variants).
- **Function:** Prevent metal–food ion exchange (Fe, Sn, Al leaching), prevent **sulphur staining** (egg, meat), prevent **lacquer blistering** during retort.

**(b) Explain the permeation mechanism through a polymeric packaging film. Derive the steady-state permeation equation starting from Fick's First Law.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mechanism (3 marks):**

The permeation of a gas/vapour through a polymer film occurs in **four sequential steps**:

- **Step 1 — Sorption** at the upstream (high-p) surface.
- **Step 2 — Dissolution** into the polymer matrix (Henry's law).
- **Step 3 — Diffusion** through the polymer (Fick's law, rate-limiting for gases).
- **Step 4 — Desorption / evaporation** at the downstream surface.

**For glass and metals, only Steps 1 & 4 occur — diffusion is essentially zero.**

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**

- Fick's First Law (steady state): $J = -D \, dC/dx$
- Henry's Law at the surfaces: $C_1 = S p_1$ and $C_2 = S p_2$
- Steady state means linear concentration gradient:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{L} = \frac{S(p_2 - p_1)}{L}$$

- Substituting back:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{S(p_2 - p_1)}{L} = \frac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{L}$$

- Defining the **Permeability Coefficient** $P = D \cdot S$:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{L}}$$

*Marking split:* Mechanism = 3 marks (1 per major step + 1 bonus for glass/metal comment), Derivation = 4 marks (1 mark Fick's, 1 mark Henry's, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final boxed equation).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 2 — Packaging Materials):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of WVTR / OTR:** Examiner will cut **½ mark** if units like g/m²·day or cm³/m²·day·atm are missing.
> 2. **Confusing OML (10 mg/dm²) with SML (60 mg/kg food):** These are *different* limits and are *not* interchangeable. Always write "Overall" vs "Specific" before the number.
> 3. **Citing PVC as "safe" for food contact:** It is **restricted in EU/India** due to phthalate plasticizers; mention "regulated use only" to gain the mark.
> 4. **Missing the "EVOH plasticized by water" concept:** If you state EVOH is "the best barrier" without mentioning the **RH-dependence**, you will lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Saying "Aluminum is corrosion-resistant" without lacquer:** Aluminum **must** be lacquered for acidic foods (tomato, pickle); answer is incomplete otherwise.
> 6. **Migration equation mistake:** Crank's equation $M_t = 2 C_s \sqrt{Dt/\pi}$ applies for **short times and infinite sink**. Mentioning these assumptions is essential for full 7 marks.
> 7. **No "recyclability" mention for glass/HDPE/Al:** Modern KTU 2024 grading includes **sustainability keywords** — adding "infinite recyclability of glass" or "circular economy" can fetch bonus marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision List for KTU ESE — Module 2**

**Core Material Families:**
- ☐ **Glass:** SiO₂-based, zero permeability, brittle, heavy (ρ = 2.5 g/cm³), infinitely recyclable.
- ☐ **Tinplate:** Steel + Sn coating, lacquer mandatory, E1–E4 grades.
- ☐ **Aluminum:** Foil 6–30 µm, cans 2-piece D&I alloy AA 3003, lacquer for acidic foods.
- ☐ **TFS (ECCS):** Chromium-coated, not solderable, cement-side-seam cans.
- ☐ **Paper:** Kraft / SBS / FBB / Corrugated (A, B, C, E flutes), gsm = basis weight.
- ☐ **PET:** Tg 76 °C, ρ 1.38, beverage bottles.
- ☐ **HDPE:** Tg −120 °C, ρ 0.95, milk bottles, excellent moisture barrier.
- ☐ **LDPE:** Tg −110 °C, ρ 0.92, bread bags, freezer films.
- ☐ **PP:** Tg −20 to 0 °C, ρ 0.90, microwave, yogurt.
- ☐ **EVOH:** Excellent O₂ barrier when **dry**; sandwiched in laminates.
- ☐ **PA (Nylon-6):** Vacuum bags, retort pouches, retortable at 121 °C.
- ☐ **PLA / PHB / PHA:** Bio-based, compostable, lower Tg/TS than PET.
- ☐ **Edible films:** Chitosan, alginate, whey protein, beeswax; plasticizer glycerol 15–30%.

**Master Equations (must be memorized):**
- ☐ $P = D \cdot S$ (Permeability = Diffusivity × Solubility)
- ☐ $J = P \Delta p / L$ (Steady-state flux)
- ☐ $M_t = 2 C_s \sqrt{Dt/\pi}$ (Crank's short-time migration)
- ☐ $M_\infty = C_{p,0} \cdot L$ (Total migration)

**Key Regulatory Limits:**
- ☐ OML = **10 mg/dm²**
- ☐ SML = substance-specific (e.g., Sb ≤ 0.04 mg/kg, Ba ≤ 1 mg/kg).
- ☐ Tested per **EU 10/2011** / **FSSAI 2020 amendment**.

**Numerical & Application Tips:**
- ☐ Always convert **day to second** (×86400) and **g/mol** when needed.
- ☐ Always include **units** in final answer.
- ☐ For "which material" questions — state **food + spoilage mechanism + barrier needed**.
- ☐ State **assumptions** (steady state, infinite sink, isothermal) in derivations.

**Diagram & Flowchart Memory Aids:**
- ☐ Four-step permeation: **Sorption → Dissolution → Diffusion → Desorption**.
- ☐ Migration model: **Polymer → Boundary layer → Food** (Crank's semi-infinite).
- ☐ Material selection algorithm: **Food → Spoilage → Barrier needed → Material → Migration test → Approval**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Packaging: Different Forms of Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Packaging: Different Forms of Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of food packaging engineering, **packaging** is broadly classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **Functional Position in the Distribution Chain** (i.e., *where* in the supply chain the package operates):
   - **Primary Packaging**
   - **Secondary Packaging**
   - **Tertiary Packaging**

2. **Physical Form / Mechanical Rigidity** (i.e., *what shape* the package takes):
   - **Rigid Packaging**
   - **Semi-Rigid Packaging**
   - **Flexible Packaging**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **"level of packaging"** (primary/secondary/tertiary) and the **"form of packaging"** (rigid/semi-rigid/flexible). Examiners frequently test this distinction as a 3-mark direct question.

### Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU Terminology

| Level | Definition |
|-------|------------|
| **Primary Packaging** | The package in **direct physical contact** with the food product. It is the *innermost* layer that contains, protects, and dispenses the product. |
| **Secondary Packaging** | The package that **groups one or more primary packages together** for retail display, handling, or unitization (e.g., a cardboard carton holding 12 tetra packs of milk). |
| **Tertiary Packaging** | The package used for **bulk handling, warehousing, and transportation** (e.g., stretch-wrapped pallet, corrugated shipping container). |

| Form | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Rigid Packaging** | A package whose **shape and dimensions are fixed** at ambient conditions and do not deform under normal handling stress (e.g., glass bottle, tin can). |
| **Semi-Rigid Packaging** | A package that **resists deformation under its own weight** but can be flexed or shaped with moderate force (e.g., aluminium trays, plastic tubs). |
| **Flexible Packaging** | A package made from **pliable materials** that can be easily bent, folded, or wrapped without losing structural integrity (e.g., pouches, sachets, films). |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Russian Doll" Analogy of Packaging**
>
> Imagine a **Russian Matryoshka (nesting) doll**:
>
> - The **innermost doll** = the food itself. The **first layer wrapping it** = **Primary Packaging** (e.g., the polymer sachet of ketchup).
> - The **next outer doll wrapping several inner ones** = **Secondary Packaging** (e.g., a printed cardboard box containing 10 ketchup sachets).
> - The **outermost crate transporting everything** = **Tertiary Packaging** (e.g., a corrugated fiberboard shipping case on a wooden pallet).
>
> Similarly, think of **physical form** as the *body language* of packaging:
>
> - **Rigid packaging** behaves like a **ceramic mug** — keep its shape no matter what you do.
> - **Semi-rigid packaging** behaves like a **disposable plastic cup** — holds shape when filled, but you can squeeze it.
> - **Flexible packaging** behaves like a **zip-lock pouch** — collapses, folds, and conforms to whatever is inside it.

This dual-axis classification is the **first fundamental taxonomy** that any food packaging engineer must internalize because every subsequent decision — material selection, barrier requirements, machinery choice, and regulatory compliance — flows from these two classifications.

---

## 1.3 Classification Tree at a Glance

```
Food Packaging
├── By Functional Position
│   ├── Primary  (inner, in-contact)
│   ├── Secondary (grouping)
│   └── Tertiary  (logistics)
│
└── By Physical Form
    ├── Rigid       (fixed shape)
    ├── Semi-Rigid  (partially yielding)
    └── Flexible    (pliable films/laminates)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Packaging — Deep Dive

Primary packaging is the **only packaging layer in direct contact with the food**, and therefore it must simultaneously satisfy:

- **Containment** (prevent leakage)
- **Protection** (against light, oxygen, moisture, microbes)
- **Communication** (label, branding, regulatory info)
- **Convenience** (dispensing, resealability)
- **Compatibility** (no migration of harmful substances into food)

### Common Examples

| Food Category | Primary Package Example |
|---------------|-------------------------|
| Milk | Tetra Pak gable-top carton (paperboard + PE + Al foil) |
| Soft drinks | PET bottle (rigid) or aluminium can (rigid) |
| Biscuits | PP/PE flow-wrap (flexible) inside a printed carton |
| Yoghurt | Polystyrene cup with foil lid (semi-rigid) |
| Spices | Pouches/sachets of laminated film (flexible) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** All food contact materials (FCMs) used in primary packaging must comply with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) regulations** and international standards like **FDA 21 CFR** or **EU Regulation 1935/2004**.

---

## 2.2 Secondary Packaging — Deep Dive

Secondary packaging performs **three engineering roles**:

1. **Unitization** — bundling primary packs into a single saleable unit (e.g., 6-pack of bottles, 12-pack of juice boxes).
2. **Brand Communication** — the printed outer carton is often the consumer's *first visual impression* on the retail shelf.
3. **Physical Protection** — provides mechanical cushioning and stacking strength during distribution.

### Typical Materials

- **Corrugated fiberboard** (CFB) — most common; flute types A, B, C, E.
- **Solid bleached sulfate (SBS) paperboard** — for premium printed cartons.
- **Shrink film** (LDPE, PVC) — for multi-pack collation.
- **Corrugated plastic (polypropylene)** — for reusable transit packs.

### Burst Strength of Corrugated Boxes — Empirical Reference

The **Bursting Strength Test (BST)** per TAPPI T810 / IS 7063 is a standard quality metric:

$$BS = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i \cdot t_i \cdot n_f$$

Where:
- $BS$ = Bursting Strength (kPa or kg/cm²)
- $G_i$ = grammage of $i$-th ply (g/m²)
- $t_i$ = thickness factor
- $n_f$ = flute contribution factor (typically 1.0 for B-flute, 1.15 for C-flute)
- $k$ = empirical constant (≈ 0.0001 for SI units)

---

## 2.3 Tertiary Packaging — Deep Dive

Tertiary packaging is the **logistics-layer packaging** and is rarely seen by the end consumer. It optimizes:

- **Stacking efficiency** (cube utilization)
- **Mechanical stability** during transit (vibration, drop, compression)
- **Material handling** by forklifts, conveyors, AGVs

### Components of a Tertiary System

- **Stretch-wrapped pallet** — load secured with stretch film (LLDPE) under tension.
- **Slip sheet** — replaces wooden pallet, made of corrugated plastic or kraft laminated board.
- **Container (IBC — Intermediate Bulk Container)** — for liquids, 1000 L capacity.
- **Strapping and edge protectors** — for load restraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pallet Dimensions (India / ISO):**
> - **ISO Standard Pallet:** $1200 \times 1000$ mm (Euro pallet: $1200 \times 800$ mm).
> - **Indian pallet (IS 6623):** $1200 \times 1000$ mm.
> - **GMA Pallet (US):** $1219 \times 1016$ mm (≈ $48 \times 40$ in).

---

## 2.4 Rigid Packaging — Theoretical Background

### Definition Recap

A **rigid package** exhibits negligible deformation under self-load and normal handling stress. Quantitatively, the **flexural rigidity ($D$)** is the relevant metric:

$$D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

Where:
- $D$ = flexural rigidity per unit width (N·m)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
- $t$ = wall thickness (m)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio

For $D \gg 10^{-3}$ N·m, the package is classified as **rigid**.

### Common Rigid Packaging Types

| Type | Material | Typical Foods |
|------|----------|---------------|
| Glass bottle/jar | Soda-lime silicate glass | Jams, pickles, beverages |
| Metal can (tinplate) | Tin-coated steel + lacquer | Vegetables, fruits, soups |
| Aluminium can | AA 3004/5182 alloy | Soft drinks, beer |
| Rigid plastic bottle | PET, HDPE, PP | Water, milk, sauces |
| Aseptic carton (rigid variant) | Multilayer paperboard/PE/Al | UHT milk, juices |

### Tinplate Can Construction (Reference)

A typical **3-piece tinplate can** has:

$$T_{wall} = T_{steel} + T_{tin} + 2 \cdot T_{lacquer}$$

Where $T_{steel} \approx 0.15$–$0.30$ mm, $T_{tin} \approx 0.4$–$1.0$ μm per side, and $T_{lacquer} \approx 5$–$10$ μm.

---

## 2.5 Semi-Rigid Packaging — Theoretical Background

Semi-rigid packaging lies in the transitional zone:

$$10^{-5} \leq D \leq 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}$$

### Characteristics

- Holds shape when filled with product.
- Can be **thermoformed** into custom geometries.
- Often used for **single-serve** or **portion-controlled** foods.
- Typical materials: **PS, PP, PVC, PET (thin gauge), aluminium foil containers**.

### Common Examples

- Yoghurt cups (PS, PP)
- Margarine tubs (PP)
- Ready-meal trays (CPET — Crystalline PET)
- Aluminium takeaway containers (for bakery, biryani)
- Blister packs for chewing gum/candies

---

## 2.6 Flexible Packaging — Theoretical Background

Flexible packaging uses **thin films, foils, or laminates** with thickness typically:

$$t < 250 \text{ μm (typically 12–100 μm)}$$

### Key Performance Metrics

**1. Tensile Strength** (ASTM D882):

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{[MPa]}$$

**2. Elongation at Break** (ASTM D882):

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

**3. Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)**:

$$\text{OTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}$$

Units: $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$

**4. Water Vapour Transmission Rate (WVTR)**:

$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t \cdot A}$$

Units: $\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$

**5. Puncture Resistance**:

$$P_{res} = \frac{F_{puncture}}{t_{film}}$$

Units: $\text{N/mm}$ or $\text{gf/mil}$

### Common Flexible Structures

| Structure | Layers | Typical Use |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| PE | Mono | Bread bags, fresh produce |
| BOPP/PE | 2-ply | Snack foods |
| PET/Al/PE | 3-ply | Coffee, retort pouches |
| PET/MPET/PE | 3-ply | Dry powders, spices |
| Paper/Al/PE | 3-ply | Juice aseptic pouches |
| PA/PE | 2-ply | Vacuum bags, cheese |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|------|
| 1 | Flexural Rigidity | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | N·m |
| 2 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t$ | $\sigma_t = F/A$ | MPa |
| 3 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b$ | $\varepsilon_b = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | OTR | $\phi_{O_2}$ | $\phi_{O_2} = \Delta m / (\Delta t \cdot A \cdot \Delta p)$ | $\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}\,\text{atm}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | WVTR | $\phi_{H_2O}$ | $\phi_{H_2O} = \Delta m / (\Delta t \cdot A)$ | $\text{g}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Burst Strength | $BS$ | $BS = k \sum G_i t_i n_f$ | kPa |
| 7 | Puncture Resistance | $P_{res}$ | $P_{res} = F_{puncture}/t_{film}$ | N/mm |

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility — Why This Classification Matters

In real-world food industry practice, the choice of packaging form and level directly impacts:

- **Shelf life extension** — flexible laminates with Al foil can reduce OTR to $< 1\,\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$.
- **Logistics cost** — tertiary packaging that optimizes pallet cube utilization can reduce freight cost by **15–25%**.
- **Sustainability** — flexible packaging typically uses 70–90% less material by mass than rigid equivalents.
- **Consumer convenience** — resealable flexible pouches (e.g., ZIP-PAK) increase perceived product value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Comparative Derivations

## 3.1 Comparative Selection Matrix — Choosing the Right Form

The selection of a packaging form for a given food product follows a **decision algorithm**. Below is the step-by-step logic that a KTU answer should reproduce.

### Step 1 — Identify Food Sensitivity Profile

| Food Property | Implication |
|---------------|-------------|
| High moisture / aqueous | Needs high WVTR barrier |
| Fat-rich | Needs oxygen + light barrier |
| Acidic (pH < 4.5) | Needs acid-resistant coating |
| Aqueous, hot-fill | Needs heat-resistant rigid container |
| Dry, hygroscopic | Needs moisture barrier |

### Step 2 — Map Sensitivity → Required Barrier Performance

For each barrier property, threshold values guide material selection:

$$\text{WVTR}_{required} = \frac{\Delta m_{max}}{A_{pkg} \cdot t_{shelf}}$$

For example, for a snack food where $50$ mg water ingress is the spoilage limit, $A_{pkg} = 200\,\text{cm}^2$, and shelf life $= 180$ days:

$$\text{WVTR}_{max} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g}}{0.02\,\text{m}^2 \cdot 180\,\text{d}} \approx 0.014\,\text{g}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$$

This is **extremely low**, achievable only by **Al-foil-containing laminates** (flexible) or **rigid metal/glass** with hermetic seals.

### Step 3 — Compare Material Barrier Performance

A typical comparison (single-layer reference, $25\,\text{°C}$):

| Material | OTR | WVTR | Form |
|----------|-----|------|------|
| LDPE | 7500 | 18 | Flexible |
| HDPE | 2900 | 8 | Flexible/Rigid |
| PET | 120 | 22 | Rigid/Semi |
| PP | 2400 | 12 | All |
| PVC | 150 | 30 | Rigid/Semi |
| PVDC | 25 | 4 | Coating on flexible |
| Aluminium foil (25 μm) | < 0.1 | < 0.1 | Flexible laminate component |
| Glass | < 0.01 | < 0.01 | Rigid |
| Tinplate | < 0.01 | < 0.01 | Rigid |

*(All values approximate, normalized at $25\,\text{°C}$/50% RH, units $\text{cm}^3$ or $\text{g}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$.)*

### Step 4 — Form Selection Decision Tree

```
Is the food liquid/pourable?
├── YES → Rigid (bottle, can) or Pouch (flexible with spout)
└── NO
    ├── Is shelf life > 6 months at ambient?
    │   ├── YES → Rigid metal/glass OR Flexible with Al foil
    │   └── NO
    │       ├── Hot-fill required? → Rigid (glass, tinplate, CPET)
    │       └── Ambient fill → Semi-rigid (tray) or Flexible (film)
```

---

## 3.2 Derivations of Key Packaging Mechanics

### 3.2.1 Burst Strength of a Corrugated Box (Derivation)

The empirical burst strength formula is derived from the **Mullen-type test principle**, where internal hydrostatic pressure ruptures the board.

$$\boxed{BS = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i \cdot t_i \cdot n_f}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step (i):** Each ply $i$ of the corrugated board contributes a resistance proportional to its grammage $G_i$ (g/m²) and thickness $t_i$ (mm).

$$R_i = k \cdot G_i \cdot t_i$$

**Step (ii):** The flute geometry enhances bursting resistance via the factor $n_f$. For an **E-flute** (≈ 1.5 mm pitch), $n_f \approx 0.95$; for **B-flute** (≈ 3 mm pitch), $n_f \approx 1.0$; for **C-flute** (≈ 4 mm pitch), $n_f \approx 1.15$.

**Step (iii):** Total resistance is the **additive sum** of all plies:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i t_i n_f$$

**Step (iv):** Setting $BS = \sum R_i$:

$$BS = k \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i t_i n_f$$

Where for SI units $k \approx 0.0001$ to give BS in kPa.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Increasing grammage, ply count, or using a thicker flute (C-flute) increases burst strength **linearly**. However, $n_f$ saturates beyond a certain flute thickness — making **A-flute** (largest, ≈ 5 mm) **less efficient** per unit mass than **C-flute** for burst resistance.

---

### 3.2.2 Stacking Strength of a Carton — McKee Formula

For predicting the **top-load compression strength** of a corrugated box:

$$\boxed{BCS = k \cdot E_c \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot Z^{0.5}}$$

Where:
- $BCS$ = Box Compression Strength (N)
- $E_c$ = Edgewise compression modulus of board (MPa)
- $t$ = Caliper / board thickness (mm)
- $Z$ = Box perimeter (mm)
- $k$ = empirical constant (≈ 0.0035 in SI)

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step (i):** Column-buckling theory gives the Euler critical load for a hollow column:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$$

For a rectangular box, $I \propto t^2 \cdot Z$ approximately, giving $P_{cr} \propto E t^2 Z / L^2$.

**Step (ii):** Empirical corrections due to panel buckling, flute direction, and humidity adjustments introduce the non-integer exponents:

$$BCS \approx \frac{\pi^2 E_c}{L^2} \cdot \frac{Z t^2}{12} \cdot \phi_{humidity}$$

**Step (iii):** Linearizing humidity factor $\phi_{humidity}$ into a constant and combining:

$$BCS = k E_c t^{2.33} Z^{0.5}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The McKee formula explains why **doubling the box perimeter increases compression strength by only $\approx 41\%$** (since $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$), not 100%. This counterintuitive result is often asked as a conceptual question.

---

### 3.2.3 Multi-Layer Laminate Barrier — Additive Resistance Model

For a laminate of $n$ layers, the total barrier transmission rate is given by:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\Phi_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{t_i}{\Phi_i \cdot t_i^{ref}} \cdot \frac{1}{P_{ref}}}$$

A simpler practical approximation (valid for $\Phi_i$ independent):

$$\Phi_{total}^{-1} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\Phi_i}$$

Where $R_i$ is the **resistance** of layer $i$ (reciprocal of permeability).

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step (i):** Fick's first law for steady-state diffusion through layer $i$:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{\Delta c_i}{\Delta x_i}$$

**Step (ii):** At steady state, the flux $J$ is constant across all layers. The driving concentration difference splits across layers:

$$\Delta c_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta c_i = J \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta x_i}{D_i}$$

**Step (iii):** Defining permeability $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ (where $S_i$ = solubility) and resistance $R_i = \Delta x_i / P_i$:

$$\Delta c_{total} = J \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step (iv):** Total flux becomes:

$$J = \frac{\Delta c_{total}}{\sum R_i}$$

Equivalently, in terms of transmission rate $\Phi$:

$$\Phi_{total} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\Phi_i} \right)^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Laminating a high-barrier layer (e.g., 25 μm Al foil with $\Phi \approx 0$) with cheaper structural layers (PET, PE) gives **near-zero total transmission** — this is the foundation of the **tetra pak / retort pouch** barrier architecture.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Form of Packaging vs. Key Attributes

| Attribute | Rigid | Semi-Rigid | Flexible |
|-----------|-------|------------|----------|
| **Material mass per unit product** | High | Medium | Low (typically 1/10 of rigid) |
| **Breakage / failure mode** | Brittle fracture | Cracking | Puncture / seal failure |
| **Stacking efficiency** | Excellent (geometric) | Good | Excellent (conforms) |
| **Transportation cost** | Higher | Medium | Lowest |
| **Recyclability** | High (mono-material) | Moderate | Challenging (multi-layer) |
| **Consumer convenience** | Good (rigid grip) | Good | Excellent (lightweight, resealable) |
| **Barrier potential (with Al)** | Excellent | Limited | Excellent |
| **Capital cost of filling line** | Very High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Suitability for retort** | Excellent | Limited | Good (retort pouches) |
| **Sustainability score** | ★★ (high material use) | ★★★ | ★★★★ (lightweight) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Packaging Classification Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete functional architecture** of food packaging classification as taught in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Food Packaging System"] --> B["Functional Position Axis"]
    A --> C["Physical Form Axis"]

    B --> B1["Primary Package"]
    B --> B2["Secondary Package"]
    B --> B3["Tertiary Package"]

    B1 --> B1A["Direct Food Contact"]
    B1 --> B1B["Dispense and Protect"]
    B1 --> B1C["Label and Communicate"]

    B2 --> B2A["Unitize Primary Packs"]
    B2 --> B2B["Brand Display Surface"]
    B2 --> B2C["Provide Mechanical Cushion"]

    B3 --> B3A["Bulk Warehouse Storage"]
    B3 --> B3B["Transport Stability"]
    B3 --> B3C["Pallet and Container Systems"]

    C --> C1["Rigid Form"]
    C --> C2["Semi Rigid Form"]
    C --> C3["Flexible Form"]

    C1 --> C1A["Glass Bottle"]
    C1 --> C1B["Tinplate Can"]
    C1 --> C1C["PET Bottle"]
    C1 --> C1D["Aluminium Can"]

    C2 --> C2A["PS Yogurt Cup"]
    C2 --> C2B["PP Tub"]
    C2 --> C2C["CPET Tray"]
    C2 --> C2D["Aluminium Tray"]

    C3 --> C3A["Pouches and Sachets"]
    C3 --> C3B["Laminate Films"]
    C3 --> C3C["Flow Wrap"]
    C3 --> C3D["Stand Up Pouch"]

    B1A -.-> C1A
    B1A -.-> C2A
    B1A -.-> C3D

    B2A -.-> C3A
    B2A -.-> C1C

    B3A -.-> C1D
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Selection Algorithm

This second Mermaid block visualizes the **decision flow** an engineer follows when selecting a packaging form for a new food product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: New Food Product"] --> S1["Identify Food Sensitivity Profile"]
    S1 --> S2{"Liquid or Solid"}
    S2 -->|Liquid| S3["Consider Rigid Bottle or Can or Pouch with Spout"]
    S2 -->|Solid| S4{"Shelf Life Required at Ambient"}
    S4 -->|Greater than 6 months| S5["Rigid Metal or Glass or Al Foil Laminate"]
    S4 -->|Less than 6 months| S6{"Hot Fill Required"}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7["Rigid Container with Heat Resistance"]
    S6 -->|No| S8["Semi Rigid Tray or Flexible Film"]

    S3 --> Q1["Check Barrier OTR and WVTR Requirements"]
    S5 --> Q1
    S7 --> Q1
    S8 --> Q1

    Q1 --> Q2{"Light Sensitive Food"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3["Add Opaque or Pigmented or Al Foil Layer"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4["Proceed with Transparent or Translucent Option"]

    Q3 --> END["Final Package Selection"]
    Q4 --> END
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Multi-Layer Flexible Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Outer Layer: PET 12 micrometer"] --> L2["Middle Layer: Aluminium Foil 9 micrometer"]
    L2 --> L3["Adhesive: PU or Acrylic 2 to 3 g per sqm"]
    L3 --> L4["Inner Layer: LDPE or CPP 50 to 80 micrometer"]
    L4 --> L5["Food Contact Surface"]

    L1 -.Barrier Light and Mechanical.-> L2
    L2 -.Barrier OTR and WVTR.-> L3
    L3 -.Bond Strength.-> L4
    L4 -.Heat Seal and Food Safety.-> L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the classic **PET/Al/PE** structure found in nearly every retort pouch, coffee pack, and aseptic carton inner liner. Each layer performs one critical engineering function.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging with one food example each.

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Primary Packaging** is the package that is in **direct contact with the food product**. It is the innermost layer whose primary function is containment, protection, and dispensing of the product.
*Example:* The PET bottle containing a soft drink. **(1 mark)**

**Secondary Packaging** is the package that **groups together one or more primary packages** to form a unit load suitable for retail display and handling.
*Example:* A printed cardboard carton holding 6 PET bottles. **(1 mark)**

**Tertiary Packaging** is the outermost packaging used for **bulk storage, transport, and distribution**. It is rarely seen by the end consumer.
*Example:* A corrugated shipping case on a stretch-wrapped wooden pallet. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between rigid, semi-rigid, and flexible packaging with suitable food examples.

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional I]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Form | Defining Property | Example |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| **Rigid** | Shape does not deform under normal handling; flexural rigidity $D \gg 10^{-3}$ N·m | Glass jar of jam, tinplate can of baked beans. **(1 mark)** |
| **Semi-Rigid** | Holds shape when filled but yields under moderate force; $10^{-5} \leq D \leq 10^{-3}$ N·m | Polystyrene cup of yoghurt, aluminium tray of biryani. **(1 mark)** |
| **Flexible** | Made of pliable films/laminates; easily folded and conformed; $D \ll 10^{-5}$ N·m | Pouches, sachets, flow-wraps, retort pouches. **(1 mark)** |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternative questions (A or B). Each question typically has sub-parts (a) of **7 marks** and (b) of **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[A] (a)** Explain in detail the **different forms of food packaging** — rigid, semi-rigid, and flexible — with respect to their **defining mechanical properties, materials used, and typical food applications**. **(7 marks)**

**[A] (b)** A snack food manufacturer wants to pack a **fat-rich, light-sensitive, hygroscopic** roasted nut product with a **target shelf life of 12 months** at ambient conditions. Recommend the most suitable **form and material of packaging** with justification based on barrier properties. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Forms of Packaging (7 marks)

**(i) Rigid Packaging (2 marks):**
Rigid packaging maintains its shape under self-load and normal handling stress. Flexural rigidity $D \gg 10^{-3}$ N·m. Materials include glass, tinplate steel, aluminium alloys, and rigid plastics (PET, HDPE). Examples: glass milk bottles, tin cans for soups, aluminium beverage cans, PET water bottles. Advantages: excellent barrier (when metal/glass), stackability, premium feel. Disadvantages: high material weight, breakage risk, higher transport cost.

**(ii) Semi-Rigid Packaging (2 marks):**
Semi-rigid packaging resists deformation under its own weight but yields under moderate force. Typical $10^{-5} \leq D \leq 10^{-3}$ N·m. Materials include PS, PP, PVC, thin-gauge PET (CPET), aluminium foil containers. Examples: yoghurt cups, margarine tubs, ready-meal trays, blister packs. Advantages: thermoformable into custom shapes, lower mass than rigid, microwaveable. Disadvantages: lower barrier than metal/glass.

**(iii) Flexible Packaging (2 marks):**
Flexible packaging uses pliable films or laminates, typically 12–250 μm thick. $D \ll 10^{-5}$ N·m. Materials: PE, PP, PET, PA, aluminium foil, paper. Formed into pouches, sachets, bags, flow-wraps. Advantages: lightweight (often 1/10 the mass of rigid equivalents), excellent barrier (with Al foil laminates), resealable options. Disadvantages: difficult to recycle multi-layer laminates, puncture sensitivity.

**(iv) Comparative Summary (1 mark):** A comparative table (refer Section 3.3 of these notes) summarizing mass, recyclability, barrier potential, and cost should be presented.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Packaging Recommendation (7 marks)

**Given:** Fat-rich, light-sensitive, hygroscopic roasted nuts; target shelf life = 12 months at ambient.

**Step (i): Identify required barriers (2 marks):**
- Oxygen barrier (fat oxidation rancidity): $\text{OTR}_{target} < 10\,\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$.
- Moisture barrier (hygroscopic): $\text{WVTR}_{target} < 1\,\text{g}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$.
- Light barrier (light-induced oxidation): opaque or Al foil layer.

**Step (ii): Eliminate unsuitable forms (2 marks):**
- **Transparent rigid/semi-rigid (PET, PS):** fails light barrier → **eliminated**.
- **Mono-material PE films:** insufficient OTR and WVTR → **eliminated**.

**Step (iii): Recommend suitable form and structure (2 marks):**
- **Form:** Flexible — **Stand-up Pouch (SUP)** for shelf appeal and convenience.
- **Laminate structure:** **PET (12 μm) / Aluminium foil (9 μm) / LDPE (70 μm)**.
  - PET → mechanical strength, printability, light barrier reinforcement.
  - Al foil → virtually zero OTR and WVTR, complete light opacity.
  - LDPE → heat sealability and food contact safety.
- Optional features: **reclosable ZIPPER**, **one-way degassing valve** (for freshly roasted nuts releasing CO₂).

**Step (iv): Justification (1 mark):**
The selected PET/Al/PE laminate gives OTR $< 0.1\,\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$ and WVTR $< 0.1\,\text{g}\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}$, easily meeting the 12-month shelf life target. The flexible SUP form reduces material mass by ~85% compared to a rigid glass jar, lowering logistics cost and carbon footprint.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[B] (a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **three levels of food packaging** (primary, secondary, tertiary) and explain how each contributes to the **overall protection and marketing of a packaged food product**. **(7 marks)**

**[B] (b)** Using the **McKee formula**, calculate the **Box Compression Strength (BCS)** of a corrugated box with edgewise modulus $E_c = 7$ MPa, caliper $t = 5$ mm, and perimeter $Z = 1800$ mm. Comment on how doubling the perimeter would affect the BCS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Three Levels of Packaging (7 marks)

**(i) Primary Packaging (2.5 marks):**
The package in direct contact with food — bottle, can, pouch, sachet, jar. Functions: containment, preservation, dispensing, labelling. *Example:* A foil-laminate sachet of instant coffee.

**(ii) Secondary Packaging (2.5 marks):**
Groups several primary packs into a unit for retail. Provides brand surface (printed carton), additional mechanical protection, and unitization for shelf stocking. *Example:* A printed cardboard box containing 20 instant coffee sachets.

**(iii) Tertiary Packaging (2 marks):**
Used for bulk transport and warehousing — corrugated shipping cases, shrink-wrapped pallets, stretch films, strapping. Optimizes logistics, cube utilization, and protects against vibration/compression during transit. *Example:* 24 secondary cartons shrink-wrapped on a $1200 \times 1000$ mm wooden pallet.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — McKee Formula Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $E_c = 7$ MPa, $t = 5$ mm, $Z = 1800$ mm, $k = 0.0035$ (SI).

**Step (i): Substitute into McKee formula (3 marks):**

$$BCS = k \cdot E_c \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot Z^{0.5}$$

$$BCS = 0.0035 \times 7 \times 5^{2.33} \times 1800^{0.5}$$

**Step (ii): Evaluate term by term (2 marks):**

$$5^{2.33} = e^{2.33 \ln 5} = e^{2.33 \times 1.6094} = e^{3.750} \approx 42.52$$

$$1800^{0.5} = \sqrt{1800} \approx 42.43$$

**Step (iii): Compute final value (2 marks):**

$$BCS = 0.0035 \times 7 \times 42.52 \times 42.43$$

$$BCS \approx 0.0035 \times 7 \times 1804.1$$

$$BCS \approx 44.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step (iv): Effect of doubling perimeter (Bonus — included for full marks):**

If $Z' = 2Z = 3600$ mm:

$$Z'^{0.5} = \sqrt{2} \cdot Z^{0.5} \approx 1.414 \times 42.43 \approx 60.0$$

$$BCS' \approx 0.0035 \times 7 \times 42.52 \times 60.0 \approx 62.5 \text{ N}$$

**Comment:** Doubling the perimeter increases BCS by only **41.4%** (i.e., by factor $\sqrt{2}$), not 100%, because BCS scales with $Z^{0.5}$. This confirms the **square-root dependence** on perimeter inherent in the McKee formula.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing "level of packaging" (primary/secondary/tertiary) with "form of packaging" (rigid/semi-rigid/flexible). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the student mixes up these two axes.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Stating that "doubling the perimeter doubles the BCS." The correct relationship is **$BCS \propto Z^{0.5}$**, i.e., $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times increase.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to cite barrier property **units** (e.g., OTR in $\text{cm}^3\,\text{m}^{-2}\,\text{d}^{-1}\,\text{atm}^{-1}$) when justifying material selection. Always specify units — this is a **1-mark deduction** in valuation.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not mentioning **FSSAI / FDA compliance** for primary packaging materials in direct food contact. Examiners expect this regulatory reference for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types & Forms of Packaging**

- ✅ Two orthogonal axes of classification: **Functional Position** (Primary / Secondary / Tertiary) and **Physical Form** (Rigid / Semi-Rigid / Flexible).
- ✅ **Primary package** is the only one in **direct food contact** — must satisfy FSSAI / FDA migration limits.
- ✅ **Secondary package** provides **unitization and branding**.
- ✅ **Tertiary package** handles **logistics, storage, and transport** (pallet, IBC, corrugated case).
- ✅ **Flexural rigidity $D$** is the quantitative basis for classifying forms:
  - Rigid: $D \gg 10^{-3}$ N·m
  - Semi-rigid: $10^{-5} \leq D \leq 10^{-3}$ N·m
  - Flexible: $D \ll 10^{-5}$ N·m
- ✅ **Barrier metrics:** OTR, WVTR, puncture resistance, tensile strength, elongation at break.
- ✅ **McKee formula:** $BCS \propto E_c \cdot t^{2.33} \cdot Z^{0.5}$ — perimeter effect is **square-root**, not linear.
- ✅ **Burst strength** of corrugated board: $BS = k \sum G_i t_i n_f$ — depends on grammage, thickness, and flute type.
- ✅ **Multi-layer laminate barrier:** barrier resistances **add in series** ($\Phi_{total}^{-1} = \sum R_i$).
- ✅ **Aluminium foil laminate** (e.g., PET/Al/PE) gives near-zero OTR and WVTR — the workhorse of retort pouches and aseptic cartons.
- ✅ **Sustainability trend:** flexible packaging uses up to **90% less material mass** than rigid equivalents.
- ✅ **Selection rule of thumb:** liquid + long shelf life → **rigid (can/bottle)** or **pouch with spout**; solid + ambient + long shelf life → **Al-foil laminate flexible pouch**.
- ✅ Always cite **units** for barrier properties in exam answers — missing units cost 1 mark.
- ✅ Regulatory standards to remember: **FSSAI (India), FDA 21 CFR (USA), EU 1935/2004, IS 6623 (Indian pallets), TAPPI T810 (Burst Strength), ASTM D882 (Tensile)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Container
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metal Container

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Metal Container?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **metal container** is a rigid, hermetically sealed packaging unit fabricated primarily from **tinplate**, **tin-free steel (TFS)**, or **aluminum**, used to preserve, protect, and extend the shelf-life of processed foods by providing a complete barrier against light, gases, moisture, and microbial ingress.

In the context of KTU's **PEAGT863 – Food Packaging Technology**, a metal container is not just a "tin can." It is a precisely engineered multilayer system comprising:
- A **base steel/aluminum substrate**
- A protective **tin or chromium/oxide coating**
- An internal **organic lacquer/liner**
- An external **printing and varnishing layer**
- Mechanical **seaming and closure components**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suit of Armor" for Food

Imagine a knight heading into battle. The suit of armor is made of several carefully chosen layers:
- A **strong inner steel frame** (the base metal — provides mechanical strength)
- A **polished outer mirror** (the tin or lacquer coating — protects against rust and corrosion)
- A **padded inner lining** (the lacquer — prevents food from reacting with metal)
- A **decorative outer cloth** (the printed label — marketing and identification)

Similarly, a metal food can is a **layered defense system**. Each layer has a specific job — mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, chemical inertness, and consumer appeal — and the failure of any one layer compromises the entire package.

### 1.3 Historical and Industrial Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Milestones in Metal Packaging:**
> - **1810**: Nicholas Appert develops heat sterilization for food preservation in glass — leading to the search for a more durable container.
> - **1813**: Peter Durand patents the tin-plated iron canister in **England** — the birth of the modern tin can.
> - **1904:** Rubber gaskets introduced for can lids (key for vacuum sealing).
> - **1957**: Aluminum easy-open ends introduced by Alcoa.
> - **1965**: Two-piece drawn-and-ironed (D&I) cans developed by Reynolds Metals — revolutionizing beverage packaging.
> - **Modern era**: Lightweighting reduces tinplate gauge from **0.30 mm** to **0.16 mm** while maintaining integrity.

### 1.4 Why Metal Containers Dominate Specific Food Categories

| Property | Food Industry Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Total opacity** (100 % light barrier) | Prevents photo-oxidation of vitamins, lipids, and color pigments |
| **Hermetic seal** | Enables commercial **thermal sterilization** (retort at **121 °C**) |
| **Mechanical strength** | Survives stacking, transport, and 2-year+ ambient shelf-life |
| **Formability** | Can be drawn, ironed, stamped into diverse shapes |
| **Recyclability** | Steel and aluminum are **infinitely recyclable** without quality loss |
| **Temperature tolerance** | Withstands **−40 °C to +250 °C** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Insight:**
> Examiners frequently test the **"why metal for thermally processed foods?"** question. The decisive answer is the **combination of hermetic seal + heat resistance + total light barrier**, which no single polymer or paper-based package can simultaneously provide.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Metals Used in Food Packaging

The three principal metal systems studied under KTU Module 2 are:

### A. Tinplate (Electrolytic Tinplate — ETP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Composition of Tinplate:**
> - **Base steel**: Low-carbon cold-rolled steel (C ≤ **0.12 %**, Mn ≤ **0.60 %**)
> - **Tin coating**: Pure tin (Sn ≥ **99.85 %**), applied by electrolytic deposition
> - **Passivation layer**: Chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) film, mass ~ **5 mg/m² per side**
> - **Oil film**: DOS (dioctyl sebacate) or cottonseed oil, mass **2–5 mg/m²**

The **tin coating weight** is the most critical specification. It is designated in two systems:

**System 1 — Old British Standard (g/m² per side):**
- Plain label: #25 = **2.8 g/m² per side**
- #50 = **5.6 g/m² per side**, #75 = **8.4 g/m² per side**, #100 = **11.2 g/m² per side**

**System 2 — American (lb per base box):**
- One base box = **31,360 in² = 20.232 m²** (total area, both sides)

$$m_{\text{Sn,total}} = n \times \frac{11.2}{1000} \times A$$

Where:
- $m_{\text{Sn,total}}$ = total tin mass in grams
- $n$ = coating designation (e.g., 25, 50, 100)
- $A$ = sheet area in m² (single side)

### B. Tin-Free Steel (TFS) / Electrolytic Chromium-Coated Steel (ECCS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TFS Composition:**
> - **Chromium metal layer**: **50–140 mg/m²** per side
> - **Chromium oxide layer**: **7–25 mg/m²** per side (as Cr₂O₃)
> - **Total Cr coating**: **55–165 mg/m²** per side
> - **Base**: Same low-carbon steel as tinplate

TFS is **cheaper than tinplate** but **cannot be soldered** (must use welding or adhesive bonding for side-seams) and **must be lacquered** because the chromium oxide film is porous.

### C. Aluminum and Aluminum Alloys

Pure aluminum (1xxx series) is too soft for structural cans. Common alloys used:

| Alloy | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **AA 3003** | Al + 1.2 % Mn | Drawn-and-ironed (D&I) beverage cans — body |
| **AA 3004** | Al + 1.2 % Mn + 1.0 % Mg | D&I cans — improved strength |
| **AA 5182** | Al + 4.5 % Mg | Easy-open can ends — high strength |
| **AA 5052** | Al + 2.5 % Mg + 0.25 % Cr | Fish/seafood cans |

Aluminum forms a self-healing **aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃)** layer of thickness ~ **3–5 nm** spontaneously upon air exposure, providing natural corrosion resistance.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties and Governing Equations

The **critical mechanical performance parameters** for metal container design are:

### 2.2.1 Yield Strength and Tensile Strength

For thin sheet metal used in cans, the **yield strength** $S_y$ and **tensile strength** $S_u$ must be sufficient to withstand:
- Internal pressure during **retort sterilization** (typically **+90 to +180 kPa** gauge)
- External vacuum during **cooling** (down to **−60 kPa** gauge)
- Stacking loads during **warehousing** (often **+200 to +400 kPa** axial)

The **hoop stress** in a cylindrical can body under internal pressure $p$ is given by the thin-walled pressure vessel equation:

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{\text{hoop}}$ = circumferential (hoop) stress, in **MPa**
- $p$ = internal pressure, in **MPa**
- $r$ = can radius, in **mm**
- $t$ = wall thickness, in **mm**

The **axial (longitudinal) stress** for a closed-end cylinder is:

$$\sigma_{\text{axial}} = \frac{p \cdot r}{2t}$$

Notice that the **hoop stress is twice the axial stress**. This is why can bodies always fail longitudinally (along the axis) under internal pressure — a famous KTU conceptual question.

### 2.2.2 Buckling Under External Pressure (Vacuum Collapse)

After retort cooling, the can experiences **external pressure greater than internal** (vacuum condition). The critical buckling pressure $p_{\text{cr}}$ for a thin cylindrical shell is given by the **Bresse-Bryan formula**:

$$p_{\text{cr}} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$$

Where:
- $p_{\text{cr}}$ = critical external (collapse) pressure, in **MPa**
- $E$ = Young's modulus (~ **200 GPa** for steel, **70 GPa** for aluminum)
- $t$ = wall thickness, in **mm**
- $D$ = can diameter, in **mm**
- $L$ = can height (length between stiffening rings), in **mm**

### 2.2.3 Lacquer Film Weight and Coverage

The internal lacquer is critical to prevent metal-food interaction. Lacquer coverage is measured in **mg/m²** or **g/m²**:

$$C_{\text{lacquer}} = \frac{m_{\text{dry film}}}{A_{\text{can interior}}}$$

Typical values:
- **Epoxy-phenolic lacquer**: **40–80 mg/can** for a 400 × 307 can
- **Vinyl lacquer** (for highly corrosive foods): **80–150 mg/can**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> This table consolidates **every equation** you need for KTU 2024 Module 2 numerical/analytical questions on Metal Containers.

| Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Tin coating mass | $m_{\text{Sn}} = n \times \frac{11.2}{1000} \times A$ | $n$ = grade number, $A$ = area in m² |
| Hoop stress in can | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}$ | $p$ in MPa, $r,t$ in mm |
| Axial stress in can | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{2t}$ | $p$ in MPa, $r,t$ in mm |
| Critical buckling pressure | $p_{\text{cr}} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$ | $E$ in MPa, all lengths in mm |
| Lacquer coverage | $C = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry}}}{A_{\text{interior}}}$ | mg/m² |
| Can end deflection (vacuum) | $\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{p \cdot a^4}{E \cdot t^2 \cdot k}$ | $a$ = panel radius, $k$ = end profile factor |
| Tin coating thickness (approx.) | $t_{\text{Sn}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{Sn}}}{\rho_{\text{Sn}}}$ | $\rho_{\text{Sn}} = 7.3$ g/cm³ |
| Aluminum oxide thickness | $t_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} \approx 3 \text{ to } 5$ nm | Natural passive layer |

## 2.4 Can Construction — The Two Principal Architectures

### 2.4.1 Three-Piece Cans (3-P)

A 3-piece can consists of:
1. **Body** (cylindrical wall, formed from a flat rectangle with a longitudinal **side-seam**)
2. **Bottom end** (already attached via **double-seam** before filling)
3. **Top end** (attached after filling via double-seam)

Side-seam joining methods:
- **Soldered** (traditional, ~70 % Pb-Sn solder, now restricted in food contact)
- **Welded** (modern, resistance welding, no solder)
- **Cemented** (adhesive-bonded, for retort applications)

### 2.4.2 Two-Piece Cans (2-P)

Two-piece cans have **no side-seam**. They are made by:
1. **Drawn-and-Ironed (D&I)**: A cup is drawn and then **ironed** through successive rings to thin the walls. Used for beverage cans.
2. **Drawn-and-Redrawn (DRD)**: Multiple drawing operations without wall-ironing. Used for food cans (tuna, pet food).

| Feature | 3-Piece | 2-Piece D&I |
|---|---|---|
| Side-seam | Yes | None |
| Bottom-seam | Yes (double-seamed) | Integral (no seam) |
| Top-seam | Yes (after fill) | Double-seamed after fill |
| Material | Tinplate / TFS | Aluminum (AA 3004) |
| Wall thickness | Uniform | **Non-uniform** (thinner in mid-body) |
| Speed | Slower | Very fast (>2000 cpm) |
| Applications | Vegetables, soups, fish | Beverages, aerosols |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop and Axial Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Can Body

We consider a **thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel** of radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$ subjected to internal gauge pressure $p$.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a half-cylinder element (for hoop stress).**

Imagine cutting the cylinder longitudinally into two halves. The internal pressure $p$ acts over the projected area of the diameter. For a unit length of the cylinder:

$$F_{\text{internal}} = p \cdot (2r) \cdot 1 = 2pr$$

This force is resisted by the two wall sections of total thickness $2t$:

$$F_{\text{wall}} = \sigma_h \cdot (2t) \cdot 1 = 2 \sigma_h t$$

**Step 2 — Equate the forces to obtain hoop stress.**

$$2pr = 2 \sigma_h t$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t}}$$

**Step 3 — Force balance on a transverse cross-section (for axial stress).**

Now cut the cylinder transversely. The internal pressure acts over the circular cross-sectional area $\pi r^2$. The resisting force acts on the annular wall area $2 \pi r t$:

$$F_{\text{internal}} = p \cdot \pi r^2$$

$$F_{\text{wall}} = \sigma_a \cdot (2 \pi r t)$$

**Step 4 — Equate the forces.**

$$p \cdot \pi r^2 = \sigma_a \cdot 2 \pi r t$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \frac{p \cdot r}{2t}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (the KTU-marked insight).**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_a} = \frac{p r / t}{p r / (2t)} = \frac{2}{1}$$

Therefore the hoop stress is **twice** the axial stress. The can will **always split longitudinally** (parallel to its axis) under internal overpressure, never transversely. This is why **biscuit, soup, and beverage cans** all show characteristic vertical cracks when they burst.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tin Coating Thickness from Mass and Density

A tinplate sheet of area **0.5 m² (single side)** carries a **#50 tin coating** (5.6 g/m² per side). Find the average tin layer thickness on one face.

**Step 1 — Identify the input parameters.**

$$m_{\text{per side}} = 5.6 \text{ g/m}^2$$

$$A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total tin mass on one side.**

$$m_{\text{Sn}} = 5.6 \text{ g/m}^2 \times 0.5 \text{ m}^2$$

$$m_{\text{Sn}} = 2.8 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the mass–volume–density relationship.**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

With $\rho_{\text{Sn}} = 7.3$ g/cm³:

$$V_{\text{Sn}} = \frac{2.8 \text{ g}}{7.3 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

$$V_{\text{Sn}} = 0.3836 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Convert volume to thickness by dividing by area.**

Convert area to cm²: $A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 \times 10{,}000 = 5{,}000 \text{ cm}^2$

$$t_{\text{Sn}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{0.3836 \text{ cm}^3}{5{,}000 \text{ cm}^2}$$

$$t_{\text{Sn}} = 7.672 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5 — Express in micrometres.**

$$\boxed{t_{\text{Sn}} \approx 0.77 \text{ \mu m}}$$

This **sub-micron** thickness explains why the tin coating, despite being extremely thin, still provides effective cathodic protection to the underlying steel.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Buckling Pressure for a Vacuum-Sealed Can

A **3-piece can** of diameter **D = 75 mm**, height **L = 110 mm**, and wall thickness **t = 0.20 mm** is cooled after retort. The vacuum inside produces an external pressure differential. Determine if the can body will buckle. Use E = **200 GPa** for steel.

**Step 1 — Recall the Bresse-Bryan formula for cylindrical shell buckling.**

$$p_{\text{cr}} = \frac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{L/D}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric ratios.**

$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{0.20}{75} = 2.667 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{110}{75} = 1.467$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(t/D)^{2.5}$.**

$$(2.667 \times 10^{-3})^{2.5}$$

Take natural log: $2.5 \times \ln(2.667 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.5 \times (-5.927) = -14.818$

$$(t/D)^{2.5} = e^{-14.818} = 3.601 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4 — Plug all values into the formula.**

$$p_{\text{cr}} = \frac{2.6 \times 200{,}000 \text{ MPa} \times 3.601 \times 10^{-7}}{1.467}$$

Numerator: $2.6 \times 200{,}000 \times 3.601 \times 10^{-7} = 0.1873$ MPa

$$p_{\text{cr}} = \frac{0.1873}{1.467}$$

$$\boxed{p_{\text{cr}} \approx 0.128 \text{ MPa} = 128 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with actual vacuum pressure.**

Typical post-retort vacuum in a can is **−30 to −50 kPa** (i.e., external gauge pressure of **30–50 kPa**).

Since $p_{\text{cr}} = 128$ kPa $\gg$ 50 kPa, **the can will NOT buckle** under normal retort cooling — confirming the design is safe. Margin of safety = $(128-50)/128 =$ **61 %**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Corrosion Behavior in Food Cans

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Galvanic Series in Food Can Context:**
> The tin coating is **cathodic** with respect to the steel substrate in **most food media**. This means if a pinhole exposes the steel, the steel corrodes **preferentially** (acts as the sacrificial anode). This is the **opposite** of the sacrificial protection seen in galvanized steel (where Zn is the anode).

| Food Type | pH Range | Corrosion Mechanism | Recommended Lacquer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acidic (tomato, citrus) | pH 3.5–4.5 | Detinning + hydrogen swelling | Vinyl or vinyl-phenolic |
| Sulfited (wine, beer) | pH 3.0–4.0 | Sulfide staining (black FeS) | C-enamel or pigmented epoxy |
| Protein-rich (meat, fish) | pH 5.5–6.5 | Sulfide staining + tin dissolution | C-enamel (sulfur-resistant) |
| Alkaline (corn, peas) | pH 6.5–7.5 | Tin dissolution + Fe exposure | Epoxy-phenolic |
| Highly acidic (pickles) | pH 2.5–3.5 | Severe detinning | Multi-coat vinyl + tin ≥ #100 |

> [!TIP]
> **"C-Enamel"** is a critical KTU term. It is an **epoxy-phenolic lacquer pigmented with zinc oxide or aluminum powder** to absorb sulfur compounds that cause black staining of fish and meat products.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layer Architecture of a Tinplate Can (Cross-Sectional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OUTER VARNISH LAYER 5 TO 10 microns PRINTING DECORATION SCRATCH RESISTANCE"] --> B["TINPLATE BASE STEEL SUBSTRATE 0.16 TO 0.30 MM MECHANICAL STRENGTH"]
    B --> C["TIN COATING 0.4 TO 1.6 MICRONS CATHODIC BARRIER AGAINST CORROSION"]
    C --> D["PASSIVATION FILM CHROMIUM OXIDE 5 MG PER M2 IMPROVES PAINT ADHESION"]
    D --> E["ORGANIC LACQUER 5 TO 15 MICRONS FOOD CONTACT BARRIER"]
    E --> F["FOOD PRODUCT ACIDIC OR NEUTRAL OR SULFITED"]
```

```mermaid
    S1 --> S2["BODY FORMING RECTANGLE ROLLED INTO CYLINDER"]
    S2 --> S3["SIDE SEAM OPERATION SOLDERED OR WELDED OR CEMENTED"]
    S3 --> S4["FLANGING TOP EDGE FLARED OUTWARD"]
    S4 --> S5["DOUBLE SEAMING BOTTOM END ATTACHED"]
    S5 --> P1["EMPTY 3 PIECE CAN READY FOR FILLING"]
    P1 --> F1["FILLING FOOD PRODUCT DISPENSED"]
    F1 --> S6["DOUBLE SEAMING TOP END CLOSED"]
    S6 --> P2["SEALED CAN READY FOR RETORT"]
```

## 4.3 Two-Piece D&I Can — Block Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ALUMINUM SLUG AA3004 DIAMETER 75 MM THICKNESS 35 MM"] --> B1["COLD CUPPING PRESS SLUG DRAWN INTO CUP 200 DEEP"]
    B1 --> C1["REDRAWING DIE WALLS THINNED TO 0.10 MM"]
    C1 --> D1["IRONING THROUGH 3 RINGS WALL THINNED TO 0.06 MM"]
    D1 --> E1["TRIMMING AND WASHING EDGE BURR REMOVED"]
    E1 --> F1["DECORATING AND LACQUERING INTERIOR AND EXTERIOR COAT"]
    F1 --> G1["FINISHED 2 PIECE CAN BODY READY FOR NECKING AND END SEAMING"]
```

## 4.4 Double-Seam Cross-Section — Schematic Block Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Double Seam** is the most critical mechanical joint in any metal can. It is the folded, interlocked mechanical junction between the can body flange and the can end curl. Failure here is the leading cause of **post-process leak and microbial spoilage**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["LAP 1.2 TO 1.5 MM LENGTH OF INTERLOCK"] --> T["TIGHTNESS 75 TO 85 PERCENT HOW DEEPLY PRESSED"] --> W["WRINKLE FREQUENCY MUST BE ZERO RADIOGRAPHIC CHECK"]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Decision Tree — When to Use Tinplate vs TFS vs Aluminum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{"WHAT IS THE FOOD PRODUCT"} -->|"ACIDIC RETORT FOODS"| TP1["USE TINPLATE WITH VINYL LACQUER GRADE #50 MIN"]
    START -->|"SULFITED OR FISH PRODUCTS"| TP2["USE TINPLATE WITH C ENAMEL SULFUR RESISTANT"]
    START -->|"DRY FOODS LIKE POWDERS"| TFS["USE TIN FREE STEEL WITH EPOXY PHENOLIC LACQUER"]
    START -->|"CARBONATED BEVERAGES"| AL["USE ALUMINUM AA3004 D AND I PROCESS"]
    START -->|"HIGH STRENGTH ENDS NEEDED"| AL2["USE ALUMINUM AA5182 FOR EASY OPEN ENDS"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["STEEL MILL HOT ROLLING COLD ROLLING ANNEALING TEMPER MILLING"] --> P1["TINPLATE LINE ELECTROLYTIC TINNING REFLOWING PASSIVATION OILING"]
    P1 --> P2["SHEET CUTTING TO CAN SIZE BLANKS"]
    P2 --> P3["CAN PLANT SLITTER BODY MAKER END LINER FLANGER TESTER"]
    P3 --> P4["QUALITY CONTROL AIR PRESSURE TEST BACTERIOLOGICAL TEST DIMENSIONAL CHECK"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between tinplate and tin-free steel (TFS) with respect to composition, cost, weldability, and lacquer requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Tinplate (ETP) | Tin-Free Steel (ECCS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Coating metal** | Tin (Sn), 0.4–1.6 µm | Chromium + Cr₂O₃, ~55–165 mg/m² |
| **Cost** | Higher (tin is expensive) | Lower (~30 % cheaper) |
| **Solderability** | Excellent | **Cannot be soldered** — porous oxide layer |
| **Weldability** | Excellent | Good (resistance welding used) |
| **Lacquer requirement** | Optional for non-corrosive foods | **Mandatory** — must always be lacquered |
| **Appearance** | Bright silvery | Dull grey |
| **Typical food use** | Acidic, seafood | Carbonated beverages, dry foods, crown caps |

(3 marks: 1 for composition, 1 for cost/weldability, 1 for lacquer requirement)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define "double seam" in a 3-piece can. List the four critical measurements used to evaluate its quality.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *double seam* is the mechanical interlock formed by folding the can body flange and the can end curl together through a seaming chuck and seaming rolls, producing a hermetic, mechanically strong, leak-proof joint.

**Four Critical Quality Measurements:**

1. **Seam thickness (T)** — overall height of the seamed joint, typically **1.20–1.40 mm** for a 73 mm dia can.
2. **Seam length / depth (L)** — measured from the bottom of the countersink to the top of the seam, typically **2.80–3.20 mm**.
3. **Tightness (Tight %)** — degree of compaction, must be **75–85 %**.
4. **Wrinkle / overlap rating** — number and uniformity of wrinkles in the body hook and cover hook; must be **uniform and wrinkle-free**.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **manufacturing process of electrolytic tinplate (ETP)**, highlighting the role of the **flow-melting (reflow) operation** and the **chromium-oxide passivation film**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3-piece tinplate can** has an internal diameter of **75 mm**, wall thickness **0.20 mm**, and is filled with food, sealed, and retort-sterilized at **121 °C**, during which the internal pressure rises to **+180 kPa** gauge. Calculate:
- (i) The hoop stress in the can body wall.
- (ii) The axial (longitudinal) stress.
- (iii) The factor of safety, given the yield strength of tinplate = **230 MPa**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **(a) Manufacturing of ETP — 7 marks**

The manufacture of electrolytic tinplate is a continuous strip process:

1. **Cold-rolled low-carbon steel strip** (thickness **0.15–0.50 mm**) is the starting substrate. It must have tightly controlled **surface roughness (Ra ~ 0.5–1.5 µm)** for proper tin adhesion.

2. **Cleaning and pickling**: Alkaline electrolytic cleaning removes rolling oils; acid pickling removes mill scale.

3. **Electrolytic tinning**: The strip passes through tin-plating cells containing **stannous sulfate (SnSO₄)** and **phenolsulfonic acid (PSA)** electrolyte. Tin is deposited on both sides at current density **20–40 A/dm²**. Cathode efficiency approaches **95–99 %**.

4. **Flow-melting (reflow)** — *the most critical step*: The strip is heated to **above the tin melting point (232 °C)**, typically by **resistance heating or induction** to **~280–300 °C** for **0.5–1.5 seconds**, then **quenched in water**. This:
   - Forms a **FeSn₂ intermetallic alloy layer** at the tin–steel interface (improves adhesion).
   - Gives the **bright, mirror-like reflective finish** characteristic of tinplate.
   - Eliminates porosity in the as-deposited tin layer.

5. **Passivation**: Electrolytic treatment in a **sodium dichromate (Na₂Cr₂O₇)** solution deposits a thin **chromium-oxide layer (5–15 mg/m² as Cr)** which prevents **tin oxide growth during storage** and improves **paint/lacquer adhesion**.

6. **Oiling**: A thin film of **DOS (dioctyl sebacate)** or **cottonseed oil** (2–5 mg/m²) is applied to prevent scratching during coiling and handling.

7. **Inspection and recoiling**: The strip is checked for **tin coating weight, alloy formation, surface defects**, then coiled for shipment.

> [!TIP]
> **Why reflow matters:** Without flow-melting, the tin layer is **porous and dull**, and the can would corrode rapidly. Reflow converts the deposit from a **discontinuous electroplated layer** into a **continuous, alloy-bonded, bright metallic film**. This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $D = 75$ mm → $r = 37.5$ mm
- $t = 0.20$ mm
- $p = 180$ kPa $= 0.180$ MPa
- $S_y = 230$ MPa

**Step (i): Hoop stress**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{0.180 \times 37.5}{0.20}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{6.75}{0.20} = 33.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (ii): Axial stress**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{p \cdot r}{2t} = \frac{33.75}{2} = 16.875 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step (iii): Factor of Safety**

The governing (maximum) stress is the hoop stress. FoS based on yield:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_h} = \frac{230}{33.75}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoS} = 6.81}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose 2 marks by:
> 1. **Mixing units** — using $p$ in kPa while $r, t$ in mm without converting $p$ to MPa. **Always convert p to MPa** (1 MPa = 1000 kPa).
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the diameter** when substituting $r$. Many students plug $D = 75$ mm directly, doubling the answer.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong stress for FoS** — must use the **larger** of hoop and axial stress (here, hoop).
> 4. **Not stating which stress is governing** — the hoop stress governs because $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_a$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Compare **3-piece and 2-piece metal cans** with respect to construction, side-seam, materials, applications, and at least **four advantages/disadvantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **mechanisms of internal corrosion** in food cans. Explain with diagrams the **galvanic couple** between tin and steel, and recommend **lacquer systems** for the following foods: (i) **tomato ketchup**, (ii) **tuna in brine**, (iii) **carbonated soft drink** in an aluminum can. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **(a) Comparison of 3-Piece and 2-Piece Cans — 7 marks**

| Feature | 3-Piece Can | 2-Piece Can |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Body + bottom + top = 3 separate pieces, joined mechanically | Body + bottom are one piece (drawn), top is attached later = 2 pieces |
| **Side-seam** | **Present** (soldered, welded, or cemented) | **None** — seamless body |
| **Bottom seam** | Yes (double-seamed) | None (integrally drawn) |
| **Top seam** | Yes (after filling) | Yes (after filling) |
| **Materials** | Tinplate, TFS | Predominantly **aluminum (AA 3004)**; some tinplate DRD |
| **Wall thickness** | Uniform | Non-uniform (thinner mid-body after ironing) |
| **Manufacturing speed** | 200–400 cans/min | **>2000 cans/min** (D&I line) |
| **Applications** | Vegetables, soups, fish, meat, fruit | Beverages, pet food, fish |
| **Recyclability** | Easy (steel separates magnetically) | Easy but requires eddy-current separation for Al |

**Advantages of 3-piece cans:**
1. Body height can be varied easily → flexible sizes.
2. Use cheaper tinplate → lower cost.
3. Side-seam allows decorative printing across the seam area.
4. Compatible with traditional retort lines.

**Disadvantages of 3-piece cans:**
1. Side-seam is a **potential leak path** — must be carefully seamed and tested.
2. Soldered seams (older types) had **lead contamination risk** — now banned for most food contact.
3. Slower production than 2-piece.
4. Wasted material from body blank corners (~10 % scrap).

**Advantages of 2-piece cans:**
1. **No side-seam** → superior leak-proof integrity.
2. Higher aesthetic value — print can run 360° uninterrupted.
3. Lighter due to thinner walls in mid-body.
4. Much faster production → lower per-unit cost.

**Disadvantages of 2-piece cans:**
1. Body height is **fixed by the die** — limited size flexibility.
2. Higher capital cost of D&I machinery.
3. Aluminum alloy selection limited by formability.

---

#### **(b) Mechanisms of Internal Corrosion in Food Cans — 7 marks**

> [!NOTE]
> **Three principal mechanisms of metal-container internal corrosion:**

**1. Detinning (acidic foods):**
At low pH (< 4.5), the tin layer dissolves:

$$\text{Sn} \rightarrow \text{Sn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$

This is **uniform dissolution** in the absence of oxygen. The dissolved Sn²⁺ is generally **non-toxic** and is considered nutritionally neutral. Most fruits and tomato-based products cause controlled detinning, which is acceptable provided the steel substrate is not exposed.

**2. Pitting corrosion (with oxygen or nitrite present):**
Dissolved O₂ or nitrate ions cause **localized pitting** of the tin layer:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$

Pitting creates **pinholes** that expose the steel, leading to localized attack and eventual perforation.

**3. Sulfide staining (protein-rich foods):**
Sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine, methionine) decompose during retort to release **H₂S**:

$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{Sn} \rightarrow \text{SnS} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(brownish discoloration)}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{FeS} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(black staining — undesirable)}$$

**Galvanic couple (Tin–Steel):**

When a pinhole exposes the steel beneath the tin, an electrochemical cell forms:

- **Anode**: Fe (steel) → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻  (corrodes)
- **Cathode**: Sn (tin) → reduction of H⁺ or O₂

$$\boxed{\text{Steel corrodes preferentially (tin is cathodic in most food media)}}$$

This is the **opposite** of galvanized steel, where zinc (the coating) is the anode and corrodes sacrificially. Hence, any pinhole in the tin coating is dangerous for the underlying steel.

**Recommended lacquer systems:**

| Food | Recommended Lacquer |
|---|---|
| **(i) Tomato ketchup (pH ~3.8)** | **Vinyl lacquer** or **vinyl-phenolic** — resistant to acid detinning; tin grade ≥ #50 |
| **(ii) Tuna in brine (protein, sulfur-rich)** | **C-enamel** — zinc-oxide-pigmented epoxy-phenolic that absorbs H₂S and prevents black FeS staining |
| **(iii) Carbonated soft drink in Al can** | **Epoxy-phenolic or vinyl organosol** lining for acid + CO₂ environment; aluminum needs internal coating because Al₂O₃ dissolves below pH 4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid (loses 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. Stating that **tin protects steel sacrificially** like zinc does — WRONG in food cans. Tin is **cathodic** to steel in most food media.
> 2. Recommending plain epoxy-phenolic for tomato ketchup — wrong; needs acid-resistant **vinyl** coating.
> 3. Confusing C-enamel (sulfur-resistant) with clear epoxy — C-enamel is **pigmented** (zinc oxide or aluminum flakes).
> 4. Saying aluminum cans are "rust-proof" — they don't rust, but they still corrode in acidic media via **dissolution of Al₂O₃ passive layer**, hence internal lacquer is mandatory.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize Before Exam**

**✅ Core Definitions**
- [ ] Tinplate = low-carbon steel + electrolytic tin coating (0.4–1.6 µm).
- [ ] TFS = low-carbon steel + Cr/Cr₂O₃ layer (must be lacquered always).
- [ ] Double seam = mechanical interlock between body flange and end curl.
- [ ] C-enamel = sulfur-resistant pigmented lacquer for fish/meat.

**✅ Critical Equations**
- [ ] $\sigma_h = pr/t$ and $\sigma_a = pr/(2t)$ — hoop stress is **2× axial**.
- [ ] $p_{\text{cr}} = 2.6 E (t/D)^{2.5} / (L/D)$ — buckling under vacuum.
- [ ] FoS always based on the **larger** of the two stresses.

**✅ Material Selection Rules**
- [ ] Acidic foods → tinplate + vinyl lacquer.
- [ ] Sulfur-rich foods → tinplate + C-enamel.
- [ ] Dry foods → TFS + epoxy-phenolic.
- [ ] Carbonated drinks → aluminum D&I can + epoxy or vinyl organosol lining.

**✅ Process Knowledge**
- [ ] Flow-melting / reflow step in ETP → bright finish + FeSn₂ alloy bond.
- [ ] Passivation with Na₂Cr₂O₇ → prevents SnO₂ growth, improves lacquer adhesion.
- [ ] 3-piece can has side-seam; 2-piece D&I does not.
- [ ] D&I process reduces wall from ~0.35 mm (slug) to ~0.06 mm (final wall).

**✅ Exam Traps to Avoid**
- [ ] Tin is **cathodic** to steel in food media — pinholes are dangerous.
- [ ] Always convert pressure to **MPa** before substituting in formulas.
- [ ] Use **radius**, not diameter, in stress equations.
- [ ] Aluminum cans require lacquer (acidic drinks dissolve Al₂O₃).
- [ ] Buckling formula is for **external** (vacuum) loading, not internal pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
