KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *Define "Overall Migration Limit" and "Specific Migration Limit" as applied to food-contact plastics. State the regulatory values used in the EU Regulation 10/2011.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Overall Migration Limit (OML):** The maximum permitted total quantity of *all* non-volatile substances (monomers, additives, oligomers) that can migrate from a food-contact material into a food simulant under defined test conditions. > - **EU Value:** **10 mg per dm²** of packaging surface (or **60 mg per kg** of food simulant). > > **Specific Migration Limit (SML):** The maximum permitted quantity of a *single identified* substance that can migrate from the packaging into the food. > - **Substance-specific.** Example: **Vinyl chloride monomer SML = 0.01 mg·kg⁻¹ food** (classified as a carcinogen). > - Specified in the *Union List* of authorised substances under EU 10/2011. **Mark Distribution:** - Definitions of OML and SML — **2 marks** - Numerical values with examples — **1 mark** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *What is "scalping" in food packaging? Give two examples with the type of polymer involved.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Scalping (Sorption):** The phenomenon in which certain food components — typically *flavour compounds, aroma volatiles, and fat-soluble vitamins* — are **absorbed into the polymer packaging wall**, resulting in loss of sensory quality and nutritional value of the food. > > **Examples:** > 1. **Orange juice in LDPE pouches** — Loss of *d-limonene* (citrus aroma) into the LDPE wall. > 2. **Milk in HDPE bottles** — Loss of *fat-soluble vitamins* (A, D, E, K) into the HDPE wall. > > The driving force is the **thermodynamic compatibility** (low Flory–Huggins $\chi_{12}$) between the food component and the polymer. **Mark Distribution:** - Definition — **1 mark** - Example 1 with explanation — **1 mark** - Example 2 with explanation — **1 mark** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Explain in detail the **three mechanisms of food–package interaction**: permeation, migration, and sorption (scalping). For each, give a representative example from real food packaging. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **polyethylene film of thickness 0.05 mm** is used to package a moisture-sensitive food product. The water vapour permeability of PE is $P = 6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ g} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$. The partial pressure difference across the film is $\Delta p_{wv} = 2100 \text{ Pa}$. Calculate the **WVTR** in $\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** **Permeation (2 marks):** Permeation is the diffusion of *small molecules* (O₂, CO₂, H₂O vapour, aroma) **through** the polymer film. The classical mechanism is the **solution–diffusion model** in three steps: (1) dissolution of permeant at the upstream face, (2) diffusion through the polymer matrix, (3) desorption at the downstream face. The governing equation is $J = P \cdot \Delta p / L$. *Example:* **O₂ permeation through BOPP** used in snack packaging — causes oxidative rancidity of chips if the OTR is too high. **Migration (2 marks):** Migration is the transfer of *low-molecular-weight substances* (monomers, plasticizers, antioxidants, oligomers) **from the polymer into the food**. It is regulated by OML (10 mg·dm⁻²) and SML (substance-specific). The driving force is concentration-gradient driven diffusion, with kinetics described by Crank's equation. *Example:* **Migration of styrene monomer from polystyrene cups** into hot beverages; **phthalate plasticizer migration from PVC** into fatty foods. **Sorption / Scalping (2 marks):** Sorption is the *reverse* of migration — food components (especially lipophilic and volatile compounds) partition **into the polymer wall**. This causes loss of flavour, aroma, and fat-soluble vitamins. The phenomenon depends on Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi_{12}$. *Example:* **Loss of d-limonene from orange juice packaged in LDPE pouches**; **loss of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D) from milk in HDPE bottles**. **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three mechanisms operate simultaneously in real packaging; the *dominant* effect depends on the polymer chemistry, food chemistry, temperature, and contact time. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given:** - Film thickness: $L = 0.05 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$ - Permeability: $P = 6 \times 10^{-13} \text{ g} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$ - Partial pressure difference: $\Delta p = 2100 \text{ Pa}$ **Step 1 — Apply the Permeation Equation:** $$ WVTR = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values:** $$ WVTR = \dfrac{(6 \times 10^{-13}) \times (2100)}{5 \times 10^{-5}} $$ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$ 6 \times 10^{-13} \times 2100 = 12600 \times 10^{-13} = 1.26 \times 10^{-9} \text{ g} \cdot \text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Division:** $$ WVTR = \dfrac{1.26 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.252 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} $$ $$ WVTR = 2.52 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to g·m⁻²·day⁻¹:** $(1 \text{ day} = 86400 \text{ s})$ $$ WVTR = 2.52 \times 10^{-5} \times 86400 $$ $$ WVTR = 2.177 \text{ g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{WVTR \approx 2.18 \text{ g} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1}} $$ This is a **moderate barrier** — acceptable for many dry foods but not for highly moisture-sensitive products like milk powder (where WVTR < 0.5 g·m⁻²·day⁻¹ is desired). **Mark Distribution (b):** - Equation statement — **1 mark** - Substitution of values — **1 mark** - Numerator — **1 mark** - Division — **1 mark** - Unit conversion to day — **1 mark** - Final numerical answer with units — **1 mark** - Interpretation/comment — **1 mark** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** Discuss the **effect of different packaging materials (glass, metals, plastics, paper)** on the packed commodity. Highlight specific migration and interaction issues for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **lag-time expression** $t_{ss} = L^2 / (6D)$ for diffusion through a polymer film. State its significance in determining diffusion coefficient experimentally. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** **Glass (1.5 marks):** - Essentially **inert** — no migration, no permeation. - **Interaction issues:** Light-catalysed reactions (e.g., "sunstruck" flavour in beer due to isohumulone + UV); metal cap corrosion by acidic foods. - **Effect on commodity:** Preserves flavour and nutrition best; suitable for premium products, preserves, beverages. **Metals (1.5 marks):** - **Impermeable** to gases and vapours. - **Interaction issues:** Acidic foods (tomato, citrus) corrode **bare tinplate** → Fe, Sn ion migration; aluminium corrodes at pH < 4 or > 8; **tin sulphide** blackening with eggs, fish. - **Mitigation:** Internal **lacquer coating** (epoxy-phenolic, organosol). - **Effect on commodity:** Excellent barrier; commonly used for canned foods, beverages, retort pouches (with laminate). **Plastics (2.5 marks):** - **PE/PP:** Low cost, good moisture barrier, but **scalping of aromas** in LDPE, additive migration (BHT, Irganox). - **PET:** Excellent clarity, moderate gas barrier, **acetaldehyde migration** in hot-fill. - **PS:** Migrates **styrene monomer** (suspected carcinogen) — limited use. - **PVC:** Migrates **phthalate plasticizers** (DEHP) and **vinyl chloride monomer** (regulated SML). - **EVOH/PVDC/PA:** Excellent O₂ barrier — but **moisture-sensitive**; used in multilayer. - **Effect on commodity:** Highly versatile but each polymer has its own chemical compatibility. **Paper & Paperboard (1.5 marks):** - **Highly permeable** to moisture and gases. - **Interaction issues:** Migration of **mineral oils (MOSH/MOAH)**, **printing inks**, **PFAS** (from grease-proof coatings), and **optical brighteners**. - **Mitigation:** PE extrusion coating, PLA bio-coating, or use of food-grade virgin pulp. - **Effect on commodity:** Suitable for dry, non-perishable foods (cereals, sugar, flour); needs barrier layer for moist/greasy foods. **Summary:** Choice of packaging material is dictated by the **food chemistry, storage conditions, regulatory limits, and consumer expectations**. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Set Up the Time-Dependent Diffusion Problem (1 mark):** Consider a polymer film of thickness $L$ exposed suddenly (at $t = 0$) to a constant permeant concentration $C_1$ on the upstream face and $C_2$ on the downstream face, with $C_2 = 0$ (vacuum or sweeping gas). Fick's second law applies: $$ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} $$ **Step 2 — Initial and Boundary Conditions (1 mark):** - $C(x, 0) = 0$ for $0 \leq x \leq L$ - $C(0, t) = C_1$ for $t > 0$ - $C(L, t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ **Step 3 — Solve Using Separation of Variables (2 marks):** The general solution is a Fourier series: $$ C(x, t) = C_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right) - \dfrac{2 C_1}{\pi} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi x}{L}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{D n^2 \pi^2 t}{L^2}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Flux (1 mark):** Differentiating with respect to $x$ and evaluating at $x = 0$ (upstream face): $$ J(t) = -D \left.\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = \dfrac{D C_1}{L}\left[1 + 2 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{D n^2 \pi^2 t}{L^2}\right)\right] $$ **Step 5 — Identify Steady-State and Lag Time (1 mark):** As $t \to \infty$, the exponential terms vanish, giving steady-state flux $J_\infty = D C_1 / L$. The intercept on the $t$-axis when $J(t)$ is extrapolated linearly from steady state is: $$ t_{ss} = \dfrac{L^2}{6 D} $$ **Step 6 — Significance (1 mark):** The lag time is measured experimentally in a **permeation cell** by plotting $J(t)$ vs. $t$, fitting the linear portion, and finding the $x$-intercept. The diffusivity is then calculated as: $$ D = \dfrac{L^2}{6 \cdot t_{ss}} $$ This is the basis of the **ASTM D1434 / D6701** standard test methods for measuring diffusion coefficients in packaging films. **Mark Distribution (b):** - Setting up diffusion equation + BCs — **2 marks** - Solving by separation of variables — **2 marks** - Flux derivation — **1 mark** - Lag-time expression — **1 mark** - Experimental significance — **1 mark** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** > 1. **Confusing OML with SML:** Students often write "migration limit = 10 mg·kg⁻¹" without specifying that OML is **10 mg·dm⁻²** (of *packaging*) and **60 mg·kg⁻¹** (of *food*) — both are EU-accepted equivalents. Examiner will deduct **0.5–1 mark** if the unit context is missing. > 2. **Mixing up permeation and migration:** Permeation is *through* the wall; migration is *into* the food. **Don't interchange them** in a 14-mark answer. > 3. **Forgetting to convert units in numerical problems:** OTR is often asked in $\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{day}^{-1} \cdot \text{atm}^{-1}$, but students forget the **atm⁻¹** in the denominator. Always carry units through the calculation. > 4. **Skipping the "long-time" vs. "short-time" distinction in Crank's equation:** For $M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$, use the $\sqrt{t}$ approximation; for $M_t/M_\infty > 0.5$, the full series is required. > 5. **Not mentioning the regulatory framework:** For "Define OML" type questions, a 14-mark answer is incomplete without **citing at least one regulation** (FSSAI, EU 10/2011, or US FDA 21 CFR). > 6. **Confusing lag time with breakthrough time:** Lag time ($L^2/6D$) is the *x-intercept* of the steady-state extrapolation; *breakthrough time* (often $0.5 \cdot L^2/D$ or longer) is when the permeant is *first detected* downstream. > 7. **Neglecting temperature dependence:** Always mention that diffusivity follows Arrhenius behaviour, $D = D_0 \exp(-E_d/RT)$ — and that at hot-fill or retort temperatures, migration rates increase **5–10×** compared to ambient. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Effect of Materials on Packed Commodities** **Core Concepts (must remember):** - ☐ Food–package interaction is governed by **3 mass-transfer mechanisms**: **Permeation, Migration, Sorption**. - ☐ **Permeation** follows the **solution–diffusion model**: $P = D \cdot S$. - ☐ **Fick's First Law** for steady-state: $J = -D (dC/dx)$. - ☐ **Fick's Second Law** for transient: $\partial C / \partial t = D \, \partial^2 C / \partial x^2$. - ☐ **Migration** is regulated by **OML = 10 mg·dm⁻²** (EU) and substance-specific **SML** values. - ☐ **Crank's equation** governs migration kinetics from a finite slab; $\sqrt{t}$ law holds for short times. - ☐ **Lag time** $t_{ss} = L^2 / 6D$ is used to experimentally determine diffusivity. - ☐ **Diffusivity** increases exponentially with temperature: $D = D_0 \exp(-E_d/RT)$. - ☐ **Scalping** is the *reverse* of migration; depends on **Flory–Huggins $\chi_{12}$** parameter. **Material-Specific Effects (must remember):** - ☐ **Glass:** Inert, impermeable, but light-sensitive foods need amber/UV-blocking glass. - ☐ **Metals:** Tinplate → Sn²⁺/Fe migration in acidic foods; mitigated by lacquer. - ☐ **Plastics:** PE/PP → moisture barrier; EVOH/PVDC/PA → O₂ barrier; LDPE → severe scalping. - ☐ **Paper:** High permeation, possible mineral oil/ink migration; needs PE coating. - ☐ **Multilayer films:** Combine advantages — e.g., **PET/Al/PE** for retort pouches. **Numerical Constants (must remember):** - ☐ $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$ - ☐ OML (EU) = $10 \text{ mg·dm}^{-2}$ = $60 \text{ mg·kg}^{-1}$ food - ☐ SML examples: Vinyl chloride = $0.01 \text{ mg·kg}^{-1}$; DEHP = $1.5 \text{ mg·kg}^{-1}$ - ☐ Standard test conditions: $23 \text{ °C}, 50\% \text{ RH}$ (or $38 \text{ °C}, 90\% \text{ RH}$ for tropical) - ☐ $\sqrt{t}$ approximation valid for $M_t / M_\infty < 0.5$ **Commonly Tested (KTU Priority):** - ☐ Permeation equation and its derivation — **HIGH PRIORITY** - ☐ Migration calculation using Crank's equation — **HIGH PRIORITY** - ☐ Comparison of barrier properties of different materials — **HIGH PRIORITY** - ☐ Regulatory migration limits and their significance — **MEDIUM PRIORITY** - ☐ Lag-time method for determining $D$ — **MEDIUM PRIORITY** - ☐ Scalping phenomenon with examples — **MEDIUM PRIORITY** - ☐ Effect of temperature on migration (Arrhenius) — **MEDIUM PRIORITY** - ☐ Selection of packaging material for specific foods — **HIGH PRIORITY**

topics.title: Package Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Package Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Package Testing

**Package Testing** is the systematic and scientific evaluation of packaging materials and the finished package using standardized mechanical, physical, chemical, barrier, and environmental test methods to determine whether the package can adequately protect, preserve, contain, and inform the enclosed food product through its entire intended supply chain, from the point of manufacture to the point of consumption.

As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT863 - Food Packaging Technology) Module 3, package testing is classified into the following broad categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must demonstrate working knowledge of **(i) mechanical tests, (ii) barrier tests, (iii) chemical/migration tests, (iv) seal integrity tests, and (v) distribution/transport simulation tests**, along with their governing standards (ASTM, TAPPI, ISO, BIS).

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Hospital Check-up for a Package"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine your food package as a person going for a complete medical check-up before being sent on a long, rough journey.
> 
> - **Mechanical tests** = checking if the "bones" (structure) are strong enough (Tensile, Burst, Tear, Puncture).
> - **Barrier tests** = checking if the "skin" can block harmful elements like moisture and oxygen (WVTR, OTR).
> - **Seal tests** = checking if the "stitches" (heat seals) will not open up under stress.
> - **Chemical tests** = ensuring the "body" does not leak any harmful chemicals into the food.
> - **Distribution tests** = simulating a bumpy truck ride, a sudden drop, and stack pressure to see if the package survives.
> 
> Just like a doctor uses specific instruments (stethoscope, X-ray, blood test), packaging engineers use instruments like the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**, **Permeability Analyzer (MOCON)**, and **Drop Tester**.

## 1.3 Why Package Testing is Indispensable

1. **Regulatory Compliance** — FSSAI, USFDA, EU regulations demand documented evidence of package safety.
2. **Shelf-Life Validation** — A package must preserve food quality for the labeled shelf life.
3. **Brand Protection** — A leaking or bursting package destroys consumer trust and brand equity.
4. **Cost Optimization** — Over-engineered packages waste material; under-engineered ones cause product loss.
5. **Logistics Assurance** — Ensures the package survives the entire **Cold Chain / Distribution Chain**.

## 1.4 Governing Standards Bodies (Mandatory for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **ASTM International** (American Society for Testing and Materials) — e.g., ASTM D882, D1709, F1929
> - **TAPPI** (Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry)
> - **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) — e.g., ISO 2859 sampling plans
> - **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — IS standards for food contact materials
> - **ISTA** (International Safe Transit Association) — Distribution simulation protocols 1A, 2A, 3A, etc.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Package Tests

Package testing is broadly divided into two levels:

### A. **Material-Level Tests (Performed on films, sheets, laminates)**
- **Tensile Test** — Measures pulling strength.
- **Elongation at Break** — Measures stretchability.
- **Burst Strength (Mullen Test)** — Measures bursting pressure resistance.
- **Tear Resistance (Elmendorf Tear)** — Measures resistance to tear propagation.
- **Puncture Resistance** — Measures resistance to sharp objects.
- **Cof (Coefficient of Friction)** — Measures slipperiness of films on production lines.
- **WVTR (Water Vapor Transmission Rate)** — Measures moisture barrier.
- **OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate)** — Measures oxygen barrier.
- **CO₂TR / N₂TR** — Gas-specific transmission.

### B. **Package-Level Tests (Performed on finished pouches/bottles/cartons)**
- **Seal Strength Test** — Heat seal integrity.
- **Hot Tack Test** — Strength of seal immediately after sealing.
- **Leak Test (Dye Penetration, Bubble Leak Test, Vacuum Leak)** — Detects pinholes.
- **Drop Test** — Free-fall impact on corners, edges, faces.
- **Compression / Stack Test** — Top-load resistance.
- **Vibration Test** — Truck/ship vibration simulation.
- **Climatic / Accelerated Aging Test** — 45°C / 75% RH storage simulation.
- **Migration / Extractives Test** — Chemical leaching into food simulants.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic of Major Tests

### 2.2.1 Tensile Test (ASTM D882 for thin films)
**Step 1:** A dumbbell-shaped or rectangular film specimen of known **gauge length (L₀)** and **cross-sectional area (A)** is mounted between two grips.
**Step 2:** The Universal Testing Machine (UTM) applies an axial tensile load at a constant crosshead speed (typically **50 mm/min** or **500 mm/min**).
**Step 3:** Load (F) and elongation (ΔL) are continuously recorded.
**Step 4:** The stress-strain curve is plotted.
**Step 5:** Tensile Strength, Young's Modulus, and Elongation at Break are calculated.

### 2.2.2 Burst Strength (Mullen Test - ASTM D774)
**Step 1:** A flat film specimen is clamped over a circular rubber diaphragm (test area = **30.5 cm²** standard).
**Step 2:** Hydraulic pressure is applied from below.
**Step 3:** Pressure increases until the film ruptures.
**Step 4:** The burst pressure is read in **kPa** or **kg/cm²**.

### 2.2.3 Elmendorf Tear Test (ASTM D1922)
**Step 1:** A pendulum swings and tears a pre-slit specimen.
**Step 2:** Energy lost by the pendulum = energy absorbed in tearing.
**Step 3:** Tear resistance is reported in **grams-force (gf)** or **millinewtons (mN)**.

### 2.2.4 WVTR Test (ASTM F1249 / ASTM E96)
**Principle:** Differential pressure or modulated IR sensor method. The film separates a dry chamber from a humid chamber; water vapor permeates; the IR sensor measures vapor concentration vs. time.

### 2.2.5 OTR Test (ASTM D3985 / F2622)
**Principle:** A coulometric sensor measures the amount of oxygen passing through the film per unit area per unit time at specified temperature and humidity.

### 2.2.6 Heat Seal Strength Test (ASTM F88)
**Step 1:** Specimen strips are sealed under controlled conditions (temperature, pressure, dwell time).
**Step 2:** Sealed strips are pulled apart in the UTM at 90° or 180°.
**Step 3:** Maximum force per unit width (N/15 mm) is the seal strength.

### 2.2.7 Drop Test (ISTA 1A / ASTM D4169)
**Sequence:** Drop on face → edge → corner → opposite corner from specified heights (typically **760 mm to 1500 mm**) based on package weight.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU ESE numerical problems and derivations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard / Unit | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Tensile Strength ($\sigma$) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | ASTM D882; **MPa** or **N/mm²** | Force per unit cross-section |
| 2 | Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Ductility of film |
| 3 | Young's Modulus ($E$) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A \cdot \Delta L}$ | **MPa** | Stiffness |
| 4 | Tear Resistance (Elmendorf) | $T = \dfrac{E_{lost}}{d}$ where $d$ = tear length | **gf** or **mN** | Resistance to tear propagation |
| 5 | Burst Strength | Direct reading of rupture pressure | ASTM D774; **kPa** | Pressure tolerance |
| 6 | WVTR | $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | **g/m²/day** | Moisture barrier |
| 7 | WVP (Permeability) | $WVP = \dfrac{WVTR \cdot x}{\Delta p}$ | **g·cm/m²·day·Pa** | Intrinsic material property |
| 8 | OTR | $OTR = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta c}{A \cdot t}$ | **cc/m²/day** | Oxygen barrier |
| 9 | Permeance | $P = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p}$ | **mol·m/m²·s·Pa** | Combined transport property |
| 10 | Drop Impact Energy | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | **Joules** | Kinetic potential energy |
| 11 | Compression (Top Load) | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{contact}}$ | **kPa** | Stack load tolerance |
| 12 | Migration Limit (Overall) | $OM \le 10 \text{ mg/dm}^2$ | EU 10/2011 | Total non-volatile transfer |
| 13 | Shelf Life from OTR | $t_{sl} = \dfrac{\text{Headspace O}_2 \text{ tolerance}}{OTR \cdot A_{pkg}}$ | **days** | Oxygen-limited shelf life |
| 14 | Seal Strength | $S = \dfrac{F_{peak}}{W_{seal}}$ | **N/15 mm** | Heat seal integrity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas directly drive **material selection** (e.g., choosing EVOH vs. PVDC for oxygen barrier), **laminate design** (PET/Al-foil/PE for retort pouches), and **shelf-life claims** printed on retail packs.

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Application of Package Testing

- **Nestlé & Amul** validate retort pouches using **seal strength, burst, and OTR** tests before commercial launch.
- **Coca-Cola PET bottles** undergo **top-load compression** and **CO₂ retention** tests to ensure carbonation for 6+ months.
- **Amazon "Frustration-Free" packaging** uses ISTA 6-Amazon.com protocols to pass **drop + vibration** without secondary boxes.
- **FSSAI** requires **overall migration** testing on all plastic articles intended for food contact (Ref. IS 9845).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Tensile Stress, Strain, and Young's Modulus (ASTM D882 Logic)

Let us derive the mechanical response of a packaging film under uniaxial tension. Consider a rectangular film specimen of:
- Original gauge length = $L_0$
- Original cross-sectional area = $A_0 = w \times t$ (width $\times$ thickness)

A tensile force $F$ is applied along the longitudinal axis.

**Step 1 — Definition of Engineering Stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}
$$

*Logic:* Stress is defined as the internal resisting force per unit area of the original cross-section.

**Step 2 — Definition of Engineering Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}
$$

*Logic:* Strain is the fractional change in length relative to the original length.

**Step 3 — Within the elastic (Hookean) region:**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

*Logic:* Hooke's Law states stress is directly proportional to strain in the elastic regime.

**Step 4 — Substituting (1) and (2) into (3):**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F / A_0}{\Delta L / L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}
$$

*Logic:* The slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the stress–strain curve gives Young's Modulus $E$, a measure of film stiffness.

**Step 5 — Expressing in units:**

$$
E \;[\text{MPa}] = \frac{F \;[\text{N}] \times L_0 \;[\text{mm}]}{w \;[\text{mm}] \times t \;[\mu\text{m}] \times \Delta L \;[\text{mm}]} \times 10^{3}
$$

*Logic:* The $10^{3}$ factor converts $\text{N}/\mu\text{m}^{2}$ into MPa.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculation of Burst Strength for a Laminated Pouch

**Given Problem-Type:** A retort pouch has a burst strength of **350 kPa**. The food inside expands by **8%** in volume when heated to **121 °C** in an autoclave. The pouch surface area is **600 cm²**. Determine if the pouch will burst. Take modulus of elasticity $E$ of the laminate as **1.8 GPa**, and assume thin-wall membrane theory.

**Step 1 — Compute Internal Pressure Due to Volume Expansion:**

For an ideal gas assumption (headspace = 20% of pouch volume):

$$
P_{int} = P_0 \cdot \frac{V_0}{V_f} = 101.3 \times \frac{100}{108} = 93.8 \text{ kPa}
$$

*Logic:* Pressure drops as volume increases. The external atmospheric pressure pushes inward.

**Step 2 — Net Pressure Differential on Pouch Wall:**

$$
\Delta P = P_{ext} - P_{int} = 101.3 - 93.8 = 7.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

*Logic:* The pouch wall experiences a net compressive inward pressure of 7.5 kPa.

**Step 3 — Hoop Stress in a Spherical Membrane (Thin-Wall Theory):**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 t}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume pouch approximates a sphere of radius $r$:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{A}{4\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{600}{4 \pi}} = 6.91 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 5 — Laminate thickness $t = 110 \; \mu\text{m}$ (typical PET/Al/PP):**

$$
\sigma_h = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{3} \times 0.0691}{2 \times 110 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{518.25}{2.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.356 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to Burst Strength:**

$$
\text{Burst Strength} = 350 \text{ kPa} = 0.35 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Since $\sigma_h = 2.356 \text{ MPa} \gg 0.35 \text{ MPa}$, the pouch WILL BURST.**

*Logic:* The internal pressure differential induces hoop stress exceeding the burst strength of the laminate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Fix:** Either reduce headspace (<5%) or use a stronger laminate (PET/Aluminum Foil/RCPP with burst strength > 500 kPa).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shelf-Life Calculation Based on OTR (Oxygen-Limited)

**Given:** A 200 g potato chip pack of surface area **A = 400 cm²** uses a metallized film with **OTR = 1.5 cc/m²/day** at **23 °C / 50% RH**. The package contains **600 cc of headspace air (20.9% O₂)**. The product can tolerate a maximum of **5 cc O₂** ingress before rancidity. Calculate the oxygen-limited shelf life.

**Step 1 — Initial O₂ in Headspace:**

$$
O_{2, \text{initial}} = 600 \times 0.209 = 125.4 \text{ cc}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert OTR to Pack Area Basis:**

$$
OTR_{\text{pkg}} = 1.5 \text{ cc/m}^2/\text{day} \times 0.04 \text{ m}^2 = 0.06 \text{ cc/day}
$$

*Logic:* Pack area = 400 cm² = 0.04 m².

**Step 3 — Total Oxygen Tolerance (Initial + Ingress Limit):**

$$
O_{2, \text{max}} = 125.4 + 5 = 130.4 \text{ cc}
$$

**Step 4 — Time for Headspace O₂ to Reach Maximum (Simplified Model):**

$$
t_{sl} = \frac{5 \text{ cc}}{0.06 \text{ cc/day}} = 83.3 \text{ days}
$$

*Logic:* Since initial O₂ is already 125.4 cc and product tolerates 130.4 cc total, only 5 cc additional O₂ can enter.

**Step 5 — Apply Accelerated Aging Correction (Q₁₀ Rule, factor 2.0 per 10 °C):**

For storage at **35 °C** (12 °C above 23 °C), the accelerating factor is:

$$
AF = 2^{(35-23)/10} = 2^{1.2} = 2.297
$$

**Step 6 — Realistic Shelf Life at 35 °C:**

$$
t_{real} = \frac{83.3}{2.297} = 36.3 \text{ days}
$$

*Logic:* Higher storage temperature drastically reduces the safe shelf life.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Migration from Plastic into Food (EU Model)

For a plastic film of thickness $L$, the diffusion coefficient $D$, and contact area $A$, the migrated mass $M_t$ at time $t$ from a single-side contact is given by Fick's Second Law solution (Crank, 1975):

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{8}{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2} \exp\left[ -\frac{D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{4 L^2} \right]
$$

For short times ($M_t / M_\infty < 0.5$):

$$
M_t \approx \frac{2 A}{L} \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot t}{\pi}} \cdot C_0
$$

*Logic:* The migrated quantity scales as $\sqrt{t}$ initially, indicating a diffusion-controlled process where $C_0$ is the initial concentration of migrant in the polymer.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix to identify the correct test for a given failure mode.

| Failure Mode in the Field | Probable Root Cause | Recommended Test | Standard |
|--------------------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------|
| Bag punctured by a bone | Low puncture resistance | Puncture Resistance Test | ASTM F1306 |
| Seal opens during transit | Insufficient heat seal | Heat Seal Strength Test | ASTM F88 |
| Chips go stale early | High OTR | Oxygen Transmission Rate | ASTM D3985 |
| Powder cakes (moisture gain) | High WVTR | Water Vapor Transmission | ASTM F1249 |
| Laminate delaminates | Poor interlayer adhesion | Bond / Peel Strength | ASTM F904 |
| Bottle cracks in stack | Insufficient top load | Compression Test | ASTM D642 |
| Chemical taste in food | Migration of additives | Overall Migration Test | EU 10/2011 |
| Leak in vacuum pack | Pinhole in seal | Dye Penetration / Bubble Test | ASTM F1929 |
| Carton crushes in transit | Low ECT / BCT | Edge Crush Test (ECT) | TAPPI T811 |
| Seal breaks on form-fill-seal | Weak hot tack | Hot Tack Test | ASTM F1921 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: Complete Package Testing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Package Received for Testing] --> B{Test Level?}
    B -->|Material Level| C[Mechanical Tests]
    B -->|Package Level| D[Performance Tests]

    C --> C1[Tensile - D882]
    C --> C2[Elmendorf Tear - D1922]
    C --> C3[Burst - D774]
    C --> C4[Puncture - F1306]
    C --> C5[COF - D1894]

    D --> D1[Barrier Tests]
    D --> D2[Seal Integrity Tests]
    D --> D3[Distribution Tests]
    D --> D4[Chemical / Migration]

    D1 --> D1a[WVTR - F1249]
    D1 --> D1b[OTR - D3985]
    D1 --> D1c[CO2TR - F2476]

    D2 --> D2a[Seal Strength - F88]
    D2 --> D2b[Hot Tack - F1921]
    D2 --> D3c[Leak Test - F1929]

    D3 --> D3a[Drop - ISTA 1A]
    D3 --> D3b[Vibration - ISTA 2A]
    D3 --> D3c[Compression - ISTA 3A]

    D4 --> D4a[Overall Migration - EU 10]
    D4 --> D4b[Specific Migration - EFSA]
    D4 --> D4c[Sensor. Analysis - GC-MS]

    C1 --> E[Result Analysis]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    C5 --> E
    D1a --> E
    D1b --> E
    D1c --> E
    D2a --> E
    D2b --> E
    D3c --> E
    D3a --> E
    D3b --> E
    D3c --> E
    D4a --> E
    D4b --> E
    D4c --> E

    E --> F{Pass / Fail?}
    F -->|Pass| G[Approve for Commercial Release]
    F -->|Fail| H[Redesign Material / Process]
    H --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Universal Testing Machine (UTM) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Load Cell] --> C1[Signal Conditioner]
    C1 --> DAQ[Data Acquisition Card]
    E1[Extensometer] --> C1
    M1[Servo Motor] --> DR[Drive Mechanism]
    DR --> C1
    DAQ --> PC[PC with Test Software]
    PC --> DISP[Display Stress vs Strain Curve]
    PC --> CALC[Calculate Tensile, Modulus, Elongation]
    CALC --> RPT[Generate Test Report PDF]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: WVTR / OTR Permeation Analyzer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SAMP[Test Film Mounted in Cell] --> CL[Divided Test Cell]
    CL --> CHAMBER1[Top Chamber: Test Gas - O2 or Water Vapor]
    CL --> CHAMBER2[Bottom Chamber: Carrier Gas - N2]
    CHAMBER2 --> SENS[Sensor - Coulometric or IR]
    SENS --> CALC[Microprocessor Calculator]
    CALC --> DISP[Display OTR or WVTR in cc/m2/day]
    CALC --> OUT[Output to Computer]
    TC[Thermostat] -.-> CL
    RH[Humidity Controller] -.-> CL
```

## 4.4 Process Diagram: ISTA Distribution Simulation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PKG[Packaged Product] --> ST1[Vibration Test - 1 hr Random]
    ST1 --> ST2[Drop Test - Corners, Edges, Faces]
    ST2 --> ST3[Compression Test - Top Load]
    ST3 --> ST4[Atmospheric Conditioning - 45C 75% RH]
    ST4 --> ST5[Final Inspection for Damage]
    ST5 --> DEC{Acceptable?}
    DEC -->|Yes| APPROVE[Approve Distribution Cycle]
    DEC -->|No| REDESIGN[Modify Package Geometry / Material]
    REDESIGN --> PKG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define Package Testing. List any four categories of package tests. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Package testing is the scientific evaluation of packaging materials and the finished package to verify that they meet the functional, protective, and regulatory requirements of the food product and its distribution chain.

**Four categories of package tests:**
1. **Mechanical Tests** — Tensile, tear, burst, puncture.
2. **Barrier Tests** — WVTR, OTR, CO₂TR.
3. **Chemical / Migration Tests** — Overall and specific migration.
4. **Distribution / Transport Tests** — Drop, vibration, compression, climatic conditioning.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Hot Tack and Heat Seal Strength. State the relevant ASTM standard. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Heat Seal Strength | Hot Tack |
|---------|-------------------|----------|
| **Definition** | Force required to pull apart a *cooled* heat seal | Strength of seal *immediately* after sealing (within 0.2–1 s) |
| **Test Temperature** | Room temperature (23 °C) | Elevated, immediately post-seal |
| **Test Significance** | Long-term package integrity | Performance on FFS lines (form-fill-seal) |
| **ASTM Standard** | ASTM F88 | ASTM F1921 |

Hot tack is critical for high-speed vertical form-fill-seal (VFFS) lines where the seal is pulled apart before cooling.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Mechanical & Barrier Testing in Depth*

> **[a]** A biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) film of thickness 30 µm and width 15 mm is subjected to a tensile test. The film breaks at a force of 18 N, and the gauge length increases from 50 mm to 165 mm. Calculate the **Tensile Strength, Elongation at Break, and Young's Modulus** (assuming elastic region ends at 0.05 strain, force 6 N). [7 Marks]

> **[b]** A flexible retort pouch made of PET/Al-foil/CPP laminate has an OTR of 0.05 cc/m²/day. The pouch contains 800 cc of headspace air and 500 g of cooked rice. The product tolerates a maximum O₂ ingress of 10 cc. The pouch surface area is 700 cm². Calculate the **oxygen-limited shelf life** and apply a Q₁₀ acceleration factor of 2.5 for storage at 38 °C. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Mechanical Properties of BOPP

**Given Data:**
- Thickness $t = 30 \; \mu\text{m} = 30 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$
- Width $w = 15 \; \text{mm} = 0.015 \; \text{m}$
- Breaking force $F_{break} = 18 \; \text{N}$
- Original gauge length $L_0 = 50 \; \text{mm}$
- Final length $L_f = 165 \; \text{mm}$
- Elastic end point: $\varepsilon = 0.05$, $F = 6 \; \text{N}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = w \times t = 0.015 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Tensile Strength at Break:**

$$
\sigma_{break} = \frac{F_{break}}{A_0} = \frac{18}{4.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.0 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} = 40 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Elongation at Break:**

$$
\varepsilon_{break} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\% = \frac{165 - 50}{50} \times 100\% = 230\%
$$

**Step 4 — Stress at Elastic Limit:**

$$
\sigma_{elastic} = \frac{6}{4.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.333 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa} = 13.33 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Young's Modulus (within elastic region):**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_{elastic}}{\varepsilon_{elastic}} = \frac{13.33 \times 10^{6}}{0.05} = 2.667 \times 10^{8} \; \text{Pa} = 266.7 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma = 40 \; \text{MPa}$, $\varepsilon = 230\%$, $E = 266.7 \; \text{MPa}$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Oxygen-Limited Shelf Life of Retort Pouch

**Given Data:**
- OTR = 0.05 cc/m²/day
- Headspace = 800 cc; Initial O₂ in headspace = $800 \times 0.209 = 167.2 \; \text{cc}$
- Pouch area $A = 700 \; \text{cm}^2 = 0.07 \; \text{m}^2$
- O₂ tolerance = 10 cc
- $Q_{10}$ factor at 38 °C = 2.5

**Step 1 — OTR in Pack-Area Basis:**

$$
OTR_{pkg} = 0.05 \times 0.07 = 0.0035 \; \text{cc/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to Reach O₂ Tolerance:**

$$
t_{25°C} = \frac{10 \; \text{cc}}{0.0035 \; \text{cc/day}} = 2857 \; \text{days}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Q₁₀ Acceleration at 38 °C (ΔT = 15 °C from 23 °C):**

$$
\text{Rule applied: } AF = 2.5^{(38-23)/10} = 2.5^{1.5} = 3.95
$$

*Note:* Per KTU convention, the entire $Q_{10}$ factor is used if explicitly given. So:

$$
t_{38°C} = \frac{2857}{3.95} = 723 \; \text{days}
$$

**Final Answer:** Oxygen-limited shelf life ≈ **723 days at 38 °C** (or ~7.9 years), which is industrially acceptable for retort products.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Distribution, Seal & Migration Testing*

> **[a]** Explain the **drop test procedure** as per ISTA 1A protocol. A 25 kg carton of packaged food is to be dropped from 760 mm. Calculate the **impact energy** in joules. [7 Marks]

> **[b]** Define **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** and **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** as per EU Regulation 10/2011. A plastic film of 2 dm² area is in contact with a food simulant at 40 °C for 10 days. The film releases 8 mg of non-volatile residue per dm². Comment on the compliance status. [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Drop Test & Impact Energy

**Step 1 — ISTA 1A Drop Test Sequence:**

The package is dropped in the following sequence:
1. **Bottom face** drop
2. **Top face** drop
3. **Longest side (edge)** drop
4. **Shortest side (edge)** drop
5. **Smallest corner** drop
6. **Largest corner** drop
7. **Diagonally opposite corner** drop
8. **One additional corner** drop (in alternate sequence)

For each drop, the package must land on the designated surface, and the test height is determined from the package weight:

| Package Weight | Drop Height (ISTA 1A) |
|---------------|----------------------|
| 0–9 kg | 760 mm |
| 9.1–18 kg | 610 mm |
| 18.1–27 kg | 460 mm |

**Step 2 — Impact Energy for 25 kg Carton Dropped from 460 mm:**

**Correction:** Since 25 kg falls in 18.1–27 kg range, drop height $h = 460 \; \text{mm} = 0.46 \; \text{m}$.

$$
E = m \cdot g \cdot h = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.46 = 112.8 \; \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — Pass/Fail Criteria:**
After all drops, the package is opened and inspected. Acceptance criteria: **No product damage, no seal failure, no functional loss of container integrity.**

**Final Answer:** Impact energy ≈ **112.8 J**.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Migration Compliance

**Definitions:**

**Overall Migration Limit (OML):** The maximum permitted total amount of non-volatile substances that can migrate from a food contact material into a food simulant. As per EU 10/2011, the OML is **10 mg/dm²** of food contact surface.

**Specific Migration Limit (SML):** The maximum permitted amount of a *specific* substance that can migrate from a food contact material into a food simulant. SMLs are substance-specific and listed in Annex I of EU Regulation 10/2011.

**Step 1 — Given Conditions:**
- Film area = 2 dm²
- Test condition = 10 days at 40 °C
- Migration observed = 8 mg/dm²

**Step 2 — Compare with OML:**

$$
OML_{allowed} = 10 \; \text{mg/dm}^2
$$
$$
OML_{observed} = 8 \; \text{mg/dm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
Since $8 \; \text{mg/dm}^2 < 10 \; \text{mg/dm}^2$, the film **COMPLIES** with EU 10/2011 OML.

**Additional Remarks:**
- The test condition (10 days at 40 °C) corresponds to a long-term storage at ambient or refrigerated conditions, as per the EU simulant assignment.
- However, for retort conditions (121 °C / 30 min), a more severe test is required.
- The OML of 8 mg/dm² indicates the film is suitable for long-term contact with aqueous, acidic, and alcoholic foods.

**Final Answer:** Film **passes the Overall Migration test** under EU 10/2011; specific migration analysis of additives (e.g., plasticizers, antioxidants) is additionally recommended.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students frequently write tensile strength in **N/mm²** instead of **MPa**. Both are dimensionally identical numerically, but the SI standard is **MPa**.
> 2. **Forgetting the √t Dependency:** Migration problems must show Fickian √t logic for short times; blindly applying steady-state is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Drop Test Height Confusion:** Students assume 760 mm for all weights. ISTA specifies **variable height based on mass** (0–9 kg: 760 mm; 9–18 kg: 610 mm; 18–27 kg: 460 mm).
> 4. **WVTR vs WVP Mix-up:** WVTR is **thickness-independent** (g/m²/day); WVP **includes thickness** in the numerator. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Seal Strength Units:** Heat seal strength is reported in **N/15 mm**, not N/mm or N. Forgetting the 15 mm normalization costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping Final Inspection in Drop Test:** A drop test is incomplete without the post-test inspection of package and product. Always state the pass/fail acceptance criteria.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Read this in the last 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- **Package Testing** = scientific evaluation of materials + finished packages.
- **Material-level tests** = tensile, tear, burst, puncture, COF.
- **Package-level tests** = seal strength, hot tack, drop, vibration, compression, leak, migration.
- **ASTM D882** governs tensile testing of thin plastic films.
- **ASTM D1922** governs Elmendorf tear resistance.
- **ASTM D774** governs burst strength (Mullen test).
- **ASTM F88** governs heat seal strength; reported in **N/15 mm**.
- **ASTM F1921** governs hot tack testing.
- **ASTM D3985** governs OTR (coulometric sensor).
- **ASTM F1249** governs WVTR (modulated IR sensor).
- **ISTA 1A** = drop test; **ISTA 2A** = vibration; **ISTA 3A** = compression.
- **EU 10/2011 OML** = **10 mg/dm²** of food contact surface.
- **WVTR units** = g/m²/day; **OTR units** = cc/m²/day; **Seal strength** = N/15 mm; **Burst** = kPa.
- **Tensile Strength** $\sigma = F/A_0$ (MPa); **Elongation** $\varepsilon = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100\%$.
- **Drop Impact Energy** $E = mgh$; for 25 kg at 460 mm ≈ **112.8 J**.
- **Q₁₀ Rule** = shelf life halves for every 10 °C rise (factor 2.0 default).
- **Fickian Migration**: $M_t \propto \sqrt{t}$ at short times.
- **Hot tack** = seal strength immediately after sealing (FFS lines).
- **Heat seal strength** = seal strength after cooling (long-term integrity).
- **Pass criteria** of drop test = no product damage, no seal failure, no functional loss.
- **Accelerated aging** = 45 °C / 75% RH for 90 days ≈ 1 year real-time.
- **Top-load compression** for PET bottles ensures stackability in warehouses.
- **Migration of plasticizers** from PVC into fatty foods is the classical SML problem.
- **Aluminum foil** offers near-zero OTR and WVTR — essential for retort and aseptic packs.
- **BOPP + Metallization** offers OTR < 1 cc/m²/day — standard for snack packs.
- **EVOH** is the highest barrier clear plastic — used in vacuum and MAP packs.
- **Always state the governing standard** in KTU answers — naming ASTM D882, F88, etc. earns extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Printing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Printing in Food Packaging** is the controlled, reproducible process of transferring a predetermined image, text, or graphical information onto a packaging substrate (paper, paperboard, plastic film, metal, or glass) using specialized inks, dyes, or toners, while simultaneously ensuring that the printed layer is **safe for direct or indirect food contact**, mechanically robust enough to survive the supply chain, and aesthetically aligned with brand and regulatory requirements.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT863), the term *Printing* is studied as a **secondary packaging operation** that integrates:

- **Substrate science** (surface energy, roughness, porosity)
- **Rheology of inks** (viscosity, tack, drying kinetics)
- **Color science & metamerism**
- **Food-contact safety regulations** (FDA 21 CFR, EU Regulation 1935/2004, FSSAI, IS 15495)
- **Mechanical durability** (rub, scratch, scuff, light-fastness)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **rubber stamp on a parcel**, but performed at industrial speed, on rolls of plastic, with microscopic precision and seven-color fidelity.

Imagine you are a baker. You want to put your shop name and ingredients list on a **transparent plastic pouch** that will hold biscuits. The pouch is a **slippery, non-absorbent surface**. Ordinary ballpoint pen ink will smear. Water-based poster paint will bead up. So you need a **specialized ink** that *sticks* to plastic, *dries fast*, and *does not migrate into the food* inside. That, in essence, is the problem the packaging industry solves through **print technology, ink chemistry, and surface engineering**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Packaging Printing

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Print speed** | $v$ | 50 – 600 m/min | Throughput |
| **Anilox line screen** | $N_L$ | 80 – 1200 lpi | Ink transfer volume |
| **Wet ink film thickness** | $t_w$ | 1 – 10 µm | Optical density |
| **Print resolution** | $R_p$ | 150 – 2540 dpi | Image sharpness |
| **Surface tension of substrate** | $\gamma_s$ | **≥ 38 dynes/cm** | Wettability requirement |
| **Ink viscosity** | $\eta$ | 15 – 60 s (DIN 4 cup) | Press stability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 38 dynes/cm threshold?** A plastic film must have a surface energy at least **8–10 dynes/cm higher** than the ink's surface tension for proper wetting. Most inks have $\gamma_{ink} \approx 30$ dynes/cm, hence the **38 dynes/cm rule-of-thumb** for corona-treated films.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Four Pillars" of Packaging Printing

Any successful print job rests on four interdependent pillars. If any one fails, the print fails.

### Pillar 1 — **Substrate Wettability**
The substrate's surface energy must exceed the ink's surface tension.

$$\gamma_s \;\geq\; \gamma_{ink} \;+\; 8 \text{ to } 10 \;(\text{dynes/cm})$$

*If $\gamma_s < \gamma_{ink}$*, ink beads up → **reticulation, pinholes, poor adhesion**.

### Pillar 2 — **Ink Transfer Volume**
The volume of ink delivered per unit area is governed by the **anilox cell geometry** in flexo/gravure.

$$V_{ink} = N_L \times V_{cell} \times \phi_{transfer}$$

where:
- $N_L$ = anilox line screen (cells per inch)
- $V_{cell}$ = volume of one engraved cell (mL/m² or BCM — billion cubic microns per square inch)
- $\phi_{transfer}$ = cell-transfer efficiency (typically **40 – 70 %**)

### Pillar 3 — **Drying / Curing Kinetics**
For water-based and solvent-based inks:

$$t_{dry} = \frac{\rho_{ink} \cdot t_w \cdot x_{solvent}}{D_{eff} \cdot C_{sat}}$$

For UV-curable inks:

$$E_{cure} = P_{lamp} \times t_{exposure} \;\geq\; E_{threshold}$$

### Pillar 4 — **Adhesion & Resistance**
Measured by **cross-hatch tape test (ASTM D3359)** giving a 0B – 5B rating.

## 2.2 The Major Printing Processes — Comparison Matrix

| Process | Image Carrier | Ink Type | Typical Substrates | Speed (m/min) | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flexography** | Flexible photopolymer plate | Water / Solvent / UV | Films, corrugated, foils, paper | 200 – 600 | Versatile, low cost |
| **Rotogravure** | Engraved metal cylinder | Solvent / Water | Films, foils, labels | 300 – 600 | Premium quality, long runs |
| **Offset Litho** | PS plate + blanket | Oxidizing / UV | Cartons, labels, paperboard | 100 – 300 | Sharper text & graphics |
| **Digital (Inkjet)** | None (non-impact) | Water / UV / Latex | Variable data, short runs | 30 – 100 | Personalization, no plate |
| **Digital (Electrophotography)** | None (laser) | Dry toner | Labels, cartons | 10 – 60 | Photo-quality, food-safe toners |
| **Screen** | Mesh stencil | Plastisol / UV | Glass, metal, thick films | 5 – 50 | Heavy ink deposit, 3D effects |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_s \geq \gamma_{ink} + 8\text{–}10$ dynes/cm | Wettability check |
| 2 | $V_{ink} = N_L \cdot V_{cell} \cdot \phi_{transfer}$ | Ink volume delivered |
| 3 | $\text{BCM} = \text{cells/in}^2 \times \text{cell vol}$ | Anilox specification |
| 4 | $E_{cure} = P \cdot t$ | UV dose received (mJ/cm²) |
| 5 | $L^*a^*b^*$ ΔE tolerance: **ΔE ≤ 2** | Color-matching acceptance |
| 6 | Tack $= \dfrac{F_{split}}{v}$ | Ink tack in offset |
| 7 | $R_a$ (roughness) **≤ 0.8 µm** | Print smoothness requirement |
| 8 | $\text{Set-off \%} = \dfrac{D_{reverse}}{D_{fresh}} \times 100$ | Drying adequacy (target **< 15 %**) |
| 9 | Cobb value $\leq 30 \text{ g/m}^2$ | Paper absorbency for offset |
| 10 | $\phi_{transfer} = 1 - e^{-k t_w}$ | Empirical ink transfer efficiency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Brand protection** — anti-counterfeit printing (microtext, UV-fluorescent inks, holograms)
- **Cold-chain traceability** — variable-data inkjet printing of batch codes, QR codes
- **Active packaging** — oxygen-scavenging inks, time-temperature indicator inks
- **Sustainability** — water-based & bio-based ink systems replacing solvent-borne inks
- **Smart packaging** — conductive ink printing of RFID antennas on packaging

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Anilox Line Screen and Ink Lay-Down

### Step 1: Define the volume of one anilox cell
A standard hexagonal/honeycomb cell is modeled as a pyramidal/hexagonal well. For an engraved cell of depth $d$ and opening width $w$:

$$V_{cell} = k \cdot w^2 \cdot d$$

where $k$ is a geometric factor (≈ 0.866 for hex, ≈ 1.0 for pyramidal).

### Step 2: Relate cell count to line screen
$N_L$ is the number of cells per linear inch. The number of cells per square inch:

$$N_{cells} = N_L^2 \cdot \alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the **cell-packing density factor** (0.55 – 0.83 depending on cell geometry).

### Step 3: Compute total ink volume per unit area (in BCM)
**BCM** = Billion Cubic Microns per Square Inch. Multiply cell volume × cell count, then scale.

$$V_{ink}\,(\text{BCM}) = \frac{V_{cell}\,(\mu m^3) \times N_{cells}}{10^9}$$

### Step 4: Apply transfer efficiency

$$V_{transferred} = V_{ink} \times \phi_{transfer}$$

### Step 5: Convert to wet film thickness

$$t_w = \frac{V_{transferred} \times 10^{-6}}{A_{substrate}}$$

For an anilox of $N_L = 800$ lpi, $V_{cell} = 4.5$ BCM, $\alpha = 0.7$, $\phi_{transfer} = 0.55$:

$$V_{transferred} = 4.5 \times 10^9 \times 800^2 \times 0.7 \times 0.55 \times 10^{-9} \; \mu m^3/\text{in}^2$$

$$\boxed{V_{transferred} \approx 1.10 \times 10^6 \;\mu m^3/\text{in}^2 \;\Rightarrow\; t_w \approx 1.7 \;\mu m}$$

This matches the empirical **1 – 3 µm** wet-film range for flexo process printing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Corona Treatment Level for a Given Substrate

### Step 1: Start with the Young-Dupré work of adhesion

$$W_{adh} = \gamma_s + \gamma_{ink} - \gamma_{int}$$

### Step 2: For spreading to be spontaneous, $W_{adh} \geq 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^{d}\gamma_{ink}^{d}} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^{p}\gamma_{ink}^{p}}$

where superscripts $d$ = dispersive, $p$ = polar (Fowkes / Owens-Wendt-Rabel-Kaelble model).

### Step 3: For a typical solvent-based ink $\gamma_{ink} = 30$ dynes/cm with $\gamma_{ink}^{d} = 26$, $\gamma_{ink}^{p} = 4$
Solving for minimum $\gamma_s^p$ gives:

$$\gamma_s^p \geq \frac{\left(2\sqrt{\gamma_{ink}^{p}}\right)^2}{4} \geq 4 \; \text{dynes/cm polar component}$$

### Step 4: Total substrate surface energy required

$$\gamma_s \geq 30 + 8 = 38 \; \text{dynes/cm}$$

### Step 5: Polyethylene (PE) naturally has $\gamma_s \approx 31$ dynes/cm, so **corona treatment** must raise it by:

$$\Delta \gamma_{corona} = 38 - 31 = 7 \; \text{dynes/cm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dyne inks** (formamide/ethyl-cellulose mixtures) are used in production to *verify* the surface energy of a treated film at the line-speed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Print Defects, Root Cause & Remedy

| Defect | Symptom | Probable Root Cause | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pinholes** | Tiny bare spots | Substrate contamination, low $\gamma_s$ | Corona / rewash, filtration |
| **Mottle** | Uneven ink density | Anilox wear, ink viscosity too high | Replace anilox, dilute ink |
| **Hickeys** | Foreign-particle void | Dust, dried ink skin | Cover press, install filtration |
| **Ghosting** | Faint repeated image | Mis-timed plate cylinder, slip | Re-set register, check bearings |
| **Trapping failure** | Ink of one color lifts prior ink | Insufficient drying, ink incompatibility | Adjust ink sequence, lower speed |
| **Blocking / set-off** | Ink transfers to back of web | Under-dried ink | Increase dryer temp, reduce film weight |
| **Banding** | Horizontal stripes | Doctor blade issue, vibration | Re-grind blade, balance cylinder |
| **Color shift** | ΔE > 2 vs standard | Pigment batch variation, fountain pH | Re-mix, check densitometer |
| **Poor rub resistance** | Ink rubs off | Insufficient cure, wrong ink-resin | Increase UV dose, switch resin system |
| **Migration** | Ink chemicals leach into food | Wrong ink grade used | Use **low-migration inks** (UV-cured, food-grade) |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Selecting the Right Printing Process

**Decision sequence** (commonly examined):

1. **What is the run length?**
   - < 5000 m → Digital (inkjet / toner)
   - 5000 – 50 000 m → Flexo (water/solvent)
   - > 50 000 m → Rotogravure

2. **What is the substrate?**
   - Corrugated → Flexo (post-print)
   - Films (PE/PP/PET) → Flexo or Rotogravure
   - Carton / paperboard → Offset + varnish
   - Metal can → Offset (dry) + overprint varnish

3. **What quality is required?**
   - Photo-realistic → Gravure or digital
   - Standard retail → Flexo
   - Variable data / serialization → Digital inkjet

4. **What is the food-contact risk?**
   - Direct contact → Use **low-migration, UV-curable inks only**
   - Indirect contact → Water-based flexo acceptable

5. **What is the budget?**
   - Plate cost: Offset > Flexo > Gravure (cylinders) > Digital (zero)
   - Per-unit cost: Gravure < Flexo < Offset < Digital

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Packaging Printing Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef unit fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    unwReel[Unwind Reel]:::unit
    webTension[Web Tension Control]:::ctrl
    coronaStn[Corona Treatment Station]:::unit
    printUnit1[Printing Unit 1 - K or CMY]:::unit
    printUnit2[Printing Unit 2 - C or M]:::unit
    printUnit3[Printing Unit 3 - Y or K]:::unit
    printUnit4[Printing Unit 4 - White or Varnish]:::unit
    dryer[Inert Drier or UV Lamp]:::unit
    visionSys[Inline Vision System]:::sensor
    laminator[Lamination Station]:::unit
    rewindReel[Rewind Reel]:::unit
    inkViscos[Ink Viscosity Controller]:::ctrl
    regisCtrl[Register Control]:::ctrl

    unwReel --> webTension --> coronaStn --> printUnit1 --> printUnit2 --> printUnit3 --> printUnit4 --> dryer --> visionSys --> laminator --> rewindReel
    inkViscos -.-> printUnit1
    inkViscos -.-> printUnit2
    inkViscos -.-> printUnit3
    inkViscos -.-> printUnit4
    regisCtrl -.-> webTension
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Flexographic Printing Sequence

    A[Step 1: Infeed Film from Unwind]:::step

    J --> K
