# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List the four major printing processes used in food packaging and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flexography** — Uses a flexible photopolymer plate; the most versatile and dominant process for films, corrugated boxes, and labels.
2. **Rotogravure** — Uses an engraved metal cylinder; gives the highest quality and is economical for very long runs (> 500 000 m).
3. **Offset Lithography** — Uses a planographic plate plus a rubber blanket; the preferred process for paperboard cartons, paper labels, and metal can decoration.
4. **Digital Printing (Inkjet / Electrophotography)** — Plate-less, data-driven process; ideal for variable data, short runs, and personalization (batch codes, traceability, anti-counterfeit).

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by "low-migration" ink? Why is it mandatory for food-contact applications?**

**Model Answer:**

A **low-migration ink** is one whose components (photo-initiators, monomers, plasticizers, solvents, colorants) have been **specifically formulated and tested** to ensure that the quantity of substances that can transfer into the food remains below the **specific migration limit (SML)** defined in EU Regulation 10/2011 and FDA 21 CFR § 175.300 — typically **≤ 10 µg/kg food** for non-listed substances.

It is mandatory in food-contact applications because ordinary UV-curable inks contain **low-molecular-weight photoinitiators** (e.g., benzophenone, 4-MBP) that can diffuse into the food through the packaging, posing **toxicological and organoleptic risks** (off-odour, off-taste, contamination).

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **flexographic printing process** used in flexible food packaging. Explain the role of the anilox roller, doctor blade, photopolymer plate, and impression cylinder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **flexography, rotogravure, and offset lithography** in terms of image carrier, ink type, substrate suitability, print speed, and typical run length. Mention one specific food-package example for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Definition:** Flexography is a *direct-relief* rotary printing process that uses a flexible photopolymer printing plate, raised image areas, and a metered inking system based on an engraved anilox roller.

**Functional steps:**

1. **Infeed** — The substrate (PE, PP, PET, aluminium foil, paper) is unwound under controlled web tension.
2. **Surface preparation** — A **corona-discharge treater** raises the surface energy to **≥ 38 dynes/cm**.
3. **Inking** — The **anilox roller** rotates in the ink pan; ink fills the engraved cells (80 – 1200 lpi, 1 – 12 BCM).
4. **Doctor blade** — A steel or polymer blade scrapes excess ink, leaving ink *only* in the cells. Modern designs use a *chambered doctor blade* for higher line screens.
5. **Plate cylinder** — A **photopolymer plate** (thickness 1.14 – 6.35 mm) is mounted on the plate cylinder. Raised image areas pick up ink from the anilox.
6. **Impression cylinder** — Presses the substrate against the plate, transferring the image.
7. **Drying** — Hot-air dryer (water/solvent) or **UV lamp** (UV-curable).
8. **Inline inspection** — Vision systems check register, color, and defects.
9. **Rewind / laminate** — The printed web is rewound or sent inline to a laminator.

**Role of each component:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Anilox roller | Meters precise, uniform ink volume to the plate |
| Doctor blade | Removes excess ink from the anilox surface |
| Photopolymer plate | Carries the raised image (relief) |
| Impression cylinder | Provides the reaction force for ink transfer |

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.2).**

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Parameter | **Flexography** | **Rotogravure** | **Offset Lithography** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Image carrier | Flexible photopolymer plate | Engraved chrome/copper cylinder | Planographic PS plate + blanket |
| Ink type | Water, solvent, UV | Solvent (low-bn), water, UV | Oxidizing paste, UV |
| Substrates | Films, corrugated, foils | Films, foils, labels | Paperboard, paper, metal sheet |
| Print speed | 200 – 600 m/min | 300 – 600 m/min | 100 – 300 m/min |
| Run length | 5 000 – 500 000 m | > 200 000 m (up to millions) | 1 000 – 100 000 sheets |
| Setup cost | Medium | Very high (cylinder) | Medium (plates) |
| Per-unit cost (high vol) | Low | Lowest | Medium |
| **Food-package example** | Bread / snack wrappers, milk pouches | Chocolate bar flow-wraps, biscuit laminates | Folding cartons for cereals & pizza boxes |

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **"printability"** of a packaging substrate. Discuss how **surface energy, roughness, porosity, and chemical nature** influence printability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **typical defects encountered in packaging printing** (at least six), their root causes, and the engineering remedies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Definition of printability:**
> Printability is the *combined property* of a substrate that determines the quality, fidelity, and durability of a printed image on it. It is a function of **surface energy, roughness, porosity, absorbency, chemical inertness, and dimensional stability**.

**Factors influencing printability:**

1. **Surface energy ($\gamma_s$)**
   - Must be ≥ ink's $\gamma_{ink}$ by 8 – 10 dynes/cm.
   - Low-energy films (PE, PP) require **corona** or **flame** treatment to raise $\gamma_s$ from 31 to **≥ 38 dynes/cm**.

2. **Roughness ($R_a$)**
   - Smoother surfaces (BOPP, PET) → sharper dot reproduction.
   - Rough surfaces (kraft, corrugated) → ink penetration, mottle, dot gain.
   - Typical printability: $R_a$ ≤ **0.8 µm** for high-quality process work.

3. **Porosity / Absorbency**
   - High porosity (newsprint, kraft) → excessive ink absorption → poor rub resistance.
   - Cobb test value ≤ **30 g/m²** is the standard for offset paper.

4. **Chemical nature**
   - Inert surfaces (glass, metal) require special adhesion promoters.
   - Plasticizer migration in flexible PVC can cause ink softening.

5. **Dimensional stability**
   - Hygroscopic papers expand/contract → register issues.
   - Films must be tension-controlled to avoid stretch-related distortion.

6. **Crystallinity & orientation**
   - BOPP has high surface crystallinity → low wettability → needs treatment.

#### Model Solution — (b)

| # | Defect | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pinholes** | Substrate contamination, low $\gamma_s$ | Re-corona, cleaner environment |
| 2 | **Mottle** | Worn anilox, ink too viscous | Replace anilox, adjust ink |
| 3 | **Hickeys** | Dust / dried ink skin | Cover ink pans, install filter |
| 4 | **Ghosting** | Plate cylinder eccentricity | Re-grind cylinder, re-set timing |
| 5 | **Blocking / set-off** | Under-cured ink | Increase UV dose, reduce laydown |
| 6 | **Banding** | Doctor-blade wear, vibration | Replace blade, balance rollers |
| 7 | **Color shift (ΔE > 2)** | Pigment batch variation, pH drift | Use spectrophotometer control |
| 8 | **Poor rub resistance** | Wrong resin, under-cure | Switch to higher-grade ink, raise UV dose |
| 9 | **Trapping failure** | Ink incompatibility, low tack | Re-sequence, use overprint varnish |
| 10 | **Migration** | Wrong ink grade | Switch to **low-migration ink** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — read carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "wettability" with "adhesion"** — wettability is *spreading*; adhesion is *sticking after drying*. Both are required.
> 2. **Do NOT state "corona treatment increases surface tension of the ink"** — corona raises the **substrate's surface energy**, not the ink's.
> 3. **Avoid one-word answers** in Part A. Even for 3-mark questions, mention a defining parameter or numerical value (e.g., *≥ 38 dynes/cm*, *BCM*, *ΔE ≤ 2*).
> 4. **Internal choice mismatch** — if you start answering Question B-A, you *cannot* take Question B-B. Choose based on which side you have prepared better.
> 5. **For Part B 14-mark questions, draw the diagram (5–6 marks)** — a missing diagram in flexography/gravure/offset guarantees a 5–7 mark loss.
> 6. **Food-safety angle is high-weightage** — any answer on packaging inks *must* mention **migration limits, low-migration inks, FDA/EU/FSSAI** even if not asked.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for "Printing" — KTU PEAGT863**

- ✅ **Printing** in food packaging = image transfer + food-contact safety + durability.
- ✅ Four major processes: **Flexo, Gravure, Offset, Digital**.
- ✅ **Surface energy rule:** $\gamma_s \geq \gamma_{ink} + 8$ to $10$ dynes/cm.
- ✅ **Corona treatment** is used to raise PE/PP film surface energy to **≥ 38 dynes/cm**.
- ✅ **Anilox roller** meters ink; volume expressed in **BCM** (billion cubic microns/in²).
- ✅ **Doctor blade** controls excess ink on anilox.
- ✅ **Photopolymer plate** is the relief image carrier in flexo.
- ✅ **Run-length rule of thumb:** < 5000 m → Digital; 5000 – 50 000 m → Flexo; > 50 000 m → Gravure.
- ✅ **UV inks** need dose $E_{cure} = P \cdot t$ exceeding threshold; give best rub resistance.
- ✅ **Water-based inks** dominate sustainability agenda; need hot-air drying.
- ✅ **Solvent-based inks** give best print quality but face **VOC regulation**.
- ✅ **Low-migration inks** mandatory for direct food contact; SML ≤ 10 µg/kg.
- ✅ **Color acceptance:** ΔE ≤ 2 in CIEL\*a\*b\* space.
- ✅ **Printability** = surface energy + roughness ($R_a$ ≤ 0.8 µm) + porosity + chemistry.
- ✅ **Cobb value** ≤ 30 g/m² for offset paper; indicates absorbency.
- ✅ **Drying adequacy** indicator: **Set-off %** < 15 % at the rewind.
- ✅ **Common defects**: pinholes, mottle, hickeys, ghosting, blocking, banding, color shift, poor rub, trapping failure, migration.
- ✅ **Regulations** to remember: FDA 21 CFR § 175.300; EU 1935/2004; EU 10/2011; FSSAI; IS 15495 (India).
- ✅ **Active / smart printing**: oxygen-scavenging inks, TTI inks, RFID antenna printing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labelling and Lamination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Labelling and Lamination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 LABELLING — Formal Definition

In the context of food packaging technology, **labelling** is defined as the application of any printed, written, or graphic matter that is present on the immediate container, the package itself, or any wrapper or tag attached to the package, which is intended to afford identification, description, grading, nutritional information, regulatory compliance, brand communication, and traceability of the food product enclosed within. According to the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, labelling is a mandatory regulatory requirement under the **Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020**, and is considered an inseparable extension of the packaging itself rather than a decorative accessory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Labelling is the communicative interface between the product, the consumer, the regulatory authority, and the supply chain. A label that fails any one of these four functions is, by KTU examiner standards, an *engineering failure* even if it looks visually appealing.

## 1.2 LAMINATION — Formal Definition

**Lamination** is the industrial process of bonding two or more plies (layers) of packaging substrates — which may be films, papers, aluminium foils, or metallised films — using an **adhesive** (chemical bonding), **molten polymer** (extrusion bonding), **heat and pressure** (thermal bonding), or **co-extrusion** (bondless lamination) to produce a single multi-layered composite web. The fundamental engineering goal is to combine the **complementary physical, chemical, mechanical, and barrier properties** of dissimilar materials so that the resulting laminate exhibits performance characteristics that no single layer can provide on its own (e.g., the moisture barrier of polyethylene combined with the oxygen and light barrier of aluminium foil).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lamination Exists:** A pure polyethylene (PE) film is a superb moisture barrier but a poor oxygen barrier and completely transparent to UV light. Conversely, a bare aluminium foil is a perfect gas and light barrier but is mechanically fragile, cannot be heat-sealed, and tears easily. Lamination unites these complementary properties into a single workable packaging web.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy for Labelling (The Passport Metaphor):**
Imagine a packaged food product as a *traveller* crossing international borders. Just as a passport contains the traveller's **name, photograph, nationality, visa stamps, and biometric chip**, a food label carries the product's **brand identity (name), visual appeal (photograph/artwork), legal origin (manufacturer address), regulatory approvals (FSSAI logo, ISI mark), and machine-readable intelligence (barcode/QR code)**. Without a passport, the traveller cannot enter the country; without a compliant label, the food product cannot legally enter the marketplace.

**Analogy for Lamination (The Layered Bulletproof Vest Metaphor):**
Think of a laminated packaging film as a *bulletproof vest*. A single layer of Kevlar can stop a small-calibre round but fails against heavier threats. By layering multiple materials — soft fabric against the body, hard ceramic plates at the centre, and a moisture-resistant outer cover — the vest achieves protection that no single layer could deliver. Similarly, a typical retort pouch laminate is a vest of **PET (outer print layer) / Aluminium foil (barrier core) / Nylon (puncture resistance) / CPP (heat-sealable inner layer)** — each layer performing a distinct defensive role.

## 1.4 Key Standards & Metrics (Always Bold These in Exams)

- **FSSAI** — Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (mandatory in India)
- **CODEX Alimentarius** — international food standards by FAO/WHO
- **FDA 21 CFR** — U.S. Food and Drug Administration labelling rules
- **EU Regulation 1169/2011** — European Union Food Information to Consumers (FIC)
- **ISO 2859** — sampling procedures for inspection by attributes
- **ASTM F88** — standard test method for seal strength of flexible barrier materials
- **ASTM F1921** — standard test method for hot tack strength
- **TAPPI T 494** — tensile breaking strength of paper and paperboard
- **GS1 Standards** — for barcodes (EAN-13, UPC-A, QR, DataMatrix)
- **Bond Strength** — typically reported in **N/15 mm** or **g/inch**
- **WVTR** — Water Vapour Transmission Rate in **g/m²·day** at specified T and RH
- **OTR** — Oxygen Transmission Rate in **cc/m²·day·atm**

## 1.5 Visual Roadmap of the Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Packaging Film] --> B[Lamination]
    B --> C[Multi-layer Web]
    D[Printing + Die-cutting] --> E[Labelling]
    E --> F[Labelled Package]
    C --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart above demonstrates that lamination occurs *upstream* in the packaging conversion process to create the substrate, whereas labelling occurs *downstream* to affix regulatory and brand communication onto the finished package.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LABELLING — Functional Components

A compliant food label is composed of the following *non-negotiable* elements, each governed by FSSAI/CODEX/FDA frameworks:

1. **Principal Display Panel (PDP):** Front face of the package containing the product name, brand, and net quantity. Must occupy at least **30 %** of the total surface area for cylindrical containers (FSSAI norm).
2. **Product Name / Common Name:** The legally recognised name of the food.
3. **List of Ingredients:** In descending order of weight composition (CODEX Standard 1-1985).
4. **Nutritional Information:** Per 100 g or per serving, including Energy (kcal/kJ), Protein, Carbohydrate, Fat, Sugar, and Sodium.
5. **Declaration of Veg / Non-Veg Symbol:** Mandatory in India — a **green circle** (veg) or **brown triangle** (non-veg) as per FSSAI.
6. **FSSAI Logo and Licence Number:** Mandatory for all licensed food business operators (FBOs).
7. **Name and Address of Manufacturer / Packer / Importer.**
8. **Net Weight / Volume / Count.**
9. **Date Marking:**
   - **Date of Manufacture (DOM)**
   - **Best Before Date (BBD)**
   - **Date of Expiry / Use By (DUE)** — for highly perishable items.
10. **Batch / Lot / Code Identification** for traceability.
11. **Maximum Retail Price (MRP)** including all taxes — mandatory in India.
12. **Consumer Care Details** — phone, email, website.
13. **Allergen Declaration** — as per FSSAI 2020 amendments.
14. **Country of Origin** — for imported products.
15. **Barcode / QR Code** — for supply chain traceability (GS1 standards).

## 2.2 LABELLING — Types and Substrate Materials

| **Type of Label** | **Substrate** | **Method of Application** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure-Sensitive (PS)** | Paper, BOPP, PE, Vinyl | Self-adhesive with tackified rubber/acrylic | Bottles, jars, FMCG pouches |
| **Shrink Sleeve** | PVC, PETG, OPS, PLA | Heat-shrinks (90–120 °C) over the container | Beverages, dairy, cosmetics |
| **Stretch Sleeve** | LDPE, EVA | Stretched elastically; no heat | PET bottles, beverages |
| **In-Mould Labelling (IML)** | PP, PE | Pre-placed inside mould before blow/injection moulding | Tubs, ice-cream containers |
| **Cut-and-Stack (Glue-Applied)** | Paper (wet-strength) | Pick-up glue on labeler | Cans, glass jars |
| **Reseal / Multi-Layer Peel Labels** | Laminated film composites | Pressure-sensitive with re-closable adhesive | Cheese, snack packs |
| **Booklet / Multi-Page Labels** | Paper + film laminate | Pressure-sensitive | Pharmaceuticals, premium foods |
| **RFID / Smart Labels** | PET antenna + IC chip | Adhesive-backed | Cold chain, anti-counterfeit |
| **Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI)** | Enzymatic / diffusion-based | Stickered | Chilled meat, dairy |
| **Tamper-Evident Labels** | VOID film, destructible vinyl | Pressure-sensitive | Pharma, premium oils |

## 2.3 LABELLING — Printing Technologies

- **Flexography (Flexo):** Most common for flexible packaging labels; uses relief plates, fast-drying inks, suitable for films and corrugated.
- **Rotogravure:** High-quality, long-run printing using engraved cylinders; used for premium films, wrappers.
- **Digital Printing:** Inkjet and electrophotographic; ideal for short runs, variable data (batch numbers, dates).
- **Offset Lithography:** Sheet-fed for paper labels; high resolution for cartons.
- **Screen Printing:** For special inks (metallic, fluorescent, Braille).
- **Letterpress:** Historical method, now niche.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The choice of printing technology is governed by the **substrate surface energy** (dyne level). BOPP typically requires a corona-treated surface of **≥ 38 dyne/cm** for proper ink wetting and adhesion.

## 2.4 LAMINATION — Process Classification (Deep Dive)

### 2.4.1 Adhesive (Chemical) Lamination
- **Dry Bond Lamination:** Adhesive is coated on one web, dried in an oven (solvent/water removal), then nipped against the second web under heat and pressure. Most widely used for **PET/PE, BOPP/PE, PET/Al/PE** combinations.
- **Wet Bond Lamination:** Adhesive is applied in liquid form and laminated while still wet. Used for **Paper/Aluminium foil/Paper** (cigarette packs) and **Paper/PE/Foil/PE** (Tetra Pak aseptic cartons).
- **Solventless Lamination:** Two-component polyurethane (PU) adhesive (NCO + OH) mixed and coated at room temperature. **Eco-friendly**, low energy, fast line speeds. Dominant in modern plants.
- **Hot-Melt Lamination:** Thermoplastic adhesive (EVA, SBC) melted and coated via slot die; no drying oven. Used for **book bindings, hygienic products, some snack packs**.

### 2.4.2 Extrusion Lamination
Molten polymer (LDPE, PP, EVA) is extruded through a flat die as a thin tie layer (**15–50 µm**) that simultaneously bonds two webs. Often combined with an adhesive primer (e.g., **ADMER**, maleic anhydride grafted) on polyolefin films to improve bond strength.

### 2.4.3 Thermal (Heat & Pressure) Lamination
Used in **label finishing** — paper or printed sheet is bonded to BOPP/PET film using heated nip rollers. Common for **book covers, menu cards, premium product labels**.

### 2.4.4 Co-extrusion (Bondless Lamination)
Multiple polymers are melted in separate extruders and combined inside a **co-extrusion die** (feed-block or multi-manifold) to form a multilayer film in a single process. No external adhesive. Used for **cast/Blown co-extruded films** (e.g., 5-layer PA/PE/EVOH/PE/PA barrier films).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Permeability and Bond Strength

| **Formula / Equation** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | Mass of water vapour transmitted per area per time | g/m²·day | Moisture barrier quantification |
| $OTR = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p}$ | Oxygen volume transmitted at STP per area per time per partial pressure gradient | cc/m²·day·atm | Oxygen barrier quantification |
| $\dfrac{1}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_i}{P_i}$ | Series resistance model for composite laminate barrier | Dimensionless | Predicting laminate barrier from individual layer permeabilities |
| $\sigma_{bond} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | Peel (bond) strength = peel force ÷ specimen width | N/15 mm or g/inch | Adhesive bond quality |
| $S_{total} = S_1 + S_2 + S_3$ (additive layers) | Tensile strength approximate sum for non-defect laminates | MPa | Estimating mechanical strength |
| $E_{laminate} \approx \sum E_i t_i / \sum t_i$ | Rule of mixtures for laminate modulus | MPa | Predicting stiffness |
| $W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3$ | Grammage of laminate = sum of layer grammages | g/m² | Estimating yield and cost |
| $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in extrusion die flow | Dimensionless | Co-extrusion die design |
| $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille flow in adhesive coating nip | m³/s | Adhesive flow analysis |
| $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Viscosity of adhesive / polymer melt | Pa·s | Process control in lamination |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the composite barrier equation, $P_i$ is the permeability of layer $i$ and $x_i$ is its thickness. Many students mistakenly invert the formula. Always remember: **barriers in series add resistances**, just like electrical resistors — the highest resistance layer *dominates* the total barrier.

## 2.6 Lamination — Engineering Significance

- **Barrier Optimisation:** Combining a 12 µm PET (OTR ≈ 90 cc/m²·day) with a 7 µm aluminium foil (OTR ≈ 0) yields a laminate OTR near zero, extending shelf life of oxygen-sensitive foods (meat, cheese, coffee) from days to months.
- **Mechanical Reinforcement:** A 25 µm BOPP adds stiffness to a 100 µm LDPE sealant web, enabling form-fill-seal (FFS) machine runnability.
- **Sealability:** Bare aluminium cannot be heat-sealed. Lamination with a 50 µm LDPE inner layer makes the laminate sealable on packaging machines.
- **Printability:** The outer PET receives reverse-printed graphics, while the inner aluminium is protected from direct food contact.
- **Sustainability Trade-off:** Multi-layer laminates are difficult to recycle due to inseparable layers — driving innovation in **mono-material PE/PE** or **PP/PP** structures and bio-based adhesives.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Composite Laminate Permeability (Series Resistance Model)

The series resistance model treats a multi-layer laminate as **resistors in series**, where the permeation of a gas or vapour must occur through each layer sequentially. This is the most fundamental equation in food packaging barrier analysis and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

### Step 1 — Define Permeation Rate Through a Single Layer

For a homogeneous film of thickness $x$ exposed to partial pressure $\Delta p$ across its faces, the steady-state permeation rate $Q$ (volume per unit time) follows **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** combined with **Henry's Law of Solubility**:

$$
Q = \dfrac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{x}
$$

where:
- $P$ = permeability of the material (cc·cm/cm²·s·cm Hg or equivalent)
- $A$ = area of permeation
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure gradient across the film
- $x$ = film thickness

### Step 2 — Express as Permeation Flux

Rearranging for the **permeation flux** $J$ (volume per area per time) yields the standard packaging equation:

$$
J = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{x}
$$

### Step 3 — Re-express Permeability as $P = D \cdot S$

Where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) and $S$ is the solubility coefficient (cc STP / cc · cm Hg). This decoupling is useful when analysing temperature dependence:

$$
P = D_0 S_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_p}{RT}\right)
$$

with $E_p$ being the activation energy of permeation.

### Step 4 — For a Multi-Layer Laminate in Series

In a laminate of $n$ layers, the steady-state flux $J$ through each layer must be **identical** (mass conservation). Let the individual thicknesses be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ and the partial pressures at interfaces be $p_0 > p_1 > p_2 > \cdots > p_n$. The flux through layer $i$ is:

$$
J_i = \dfrac{P_i \cdot (p_{i-1} - p_i)}{x_i}
$$

For steady state, $J_1 = J_2 = \cdots = J_n = J_{total}$.

### Step 5 — Sum the Pressure Drops

Summing all pressure drops across the laminate:

$$
p_0 - p_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_{i-1} - p_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{J \cdot x_i}{P_i}
$$

### Step 6 — Define Effective Permeability

The effective permeability of the laminate, $P_{lam}$, is defined by the same flux equation as a single film of total thickness $x_{lam} = \sum x_i$:

$$
J = \dfrac{P_{lam} \cdot (p_0 - p_n)}{x_{lam}}
$$

### Step 7 — Equate and Solve

Setting the expressions equal:

$$
\dfrac{P_{lam} \cdot (p_0 - p_n)}{\sum x_i} = \dfrac{p_0 - p_n}{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{P_i}}
$$

Cancelling $(p_0 - p_n)$ from both sides and inverting:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{x_{lam}}{P_{lam}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_i}{P_i} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{P_{lam}/x_{lam}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{P_i/x_i}}
$$

Or equivalently in terms of *permeance* $\Pi_i = P_i / x_i$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\Pi_{lam}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{\Pi_i}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The layer with the *lowest permeance* (i.e., highest barrier) dominates the total laminate barrier. For a PET/Al/PE laminate, the 7 µm aluminium foil has $\Pi_{Al} \approx 0.005$ cc/m²·day·atm, while the 12 µm PET has $\Pi_{PET} \approx 7.5$ cc/m²·day·atm. The aluminium layer is **1500× more resistive** than the PET — practically, the PET is irrelevant to OTR of the laminate, even though it carries the print.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Problem

**Problem:** A laminate consists of three layers:
- Layer 1: 12 µm PET, $P_1 = 9.0 \times 10^{-11}$ cc·cm/cm²·s·cm Hg
- Layer 2: 9 µm aluminium foil, $P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-15}$ cc·cm/cm²·s·cm Hg
- Layer 3: 50 µm LDPE, $P_3 = 2.5 \times 10^{-10}$ cc·cm/cm²·s·cm Hg

Calculate the effective permeability of the laminate and identify the rate-limiting layer.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $x_i / P_i$ for each layer:

$$
\dfrac{x_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-4}}{9.0 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.333 \times 10^{7} \;\; \text{s/cm Hg}
$$

$$
\dfrac{x_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-4}}{1.0 \times 10^{-15}} = 9.0 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{s/cm Hg}
$$

$$
\dfrac{x_3}{P_3} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \;\; \text{s/cm Hg}
$$

Step 2 — Sum the resistances:

$$
\sum \dfrac{x_i}{P_i} = 1.333 \times 10^{7} + 9.0 \times 10^{11} + 2.0 \times 10^{7} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{s/cm Hg}
$$

Step 3 — Compute total thickness:

$$
x_{lam} = 12 + 9 + 50 = 71 \; \mu m = 7.1 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{cm}
$$

Step 4 — Effective permeability:

$$
P_{lam} = \dfrac{x_{lam}}{\sum x_i/P_i} = \dfrac{7.1 \times 10^{-3}}{9.0 \times 10^{11}} \approx 7.89 \times 10^{-15} \;\; \text{cc·cm/cm²·s·cm Hg}
$$

Step 5 — Identify the rate-limiting layer by comparing each $x_i / P_i$ to the sum:

- PET contributes $\dfrac{1.333 \times 10^{7}}{9.0 \times 10^{11}} \times 100 \approx 0.00148 \;\%$
- Aluminium contributes $\dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{11}}{9.0 \times 10^{11}} \times 100 \approx 99.998 \;\%$
- LDPE contributes $\dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{7}}{9.0 \times 10^{11}} \times 100 \approx 0.00222 \;\%$

**Conclusion:** The **aluminium foil layer is overwhelmingly the rate-limiting layer** — it controls ~99.998 % of the laminate's oxygen resistance. The PET and PE contribute negligibly to the barrier but perform other critical functions (printability, sealability, mechanical strength).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Laminate Structures and Applications

| **Laminate Structure** | **Layers (Outer → Inner)** | **O₂ Barrier (cc/m²·day)** | **WVTR (g/m²·day)** | **Typical Food Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-ply (Basic)** | BOPP / PE | 100–150 | 4–6 | Snacks, biscuits |
| **Three-ply (Metallised)** | PET / Met-PET / PE | 0.5–1.0 | 0.4–0.8 | Coffee, dried soups |
| **Three-ply (Foil)** | PET / Al foil / PE | < 0.01 | < 0.05 | Aseptic milk, retort pouches |
| **Four-ply (High Barrier)** | PET / Al / Nylon / CPP | < 0.005 | < 0.05 | Ready-to-eat meals, retort |
| **Transparent High Barrier** | PET / EVOH / PE | 0.05–0.5 | 2–4 | Vacuum-packed meat, MAP |
| **Paper-based (Tetra Pak)** | PE / Paperboard / PE / Al / PE | < 0.01 | < 0.05 | UHT milk, juice cartons |
| **Mono-material (Recyclable)** | PE / PE / EVOH / PE / PE | 0.5–5.0 | 1–3 | Sustainable pouches, dry foods |
| **Cook-in / High Temp** | PET / Al / Nylon / CPP (Retort) | < 0.005 | < 0.05 | Curries, pet food |
| **Forming Web (Thermoform)** | PA / EVOH / PE | 1–5 | 3–6 | Vacuum skin packaging (VSP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Test Conditions:** O₂ values reported at 23 °C / 0 % RH (or 50 % RH for hydrophilic EVOH); WVTR at 38 °C / 90 % RH. KTU examiners expect students to state test conditions in any barrier calculation.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Bond Strength Analysis (Peel Test)

Bond strength between two laminated plies is measured by a **T-peel test** (ASTM F88) on a universal testing machine (UTM):

Step 1 — Specimen is cut to **15 mm width × 150 mm length**.

Step 2 — The two plies are manually separated at one end for **25 mm**.

Step 3 — The separated ends are clamped in the UTM grips at a **gauge length of 50 mm**.

Step 4 — The grips separate at a constant crosshead speed of **300 mm/min** at **23 °C / 50 % RH**.

Step 5 — The average peel force over the central **80 %** of the test length is recorded.

Step 6 — Bond strength is calculated as:

$$
\sigma_{bond} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w} \;\; \text{[N/15 mm]}
$$

Typical acceptable values:
- **General packaging:** ≥ 1.5 N/15 mm
- **Sterilizable pouches:** ≥ 2.5 N/15 mm
- **Boil-in-bag:** ≥ 3.5 N/15 mm
- **Retort (121 °C):** ≥ 4.5 N/15 mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Modes in Peel Test:**
> - **Adhesive Failure:** Peel occurs at the adhesive-substrate interface → indicates poor surface treatment or wrong adhesive.
> - **Cohesive Failure:** Peel occurs *within* the adhesive layer → indicates adhesive bond is stronger than the adhesive itself (acceptable).
> - **Substrate Failure (Tear):** Substrate tears before peel → indicates excellent bond (preferred for retort pouches).
> KTU examiners often ask students to identify the failure mode and infer its root cause.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the mandatory information that must be present on a pre-packaged food label as per FSSAI regulations.**

**Model Answer:**
The mandatory information required under the **FSSAI (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020** includes:
1. **Name of the food** — common or descriptive name.
2. **List of ingredients** — in descending order of weight.
3. **Nutritional information** — per 100 g or per serving (Energy, Protein, Carbohydrate, Fat, Sugar, Sodium).
4. **Declaration regarding Veg or Non-Veg** symbol (green circle / brown triangle).
5. **Name and complete address** of the manufacturer / packer / importer.
6. **Net quantity** — weight, volume, or number.
7. **Date of manufacture / packing** and **Best Before / Use By** date.
8. **Batch / Lot / Code number** for traceability.
9. **FSSAI logo and licence number** (14-digit).
10. **Maximum Retail Price (MRP)** inclusive of all taxes.
11. **Country of origin** for imported products.
12. **Instructions for use** and **storage conditions**, where applicable.
13. **Allergen declaration** for sensitive ingredients.
14. **Customer care details** — phone / email / website.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **Veg/Non-Veg symbol** and the **14-digit FSSAI licence number**, both of which are mandatory. Each omission may cost up to 0.5 marks.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between wet bond lamination and dry bond lamination. State two examples of products made by each.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Wet Bond Lamination** | **Dry Bond Lamination** |
|---|---|---|
| Adhesive state at lamination | Liquid (wet) | Solvent/water removed (dry) |
| Drying oven | Not required | Required |
| Line speed | Slower | Faster |
| Substrates suitable | Paper + Foil, Paper + Film | Film + Film, Film + Foil |
| Adhesion mechanism | Adhesive penetrates porous substrate | Adhesive film bonds by pressure and heat |
| Examples | Cigarette packs (Paper/Al/Paper), Tetra Pak cartons | PET/Al/PE retort pouches, BOPP/PE snack pouches |

| **Products** | **Wet Bond** | **Dry Bond** |
|---|---|---|
| Example 1 | Milk cartons (Tetra Pak) | Retort ready-to-eat pouches |
| Example 2 | Cigarette cartons | Stand-up pouches for coffee |
