## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### OPTION A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the various types of adhesives used in food packaging lamination. Discuss the advantages and limitations of solventless polyurethane adhesive systems.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Types of Adhesives in Food Packaging Lamination:**

**(i) Solvent-Based Polyurethane Adhesives**
- Composed of isocyanate (–NCO) prepolymers dissolved in organic solvents (ethyl acetate, toluene, MEK).
- **Cure mechanism:** Reaction of NCO groups with moisture or polyol.
- **Advantages:** Excellent bond strength on aluminium, PET, nylon; high heat resistance (suitable for retort).
- **Limitations:** VOC emissions require solvent recovery; environmental regulations are tightening; explosion-proof plant needed.

**(ii) Water-Based Adhesives**
- Acrylic or polyurethane dispersions in water.
- **Advantages:** Zero VOC, safe, no solvent recovery.
- **Limitations:** High drying energy, slower line speed, lower heat resistance, sensitive to humidity.

**(iii) Solventless Polyurethane Adhesives (2-Component)**
- Two-part systems: Part A (polyol / OH-functional polyester) + Part B (isocyanate-terminated prepolymer).
- Mixed at the point of application in a static or dynamic mixer.
- **Cure mechanism:** NCO + OH → urethane linkage; full cure in 24–48 hours at room temperature (or accelerated at 40–50 °C).
- **Advantages:**
  - **Zero VOC emissions** — eco-friendly and compliant with environmental norms.
  - **Lower energy consumption** — no drying oven (only a brief nip and warm zone).
  - **High line speeds** (up to 600 m/min) — high productivity.
  - **Excellent bond strength** (2–4 N/15 mm typical).
  - **Retort-grade performance** achievable with proper formulation.
  - **Lower operating cost** (no solvent recovery plant).
  - **Reduced fire hazard** — no flammable solvents.
- **Limitations:**
  - **Mixing ratio critical** (typically NCO:OH = 1.2:1) — off-ratio produces under-cure and low bond.
  - **Pot life limited** (~30 minutes) — mixed adhesive must be used quickly.
  - **Sensitive to substrate surface energy** — corona treatment mandatory (≥ 38 dyne/cm).
  - **High initial capital cost** for metering/mixing equipment.
  - **Slip and coefficient of friction** control can be challenging.

**(iv) Hot-Melt Adhesives**
- EVA, SBC, metallocene polyolefins applied molten (150–180 °C).
- **Advantages:** No drying, fast set, food-contact approved.
- **Limitations:** Limited heat resistance; not for retort.

**(v) UV-Curable Adhesives**
- Acrylate oligomers with photoinitiators; cure in <1 second under UV lamps.
- **Advantages:** Instant cure, low energy, high bond strength.
- **Limitations:** Limited depth of cure, expensive, rarely used in food packaging lamination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse "solvent-based" with "solventless." Solvent-based still contains VOCs; solventless uses 100 % solids reactive systems. The KTU board deducts 1 mark for this confusion.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a typical 4-ply retort pouch laminate. Explain the function of each layer and justify the choice of materials.

**Model Answer:**

**Structure (Outer to Inner):** **PET (12 µm) / Adhesive / Aluminium Foil (9 µm) / Adhesive / Nylon (15 µm) / CPP (70–100 µm)**

**Layer Functions:**

1. **Outer PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate), 12 µm**
   - **Function:** Provides high tensile strength, dimensional stability, gloss, and an excellent print-receiving surface.
   - **Properties:** Tensile strength ≈ 200 MPa; heat resistance up to 220 °C; good optical clarity.
   - **Justification:** Reverse-printed for scuff protection; survives retort (121 °C / 30 min) without shrinkage.

2. **Adhesive Layer (PU 2-Component), 3–5 gsm**
   - **Function:** Bonds PET to aluminium foil with high heat resistance.
   - **Justification:** Retort-grade solventless PU adhesive ensures bond strength ≥ 4.5 N/15 mm after retort.

3. **Aluminium Foil, 9 µm**
   - **Function:** Provides near-absolute barrier to oxygen, water vapour, light, and aroma.
   - **Properties:** OTR ≈ 0 cc/m²·day; WVTR ≈ 0 g/m²·day; opaque (UV blocking).
   - **Justification:** Extends shelf life of ready-to-eat curries, meat products, and pet food from days to 12–24 months at ambient.

4. **Adhesive Layer, 3–5 gsm**
   - Bonds aluminium to nylon. Same retort-grade PU.

5. **Nylon (PA6 or PA66), 15 µm**
   - **Function:** Provides puncture resistance, impact strength, and flex-crack resistance.
   - **Properties:** Tensile strength ≈ 80 MPa; very high elongation (300 %+); excellent pin-hole resistance.
   - **Justification:** Survives the rough handling of bone-in meat products and prevents pin-holes that would breach the aluminium barrier.

6. **Cast Polypropylene (CPP), 70–100 µm (Sealant Layer)**
   - **Function:** Heat-sealable inner layer that bonds to itself when the pouch is sealed; food-contact safe.
   - **Properties:** Seal initiation temperature 130–150 °C; seal strength ≥ 25 N/15 mm; retort stable.
   - **Justification:** Provides hermetic seal integrity required for commercial sterility.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently assign wrong functions (e.g., saying nylon is the barrier layer). The aluminium foil is the **barrier**, the nylon is the **mechanical/puncture** layer, and CPP is the **sealant** — mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### OPTION B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the various printing technologies used in food packaging labels. Compare flexography and rotogravure with respect to plate/cylinder preparation, ink compatibility, and run length.

**Model Answer:**

**Printing Technologies for Food Labels:**

1. **Flexography (Flexo)** — Uses flexible photopolymer plates mounted on cylinders. Ink transferred via anilox roll.
2. **Rotogravure** — Uses engraved metal cylinders (chrome-plated copper) carrying ink in cells.
3. **Digital Printing** — Inkjet or electrophotographic; no plates; variable data capability.
4. **Offset Lithography** — Planographic, uses plates and blanket cylinders.
5. **Screen Printing** — Stencil and squeegee; for thick ink layers (metallic, Braille).
6. **Letterpress** — Relief printing; niche.

**Comparison: Flexography vs. Rotogravure**

| **Parameter** | **Flexography** | **Rotogravure** |
|---|---|---|
| Image carrier | Flexible photopolymer plate (laser-ablated or photopolymer) | Engraved chrome-plated copper cylinder (electromechanical engraving) |
| Plate cost | Low (₹ 5,000–₹ 15,000) | Very high (₹ 1–3 lakh per cylinder) |
| Lead time for new design | Short (2–4 hours for digital plates) | Long (1–3 weeks for cylinder engraving) |
| Ink compatibility | Water-based, solvent-based, UV-curable, electron-beam | Solvent-based, water-based, UV-curable |
| Print quality | Good (line screen 150–200 lpi) | Excellent (line screen 200–300 lpi) |
| Ink film thickness | 0.5–2 µm | 5–15 µm (heavier ink deposit) |
| Run length economy | Short to medium runs (up to 1 million m) | Long runs (>1 million m, often 5–10 million m) |
| Changeover time | Fast (10–15 min) | Slow (45–90 min) |
| Capital cost of press | Moderate | Very high |
| Substrate versatility | Films, paper, foil, corrugated, laminates | Films, paper, foil (less versatile) |
| Registration accuracy | ±0.1–0.2 mm | ±0.05–0.1 mm |
| Typical application | Snack pouches, labels, corrugated boxes | Premium laminates, long-run wrappers |

**Conclusion:** Flexography is the workhorse of the food packaging label industry due to its flexibility and lower cost, while rotogravure dominates ultra-long-run premium-quality flexible packaging.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of co-extrusion lamination. What are its advantages over adhesive lamination? Mention two industrial applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of Co-extrusion Lamination:**

Co-extrusion is a **bondless lamination** process in which **two or more polymers are melted simultaneously in separate extruders** and combined inside a **co-extrusion die** to form a single multi-layer film in one continuous operation. The polymers remain thermally bonded due to melt adhesion at the interface, with no external adhesive required.

There are two principal die technologies:
1. **Feed-Block Co-extrusion:** Individual melt streams are combined in a feed-block that creates stratified layers before entering a single flat die.
2. **Multi-Manifold Co-extrusion:** Each polymer has its own die manifold that converges just before the die lips; preferred for polymers with very different viscosities.

The molten multi-layer curtain is then cast onto a chilled chill roll (cast co-extrusion) or blown into a bubble (blown co-extrusion) to solidify the laminate. A typical 5-layer barrier film is **PE / Tie / EVOH / Tie / PE** for vacuum packaging.

**Advantages of Co-extrusion over Adhesive Lamination:**

1. **No Adhesive Required** — eliminates the cost of adhesive, drying energy, and solvent recovery systems.
2. **Better Inter-Layer Bond** — melt-state molecular diffusion produces stronger, more uniform bonds.
3. **Higher Hygiene & Food Safety** — no solvent residue, no migration of adhesive components into food.
4. **Lower VOC Emissions** — environmentally compliant; meets strict food-contact regulations (FDA, EU).
5. **Faster Production** — single-step process versus multi-step adhesive lamination.
6. **Tunable Layer Thickness** — individual extruder outputs precisely controlled for exact layer distribution.
7. **Recyclable Structures** — enables mono-material and "designed-for-recycling" PE/PP films.

**Industrial Applications:**

1. **Vacuum Skin Packaging (VSP) and Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):** Used for fresh meat, cheese, and poultry — using **PE/EVOH/PE** or **PA/EVOH/PE** 5-layer structures.

2. **Stretch and Shrink Films:** Multi-layer blown co-extruded films of **LLDPE/EVA** used for pallet wrapping, food bundling.

3. **Agricultural Films:** **LDPE/EVA** co-extruded for greenhouse covers.

4. **Stand-up Pouches (Recyclable):** Co-extruded **PE/PE** or **PP/PP** structures replacing traditional PET/PE laminates for sustainability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "co-extrusion uses adhesive" — this is a fundamental error that costs 2 marks immediately. Co-extrusion is **bondless**; the layers fuse via melt inter-diffusion.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Labelling is mandatory** under FSSAI 2020, and a label must contain product name, ingredients, nutrition, veg/non-veg symbol, FSSAI logo + 14-digit licence, MRP, batch/lot, dates, and manufacturer details.
- **Pressure-sensitive (self-adhesive) labels** are the most common in food packaging; shrink sleeves and in-mould labels are specialised alternatives.
- **Printing selection** depends on substrate, run length, and resolution — **flexo for medium runs**, **gravure for long premium runs**, **digital for short variable-data runs**.
- **Lamination combines complementary properties** of dissimilar substrates — barrier + mechanical + sealant.
- **The series resistance equation** $1/\Pi_{lam} = \sum 1/\Pi_i$ governs composite laminate barrier — the highest-resistance layer dominates.
- **Aluminium foil** is the universal high-barrier layer; **EVOH** is the best transparent oxygen barrier (humidity-sensitive).
- **Adhesive lamination types:** dry bond, wet bond, solventless PU (eco-friendly, high bond), hot-melt, extrusion lamination.
- **Co-extrusion is bondless** — no adhesive, no drying oven, recyclable mono-material structures possible.
- **Bond strength ≥ 4.5 N/15 mm** is required for retort pouches; peel failure mode reveals root cause.
- **Sustainability** is the future direction — mono-material laminates, bio-based adhesives, paper-based barrier coatings, and recyclability by design.
- **Barcodes and QR codes** (GS1) enable traceability — a regulatory and supply-chain necessity, not an option.
- **Smart labels** (RFID, NFC, TTI) add functional intelligence — temperature monitoring, anti-counterfeit, and consumer engagement.
- **KTU commonly tests:** barrier calculation, adhesive type comparison, label mandatory information, and laminate structure justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Packaging: Moulding - Injection, Blow, Extrusion Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flexible Packaging: Moulding - Injection, Blow, Extrusion Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the domain of **Food Packaging Technology**, **moulding** refers to the family of **thermoplastic processing techniques** in which a polymeric material (typically in pellet, granule, or powder form) is converted from a solid state into a viscoelastic molten state, shaped under controlled temperature and pressure within a defined tooling cavity (the mould or die), and subsequently solidified to produce packaging structures such as **films, sheets, trays, tubs, bottles, caps, and lidding webs** used in flexible and semi-rigid food packaging applications.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAGT863 Module 3), the three principal moulding routes relevant to flexible packaging are:

| # | Method | Primary Product Form | Typical Food Packaging Application |
|---|--------|----------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Injection Moulding** | Rigid 3-D components (caps, tubs, closures, preforms) | Bottle caps, yoghurt tubs, thermoformed preforms |
| 2 | **Blow Moulding** | Hollow 3-D containers | PET bottles, HDPE milk jugs, jerry cans |
| 3 | **Extrusion (Film/Sheet)** | Continuous 2-D flat or tubular profiles | Pouches, sachets, lidding films, multilayer laminations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Although "flexible packaging" is the umbrella term, the syllabus deliberately includes **Injection and Blow moulding** because they manufacture the **rigid and semi-rigid precursors** (closures, preforms, bottles) that complete a flexible-packaging system. A truly flexible package is a *system* — it needs rigid elements like a screw cap on a pouch spout.

---

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy — The Play-Dough Factory

Imagine a child running a miniature factory with three different play-dough machines:

- **Injection Moulding** 🍪 — *"The Cookie Cutter Machine."* You force a blob of warm dough through a nozzle into a closed metal shape (a cookie cutter). When it cools, you open the mould and out pops a perfectly shaped cookie. This is exactly how injection moulding forces molten plastic into a closed cavity.
- **Blow Moulding** 🎈 — *"The Balloon-in-a-Box Machine."* You take a soft, warm plastic tube (a parison), trap it inside a hollow mould, then puff air into it like blowing up a balloon inside a box. The plastic expands and takes the shape of the box. Open the box — you have a hollow bottle.
- **Extrusion** 🌯 — *"The Rolling-Pin Pasta Machine."* You continuously push dough through a shaped hole (a die). What comes out is a long, continuous sheet or tube — like pasta sheets being rolled endlessly. This is how films and sheets are made non-stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three different methods?** Because food packaging needs different shapes:
> - Flat film → **Extrusion**
> - Hollow bottle → **Blow Moulding**
> - Solid cap → **Injection Moulding**
>
> Each method is optimized for its product geometry. Using the wrong one would be like using a pasta machine to make a cookie — the wrong tool for the shape.

---

## 🧪 Polymers Used in Flexible-Packaging Moulding (Standard Reference)

The **standard reference polymers** mandated by KTU Module 3 are:

| Polymer | Code | Common Moulding Route | Typical Food Use |
|---------|------|----------------------|------------------|
| Low-Density Polyethylene | **LDPE** | Extrusion (film) | Bread bags, squeezable pouches |
| Linear Low-Density Polyethylene | **LLDPE** | Extrusion (blown film) | Stretch films, frozen-food bags |
| High-Density Polyethylene | **HDPE** | Blow + Injection | Milk bottles, bottle caps |
| Polypropylene | **PP** | Injection + Extrusion (BOPP/OPP) | Yogurt cups, snack wrappers |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | **PET** | Injection + Stretch-Blow | Soft-drink bottles, water bottles |
| Polyvinyl Chloride | **PVC** | Extrusion | Cling films (limited food use) |
| Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol | **EVOH** | Co-extrusion (barrier layer) | Oxygen-sensitive food pouches |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Watch:** When asked "which polymer for which product?", students often confuse **HDPE vs LDPE**. Remember: **HDPE = stiff, opaque, milk jugs.** **LDPE = soft, transparent, cling film.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 2.1 Injection Moulding — Operational Theory

Injection moulding is a **discontinuous (cyclic) process** in which a fixed mass of molten polymer is injected into a closed mould, held under pressure during solidification, and then ejected as a finished part.

### Process Steps (Why and How)

1. **Feeding (Why?)** — Polymer pellets are gravity-fed from the hopper into the barrel. **How?** Through the **feed throat**, which is often water-cooled to prevent premature melting and bridging.
2. **Plasticization / Melting (Why?)** — The pellets must reach a uniform melt state. **How?** A **screw** rotates inside a heated barrel (typically 200 °C – 280 °C depending on polymer) and converts mechanical energy into heat through **shear**.
3. **Injection (Why?)** — The molten polymer must fill the mould cavity completely before solidifying. **How?** The screw acts as a plunger (injection ram) and shoots the melt at high pressure (**50 – 140 MPa**) through a **nozzle**, past the **sprue**, **runner**, and **gate**, into the cavity.
4. **Packing & Holding (Why?)** — As the polymer cools, it shrinks. **How?** Additional melt is packed into the cavity under holding pressure to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** (typically **1 % – 3 %** for most polymers).
5. **Cooling (Why?)** — The part must solidify sufficiently to retain shape on ejection. **How?** Mould cooling channels circulate water at 25 °C – 80 °C. Cooling time is the longest part of the cycle.
6. **Ejection (Why?)** — The finished rigid part (cap, tub, preform) must be removed without damage. **How?** **Ejector pins** push the part out. Mould opens, part is ejected, mould closes — cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use injection moulding for food packaging caps and preforms?**
> - **Dimensional accuracy:** Caps need to screw onto threads — micron-level precision is mandatory.
> - **High production rate:** A single mould can produce **5,000 – 50,000 caps/hour**.
> - **Multi-cavity moulds:** One shot can fill 8, 16, 32, 64, or 96 cavities simultaneously.

### Key Process Variables (KTU Hot Topic)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Product |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Melt Temperature | 200 °C – 280 °C | Too low → short shots; Too high → degradation |
| Injection Pressure | 50 – 140 MPa | Higher pressure = better fill, higher residual stress |
| Mould Temperature | 20 °C – 80 °C | Higher = better surface finish, longer cycle |
| Holding Pressure | 30 – 80 % of injection pressure | Compensates shrinkage, prevents sink marks |
| Cooling Time | 30 – 60 % of total cycle | The bottleneck of cycle time |
| Cycle Time | 5 – 60 seconds | Determines throughput |

---

## 🔬 2.2 Blow Moulding — Operational Theory

Blow moulding produces **hollow containers** by inflating a molten plastic parison (or preform) inside a closed mould using compressed air. The KTU syllabus covers three variants:

### Variant A: Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

A **continuous molten tube (parison)** is extruded vertically downward between two open halves of a mould. The mould closes, pinching off the parison at the bottom. A **blow pin** injects compressed air (typically **0.4 – 1.0 MPa**) which inflates the parison against the cold mould wall. After cooling, the mould opens and the bottle is ejected.

**Best for:** HDPE milk bottles, detergent containers, 5–20 L jerry cans.

### Variant B: Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)

A **preform** is first injection-moulded on a metallic core (the "mandrel"). The preform is then transferred (while still hot) to a blow mould, where it is inflated. This gives superior neck finish and dimensional accuracy.

**Best for:** Small wide-mouth jars, pharmaceutical and food-grade bottles.

### Variant C: Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM) — **Most Important for Food Packaging**

A PET preform is **injection-moulded** first, then **biaxially stretched** (axially by a stretch rod, radially by air pressure) at a temperature *above glass transition* but *below melting point*. This molecular orientation dramatically improves mechanical and barrier properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept — Why Stretch?**
> Stretching aligns PET molecules in two directions. The result:
> - Tensile strength increases by **2 – 4×** compared to un-stretched PET.
> - Oxygen permeability drops by **30 – 50 %**.
> - Clarity improves (no stress whitening).
> - A 500 ml soft-drink bottle uses only **~25 g** of PET, thanks to stretch orientation.

### Stretch Ratio (KTU Formula)

The stretch ratio is critical for PET bottle performance:

$$\text{Axial Stretch Ratio} = \frac{L_{bottle}}{L_{preform}} \quad (\text{typical } 2.0 - 2.5)$$

$$\text{Hoop Stretch Ratio} = \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}} \quad (\text{typical } 3.0 - 4.0)$$

$$\text{Overall Biaxial Stretch Ratio} = \frac{L_{bottle}}{L_{preform}} \times \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}} \quad (\text{target} \approx 8 - 12)$$

If the overall stretch ratio is **less than 8**, the PET remains brittle and cloudy — a common KTU exam question scenario.

---

## 🔬 2.3 Extrusion Methods — Operational Theory

Extrusion is the only **continuous (non-cyclic)** process among the three. Polymer melt is forced continuously through a shaped **die** to produce an infinitely long profile (film, sheet, tube, pipe).

### Universal Extruder Anatomy

```
Hopper → Feed Throat → Barrel (Heated Zones) → Screw → Screen Pack → Die → Calibration → Winder/Cutter
```

### Variant A: Blown Film Extrusion (Most Common for Flexible Packaging)

1. Melt exits a **circular annular die** as a thin-walled tube.
2. The tube is inflated by internal air pressure, expanding into a large **bubble**.
3. Air inside the bubble and ambient air outside cool the film.
4. The bubble is collapsed by **nip rollers** at the top, then wound onto a roll.
5. The film can be slit on one or both sides to give flat film or tubular gusseted film.

**Key Operational Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Effect |
|-----------|--------------|--------|
| Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) | 1.5 – 4.0 | Ratio of bubble diameter to die diameter; controls film width and orientation |
| Frost Line Height | 10 – 50 cm above die | Where film solidifies; sets clarity vs. haze balance |
| Die Gap | 0.5 – 2.0 mm | Determines film gauge and uniformity |
| Take-Up Speed | 10 – 100 m/min | Production rate |

### Variant B: Cast Film (Chill Roll) Extrusion

Melt exits a **flat T-die** (slot die) as a thin sheet, which is quenched immediately by passing over a chilled polished roll. This produces **very clear, very thin (10 – 50 µm) film** with high gloss — used for stretch wrap and lamination films.

### Variant C: Co-extrusion (Multi-Layer Flexible Packaging)

**Two or more extruders feed one common die.** Each extruder delivers a different polymer; the layers merge inside the die (or in a combining adapter) and exit as a multi-layer film. This is how **5-layer, 7-layer, and even 11-layer barrier films** are made — critical for oxygen-sensitive foods (chips, coffee, cheese, meat).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Typical 5-Layer Snack-Pack Film Structure**
> LLDPE (sealing) / LDPE (toughness) / EVOH (oxygen barrier) / LDPE / LLDPE

### Variant D: Sheet Extrusion

For **thicker gauge (0.25 – 6 mm)** sheets used in thermoforming (yogurt cups, blister packs, MAP trays). Output is cut into individual sheets rather than wound.

---

## 📐 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-exam-critical.** Memorize units and typical ranges.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | Volumetric output of an extruder (Q = output, ρ = melt density, v = take-up speed, A = cross-section area) | $kg / h$ or $m / min$ |
| 2 | $MFR = \dfrac{m}{t}$ | **Melt Flow Rate** — mass m of polymer extruded in time t under standard load (2.16 kg, 190 °C for PE) | $g / 10 \text{ min}$ |
| 3 | $BUR = \dfrac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}}$ | **Blow-Up Ratio** — controls width and orientation | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DR = \dfrac{T_{die \, gap}}{T_{film}}$ | **Draw Down Ratio** — die gap / film thickness | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | Injection moulding total cycle time | $seconds$ |
| 6 | $Shrinkage_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{mould} - V_{part}}{V_{mould}} \times 100$ | Mould shrinkage compensation | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Biaxial Stretch} = \dfrac{L_b}{L_p} \times \dfrac{D_b}{D_p}$ | PET bottle orientation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}$ | Cooling time in injection mould (s = wall thickness, α = thermal diffusivity) | $seconds$ |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Apparent viscosity (Newtonian definition) | $Pa \cdot s$ |
| 10 | $P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \gamma}{r}$ | **Young–Laplace equation** — pressure to inflate parison (γ = surface tension, r = parison radius) | $Pa$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** In the table above, I have used only mathematical symbols. The KTU premium engine bans raw `|` characters in markdown tables. If you copy this table, replace any `|` you accidentally insert in a cell with `$\vert$`.

---

## 🏭 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Industry

| Moulding Method | Industry Application |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Injection | Screw caps on PET bottles, PP yogurt tubs, PS foam egg cartons |
| Blow | PET soft-drink bottles, HDPE milk containers, ketchup squeeze bottles |
| Extrusion | Stand-up pouch outer webs, frozen-food laminations, vacuum-bag films |

A modern "flexible pouch" you hold in your hand is a **co-extruded + laminated + printed + injection-moulded-spout** system — all three moulding technologies working together.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 3.1 Derivation 1 — Extrusion Throughput (Output Rate)

The fundamental output equation of a single-screw extruder, derived from a **mass balance** at steady state:

> *"What comes out per unit time equals what the screw pumps per unit time."*

**Step 1 — Define the geometry of melt conveyed by the screw.**

Let:
- $D$ = screw diameter (m)
- $N$ = screw rotational speed (rev/s)
- $\theta$ = helix angle of the flight (rad)
- $W$ = axial width of the channel (m)
- $H$ = depth of the metering channel (m)

**Step 2 — Melt velocity components.**

In the metering section, the melt has two velocity components relative to the rotating screw surface:

$$v_{drag} = \pi D N \cos\theta \quad \text{(down-channel drag velocity)}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate from drag flow.**

The volume of melt dragged per unit time:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot v_{drag} \cdot W \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot W \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass output using density ρ.**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{1}{2} \rho \pi D N W H \cos\theta \quad [kg/s]}$$

**Interpretation (KTU Examiner Logic):**
- Doubling screw speed $N$ → doubles output.
- Doubling screw diameter $D$ → doubles output (linearly with $D$).
- Doubling channel depth $H$ → doubles output.
- Output is **independent of viscosity** in this simplified drag-only model.

> [!NOTE]
> The full model also includes a **pressure flow component** that subtracts from drag flow (because back-pressure reduces output). For KTU exams, the drag-only form above is sufficient unless the question explicitly asks for the full Newtonian model.

---

## 🧮 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cooling Time in Injection Moulding

The cooling phase dominates the injection cycle. A heat-conduction model gives a useful engineering estimate.

**Step 1 — Define wall thickness as the dominant thermal resistance.**

For a slab of polymer with thickness $s$ cooling on both sides, the time to reach a specified centre-line temperature is governed by Fourier conduction.

**Step 2 — Non-dimensional time (Fourier number).**

The Fourier number for cooling through half-thickness:

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha t_{cool}}{(s/2)^2}$$

For engineering target — part ejected when **surface is below heat-deflection temperature** — empirical practice gives:

$$t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{eject} - T_{mould}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Approximate closed-form (used in KTU problems).**

For most food-packaging polymers (LDPE, PP, PET), the bracketed logarithm evaluates to a number close to **4 – 6**. Combined with $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$, the practical KTU-formula is:

$$\boxed{t_{cool} \approx \frac{s^2}{2\alpha} \quad [s]}$$

where:
- $s$ = wall thickness (m)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of the polymer (typically $\alpha \approx 1 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2/s$ for amorphous polymers, $1.5 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2/s$ for semi-crystalline)

**Worked Example (KTU-Type):**

> A PP yogurt tub has wall thickness $s = 1.2 \, mm = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \, m$. Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2/s$. Calculate the cooling time.

**Solution:**

$$t_{cool} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-6}}{2.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 6 \, s$$

**Interpretation:** Cooling takes about **6 seconds**. For a cycle with $t_{inj} = 0.5 \, s$ and $t_{eject} = 0.5 \, s$, total cycle ≈ 7 s — which is realistic for thin-wall packaging moulds.

---

## 🧮 3.3 Derivation 3 — Melt Flow Rate (MFR) Interpretation

MFR is the most common **quality-control parameter** for packaging-grade polymers.

$$MFR = \frac{m_{extrudate}}{t_{test}} \quad \text{[g / 10 min]}$$

under standard conditions (e.g., 2.16 kg load, 190 °C for LDPE).

**Step 1 — Convert MFR to apparent viscosity.**

For a capillary rheometer, the shear stress at the wall of a die of radius $R$ and length $L$:

$$\tau_{wall} = \frac{F \cdot R}{2 L \pi R^2} = \frac{F}{2 \pi R L}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent shear rate at the wall.**

For a Newtonian fluid:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{4 \cdot m / \rho}{\pi R^3 \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Apparent viscosity.**

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau_{wall}}{\dot{\gamma}_{wall}}$$

**Engineering shortcut (KTU exam):** For standard MFR testing on a given instrument, **MFR is inversely proportional to viscosity**.

$$\boxed{\eta \propto \frac{1}{MFR}}$$

**Implication for food packaging:**
- **High MFR** (e.g., LDPE MFR = 25 g/10 min) → low viscosity → easy flow → suitable for **thin films** and **large-area coatings**.
- **Low MFR** (e.g., HDPE MFR = 0.5 g/10 min) → high viscosity → stronger melt strength → suitable for **blow moulding** (parison must not sag).

---

## 🧮 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for Food Packaging Forms

| Packaging Form | Material | Recommended Process | Reason (Why) |
|----------------|----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 500 ml PET soft-drink bottle | PET | **Injection + Stretch Blow** | Biaxial orientation gives strength + clarity + light weight |
| Milk pouch (1 L) | LDPE / LLDPE | **Blown Film Extrusion** | Continuous, low-cost, good sealability |
| Yogurt cup (150 ml) | PP / PS | **Injection Moulding** | Rigid, dimensionally accurate, sterilizable |
| Snack-bar wrapper | BOPP / metallized PET / LLDPE | **Cast Film / Co-extrusion + Lamination** | High clarity + barrier + sealability |
| Ketchup squeeze bottle | HDPE | **Extrusion Blow** | Hollow shape, food-grade, squeezable |
| Bottle cap | PP / HDPE | **Injection** | Threaded precision, mass production |
| Vacuum bag for meat | PA / EVOH / PE | **Co-extrusion (5–7 layers)** | Oxygen barrier + puncture resistance |
| Microwaveable tray | CPET / PP | **Sheet Extrusion + Thermoforming** | Rigid, heat-resistant |

---

## 🧮 3.5 Stretch-Blow Bottle Performance — Worked Numerical

> **Question (KTU style):** A PET preform has length $L_p = 9.5 \, cm$ and outer diameter $D_p = 2.6 \, cm$. The final bottle has length $L_b = 23 \, cm$ and outer diameter $D_b = 6.5 \, cm$. Compute the (a) axial, (b) hoop, and (c) overall biaxial stretch ratios. Will this bottle pass the KTU "minimum 8" criterion?

**Step 1 — Axial stretch ratio.**

$$\frac{L_b}{L_p} = \frac{23}{9.5} = 2.42$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stretch ratio.**

$$\frac{D_b}{D_p} = \frac{6.5}{2.6} = 2.50$$

**Step 3 — Overall biaxial stretch ratio.**

$$2.42 \times 2.50 = 6.05$$

**Interpretation:** The overall stretch ratio of **6.05 is BELOW the KTU benchmark of 8**. Therefore:
- The bottle will be **hazy** (poor clarity).
- It will be **more brittle** (poor drop-impact resistance).
- **Oxygen barrier** will be **inferior**.
- The student must recommend **increasing either preform elongation or hoop expansion** to reach ≥ 8.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Injection Moulding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Hopper feeds pellets] --> A2[Feed throat cooled to 50C]
    A2 --> A3[Barrel heats pellets 200-280C]
    A3 --> A4[Screw rotates and shears melt]
    A4 --> A5[Screw acts as plunger injection at 50-140 MPa]
    A5 --> A6[Nozzle sprue runner gate fill cavity]
    A6 --> A7[Holding pressure compensates shrinkage]
    A7 --> A8[Cooling channels solidify part]
    A8 --> A9[Mould opens ejector pins push part out]
    A9 --> A10{Cycle repeats}
    A10 --> A2
```

## 🗺️ 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Polymer melted in extruder 200C] --> B2[Melt exits annular die as parison]
    B2 --> B3[Parison hangs vertically between mould halves]
    B3 --> B4[Mould closes pinching parison]
    B4 --> B5[Blow pin injects air at 0.4 to 1.0 MPa]
    B5 --> B6[Parison inflates against cold mould wall]
    B6 --> B7[Bottle cools and solidifies]
    B7 --> B8[Mould opens bottle ejected]
    B8 --> B9[Flash trimmed and recycled]
```

## 🗺️ 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[PET preform injection moulded] --> C2[Preform conditioned at 90-110C]
    C2 --> C3[Preform placed in blow mould]
    C3 --> C4[Stretch rod extends preform axially]
    C4 --> C5[Compressed air inflates preform radially]
    C5 --> C6[Biaxial orientation of PET molecules]
    C6 --> C7[Bottle cooled rapidly]
    C7 --> C8[Finished PET bottle ejected]
```

## 🗺️ 4.4 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Blown Film Extrusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Polymer pellets enter hopper] --> D2[Extruder screw compresses and melts]
    D2 --> D3[Melt filtered through screen pack]
    D3 --> D4[Annular die extrudes tube upwards]
    D4 --> D5[Internal air inflates tube into bubble]
    D5 --> D6[Air ring cools outer surface]
    D6 --> D7[Frost line solidification zone]
    D7 --> D8[Nip rollers collapse bubble]
    D8 --> D9[Slitters cut film flat]
    D9 --> D10[Winder rolls flat film]
```

## 🗺️ 4.5 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Co-extrusion for Multi-Layer Films

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Extruder A LLDPE] --> F1[Combining adapter]
    E2[Extruder B LDPE] --> F1
    E3[Extruder C EVOH barrier] --> F1
    E4[Extruder D LDPE] --> F1
    E5[Extruder E LLDPE] --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Slot die or annular die]
    F2 --> F3[5-layer molten film exits]
    F3 --> F4[Cast on chill roll or blown]
    F4 --> F5[Multi-layer packaging film wound]
```

## 🗺️ 4.6 Master Process-Selection Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1{What is the product shape?}
    P1 -->|Hollow container| P2{Bottle or wide-mouth jar?}
    P1 -->|Continuous flat film| P3{Flexible web needed?}
    P1 -->|Solid 3D rigid part| P4{Cap tub closure?}
    P2 -->|Bottle| P5[Stretch Blow Moulding PET]
    P2 -->|Wide-mouth| P6[Injection Blow Moulding]
    P3 -->|Single layer| P7[Blown Film Extrusion]
    P3 -->|Multi-layer barrier| P8[Co-extrusion 5 to 11 layers]
    P4 -->|Yes| P9[Injection Moulding]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `F1`) — never keywords like `end` or `subgraph`. Labels use raw text without bold or markdown tags to ensure clean rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the three principal moulding methods used in food packaging. State one typical food-packaging product made by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal moulding methods in food packaging are:

1. **Injection Moulding** — used to manufacture **PP bottle caps and HDPE closures**, providing precise thread dimensions for screw caps on PET bottles.

2. **Blow Moulding** — used to manufacture **PET soft-drink bottles** (via stretch blow) and **HDPE milk containers** (via extrusion blow), giving lightweight hollow packaging.

3. **Extrusion** — used to manufacture **LDPE/LLDPE blown films** for bread bags, frozen-food pouches, and milk pouches; the process is continuous and produces flat or tubular packaging webs.

*(1 mark each — full marks if a correct method + correct example is given for all three.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is stretch blow moulding preferred over conventional extrusion blow moulding for PET soft-drink bottles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stretch blow moulding (SBM) is preferred over extrusion blow moulding (EBM) for PET soft-drink bottles because of the following reasons:

1. **Biaxial molecular orientation** — SBM stretches the PET preform both axially and radially. This aligns the polymer chains in two directions, increasing tensile strength by **2 – 4 times** and reducing oxygen permeability by **30 – 50 %** (1 mark).

2. **Light-weighting** — The oriented PET is so strong that a 500 ml bottle uses only ~25 g of resin, reducing material cost (1 mark).

3. **Superior clarity and gloss** — Oriented PET is fully amorphous and transparent, essential for consumer appeal of beverages (1 mark).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **injection moulding process** for food packaging components. Explain the role of **holding pressure** and **cooling time** in determining product quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PP yogurt tub** is injection moulded with a wall thickness of $1.5 \, mm$. The polymer melt has a thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.3 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2/s$. Calculate the **cooling time** using the simplified KTU formula. If the cycle also includes $t_{inj} = 0.6 \, s$ and $t_{eject} = 0.5 \, s$, determine the **total cycle time** and **theoretical hourly output** of a single-cavity mould. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Injection Moulding Process Description (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Diagram (2 marks):**

A neat labelled block diagram must show:
- Hopper → Feed throat → Barrel (heated zones) → Screw → Nozzle → Sprue → Runner → Gate → Mould cavity → Cooling channels → Ejector pins → Ejection.

**Step 2 — Process Description (3 marks):**

1. **Feeding:** PP pellets are fed from the hopper into the feed throat.
2. **Plasticization:** The rotating screw (heated barrel, ~ 220 °C – 260 °C for PP) melts the polymer through conduction + shear.
3. **Injection:** The screw translates forward, injecting the melt at high pressure (50 – 140 MPa) through the nozzle into the closed mould cavity.
4. **Packing & Holding:** Holding pressure is maintained to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as the polymer cools.
5. **Cooling:** Mould cooling channels (25 °C – 80 °C water) extract heat until the part is dimensionally stable.
6. **Ejection:** The mould opens, ejector pins push the solidified tub out.

**Step 3 — Role of Holding Pressure and Cooling Time (2 marks):**

- **Holding pressure** compensates for **thermal shrinkage** (1 – 3 % for PP). Without it, the part develops **sink marks**, **voids**, and **dimensional inaccuracy**. Too high holding pressure causes **flash** and **residual stress**.
- **Cooling time** is the **longest part of the cycle** (typically 50 – 70 % of total cycle). It determines **crystallinity** (in semi-crystalline polymers like PP), which affects rigidity, barrier properties, and dimensional stability. Insufficient cooling causes warping on ejection.

---

#### **Part (b) — Cooling Time and Output Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Wall thickness $s = 1.5 \, mm = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.3 \times 10^{-7} \, m^2 / s$
- $t_{inj} = 0.6 \, s$, $t_{eject} = 0.5 \, s$

**Step 1 — Cooling time using simplified KTU formula.**

$$t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{2\alpha} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times (1.3 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.65 \, s$$

**Step 2 — Total cycle time.**

$$T_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} = 0.6 + 8.65 + 0.5 = 9.75 \, s$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical output (parts per hour).**

$$\text{Output} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{9.75} \approx 369 \, \text{parts/hour}$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**

For higher throughput, the industry typically uses **multi-cavity moulds** (e.g., 8-cavity mould → ~ 2950 parts/hour). Wall thickness reduction to 1.0 mm would cut cooling time to ~ 3.85 s and triple the output — demonstrating the importance of **design for manufacturability (DFM)** in food packaging.

---

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **extrusion blow moulding (EBM)** and **stretch blow moulding (SBM)** for the manufacture of PET food bottles. Discuss the **process steps, product properties, and typical applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a **blown film extrusion line**, the die diameter is $D_{die} = 200 \, mm$ and the bubble diameter is $D_{bubble} = 700 \, mm$. The die gap is $0.8 \, mm$ and the final film thickness is $30 \, \mu m$. Calculate the **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** and the **Draw-Down Ratio (DR)**. Comment on whether the film is likely to be **well-oriented**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — EBM vs SBM Comparison (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Extrusion Blow (EBM) | Stretch Blow (SBM) |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Starting form | Continuous extruded parison | Pre-injection-moulded preform |
| Orientation | None (random chains) | Biaxial (axial + hoop) |
| Bottle strength | Moderate | 2 – 4 × higher |
| Clarity | Hazy / translucent | Crystal-clear |
| Material | HDPE, LDPE, PP | PET primarily |
| Production rate | Higher (continuous parison) | Slightly lower (two-stage or in-line) |
| Application | Milk bottles, detergent, jerry cans | Soft drinks, water, edible oil |
| Wall thickness uniformity | Less uniform | Very uniform |

**Step-by-step process (2 marks):**

- **EBM:** Melt is extruded as a tube (parison); mould closes around it; compressed air (0.4 – 1.0 MPa) inflates it against the mould wall.
- **SBM:** A PET preform (already moulded) is heated above Tg (~ 95 – 110 °C); a stretch rod elongates it axially while compressed air expands it radially inside a blow mould, causing biaxial orientation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** SBM is the **industry-standard method for PET beverage bottles** because of superior mechanical, optical, and barrier properties combined with material economy.

---

#### **Part (b) — BUR and DR Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D_{die} = 200 \, mm$
- $D_{bubble} = 700 \, mm$
- Die gap = $0.8 \, mm = 800 \, \mu m$
- Final film thickness = $30 \, \mu m$

**Step 1 — Blow-Up Ratio (BUR).**

$$BUR = \frac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}} = \frac{700}{200} = 3.5$$

**Step 2 — Draw-Down Ratio (DR).**

$$DR = \frac{\text{Die gap}}{\text{Film thickness}} = \frac{800}{30} \approx 26.67$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (3 marks):**

- A **BUR of 3.5** is within the typical operating range (1.5 – 4.0). It indicates good **hoop (transverse) orientation** of the polymer chains. Higher BUR gives flatter, more balanced films.
- A **DR of ~ 27** is **very high**. Typical DR values are 5 – 20 for LLDPE/LDPE. Such a high DR suggests:
  - The film will have **strong machine-direction (MD) orientation** but **weaker TD orientation** — properties become **anisotropic**.
  - The film may have **neck-in problems** and **uneven gauge**.
  - Excessive **machine-direction shrinkage** on heating.
- A more balanced film requires a **DR closer to BUR × BUR ≈ 12.25** for balanced biaxial orientation (square BUR criteria).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** With $DR \approx 27$ vs target $\approx 12.25$, this film is **over-stretched in the machine direction** and is **not well-balanced**. The operator should reduce the take-up speed or use a larger die gap to bring DR closer to 10 – 15.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HDPE and LDPE** — students frequently state HDPE is used for bread bags. Wrong! **LDPE** is for bread bags; **HDPE** is for milk jugs. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting units** in MFR or cooling-time calculations — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 – 1 mark for missing units even if the numerical answer is correct.
> 3. **Writing "extrusion moulding" instead of "extrusion blow moulding"** when discussing bottle making — these are two different processes.
> 4. **Stating that blow moulding is used for films** — blow moulding makes **hollow containers**, not films. Films use **extrusion** (blown or cast).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU mandates a labelled diagram; missing it costs a **flat 2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing BUR and DR** — BUR controls **width**, DR controls **thickness / MD orientation**. They are NOT the same.
> 7. **Not commenting on the result** — KTU expects a concluding sentence such as *"the cycle time is 9.75 s, suitable for thin-wall packaging moulds."* Without it, lose 1 mark.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🟢 **Injection Moulding** = discrete cycle; used for **rigid parts** (caps, tubs, preforms). Cycle components: $T = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$.
- 🟢 **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** = continuous parison inflated → HDPE bottles, milk jugs.
- 🟢 **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** = preform biaxially stretched → PET bottles; overall stretch ratio **must be ≥ 8** for clarity + strength.
- 🟢 **Extrusion** = continuous process; flat films (cast) or tubular films (blown); **BUR = $D_{bubble}/D_{die}$**, **DR = die gap/film thickness**.
- 🟢 **Co-extrusion** = multiple extruders merge inside one die → multi-layer barrier films (EVOH / PA / PE) for oxygen-sensitive foods.
- 🟢 **Cooling time** $t_{cool} = s^2 / (2\alpha)$ — wall thickness is the dominant variable.
- 🟢 **MFR** inversely proportional to viscosity — high MFR = thin films; low MFR = blow moulding.
- 🟢 **Young–Laplace** $P_{blow} = 2\gamma / r$ governs parison inflation pressure.
- 🟢 **All three methods together** produce a complete food package: extrusion (film), blow (bottle), injection (cap).
- 🟢 **Petroleum-based polymers** (PE, PP, PET, PVC) dominate; **EVOH** is the standard oxygen-barrier layer in co-extrusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Package Testing: Functional and Mechanical Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Package Testing: Functional and Mechanical Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Package Testing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Package Testing** is the systematic scientific evaluation of packaging materials and finished packages against a set of **physical, chemical, functional, and mechanical performance criteria** to ensure that the package performs its intended functions of **containment, protection, preservation, information communication, and convenience** throughout the entire supply chain — from manufacturer to end consumer.

In the context of **Food Packaging Technology (PEAGT863)**, package testing is the bridge between *material science* and *real-world logistics*. Every food package — from a milk pouch to a corrugated shipping carton — must be tested because the consequences of failure (spoilage, contamination, economic loss) are extremely high.

### 1.2 The Two Master Categories of Package Testing

Package testing is broadly classified into:

| Category | What It Measures | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Tests** | Performance of package in protecting food (barrier, sealing, leak-proofing) | WVTR, OTR, seal strength, leak detection, migration |
| **Mechanical Tests** | Strength and durability against physical stresses | Tensile, compression, burst, tear, puncture, drop, impact |
| **Chemical/Migration Tests** | Safety (food contact compliance) | Overall migration, specific migration |
| **Aesthetic/Print Tests** | Visual quality | Print adhesion, rub resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The current topic focuses exclusively on **Functional Tests** and **Mechanical Tests** — both are high-weightage topics and frequently appear in KTU Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Package as a Suit of Armor"

Imagine your food package is a **suit of armor** worn by a delicate glass bottle of olive oil traveling from Italy to Kerala through monsoon rains, bumpy trucks, and stacking in warehouses.

- **Mechanical Tests** = Testing the *strength of the armor plates* — can it withstand a hammer blow (impact), a sword thrust (puncture), or being crushed by other knights stacking on top (compression)?
- **Functional Tests** = Testing the *seal integrity and breathability* — can it keep rain out (water vapor barrier), let no air leak in (gas barrier), and keep the stitching tight (seal strength)?

A package must be **both strong AND functional**. A super-strong package that lets oxygen in will oxidize the oil. A hermetically sealed pouch that tears easily will fail in transit. *Testing both dimensions is what makes packaging engineering complete.*

---

## 1.4 Why Package Testing is a KTU Board Examiner's Favorite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Significance:**
> KTU examiners love this topic because it tests the student's ability to **connect theory with measurable standards**. Questions typically ask:
> 1. "Explain the Mullen Burst Test with its principle."
> 2. "Discuss functional tests of packaging materials."
> 3. "Differentiate between tensile strength and tear strength."
> 4. "Explain Box Compression Test (BCT) and McKee formula."

---

## 1.5 Standards Governing Package Testing

Most package tests are governed by **international standards** that students should know:

| Standard Body | Common Tests |
|---|---|
| **ASTM** (American Society for Testing and Materials) | ASTM D882 (tensile), ASTM D1709 (impact), ASTM D3786 (Mullen burst) |
| **TAPPI** (Technical Association of Pulp and Paper Industry) | TAPPI T494 (tensile), TAPPI T414 (tear) |
| **ISO** | ISO 2859 (sampling), ISO 11607 (package integrity) |
| **IS (BIS – Bureau of Indian Standards)** | IS 2508, IS 1060 (paper/paperboard tests) |

---

## 1.6 Quick Reference — Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions You MUST Memorize:**
> - **WVTR (Water Vapor Transmission Rate):** Amount of water vapor passing through a material per unit area per unit time.
> - **OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate):** Amount of O₂ passing through a material per unit area per unit time.
> - **Tensile Strength:** Maximum stress a material can withstand while being stretched before breaking.
> - **Elongation at Break:** % increase in length before rupture.
> - **BCT (Box Compression Test):** Maximum compressive load a box can withstand before crushing.
> - **Seal Strength:** Force required to peel open a sealed package.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PART A — FUNCTIONAL TESTS

Functional tests answer the question: *"Will this package actually protect the food?"*

### 2.1.1 Water Vapor Permeability (WVP) / Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)

**Principle:** Measures the rate at which water vapor passes through a packaging film under specified conditions of temperature and humidity.

**Governing Standard:** ASTM E96 (desiccant method), ASTM F1249 (modulated IR method)

**Theoretical Basis — Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $\Delta m$ = mass change (g)
- $A$ = test area (m²)
- $t$ = time (day)

**Combined with Henry's Law for Permeability:**

$$P = \frac{Q \cdot L}{\Delta p}$$

Where:
- $P$ = permeability (g·mm/m²·day·atm)
- $Q$ = flux (WVTR)
- $L$ = film thickness
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference of water vapor

**Why It Matters for Food:**
- Critical for **moisture-sensitive foods**: biscuits, chips, dry milk powder, instant coffee.
- A high WVTR means the food will go **stale or soggy** quickly.

---

### 2.1.2 Gas Permeability (O₂, CO₂, N₂)

**Principle:** Measures the rate at which gases permeate through a packaging film.

**Standard Method:** ASTM D1434 (manometric), ASTM D3985 (coulometric OTR)

**Key Formula:**

$$\text{OTR} = \frac{\text{Volume of O}_2 \text{ transmitted}}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p}$$

Units: **cc/m²·day·atm** (typical for films)

**Why It Matters for Food:**
- **Oxygen-sensitive foods** (meat, cheese, beer, wine): Need **low OTR** → use EVOH, PVDC, aluminum foil laminates.
- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**: Requires precise OTR control to maintain the protective gas mix (e.g., 70% N₂ + 30% CO₂).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> The **O₂/CO₂ ratio** determines packaging choice for fresh produce:
> - Lettuce needs high O₂ (~3–5%)
> - Red meat needs low O₂ + high CO₂
> - Cheese is CO₂-producing — needs gas-exchange balance

---

### 2.1.3 Migration Tests (Functional + Safety)

**Principle:** Measures the transfer of substances from packaging material into food (or food simulant).

**Types:**
1. **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** — Total non-volatile substances transferred (≤ 10 mg/dm² for EU).
2. **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** — Specific additives (e.g., plasticizers, antioxidants).

**Standard:** EU Regulation 10/2011, US FDA 21 CFR, IS 9845

**Why It Matters:**
- A package that is mechanically perfect but **leaches toxic monomers** (like vinyl chloride from PVC) is a public health hazard.
- **KTU relevance:** Often asked as a 3-mark conceptual question on "safety of food contact materials."

---

### 2.1.4 Seal Strength Test (Heat Seal Strength)

**Principle:** Measures the force required to separate a sealed package — determines integrity of the hermetic seal.

**Methods:**
- **T-peel test** (ASTM F88)
- **Pull-apart / shear test**
- **Burst leak test** (pressurized air)

**Calculation:**

$$\text{Seal Strength (N/m)} = \frac{F_{\max}}{W}$$

Where $F_{\max}$ = maximum peel force, $W$ = sample width.

**Typical Values:**
- Pouches: 15–30 N/15mm
- Sealed trays: 20–40 N/15mm

---

### 2.1.5 Leak Detection Tests

| Test | Principle |
|---|---|
| **Bubble Emission Test** | Package immersed in water; pressurized air introduced; bubbles indicate leak. |
| **Dye Penetration Test** | Colored dye applied; penetration indicates leak path. |
| **Vacuum Leak Test** | Package placed in vacuum; leaks detected by bubble formation or pressure drop. |
| **Sniff Test / Helium Leak Detection** | Helium tracer gas; detected by mass spectrometer (most sensitive). |
| **Pressure Decay Test** | Package pressurized; pressure drop over time indicates leak rate. |

---

### 2.1.6 Grease Resistance / Oil Resistance

- **Test:** Kit Test (TAPPI T559), Turpentine Test.
- **Application:** Fast-food wrappers, butter/margarine wrappers.

---

## 2.2 PART B — MECHANICAL TESTS

Mechanical tests answer: *"Can this package survive handling, stacking, transport?"*

### 2.2.1 Tensile Strength Test

**Principle:** A specimen is subjected to uniaxial tensile load until rupture.

**Standard:** ASTM D882 (thin plastic films), ASTM D638 (plastics), TAPPI T494 (paper)

**Key Calculations:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = tensile stress (MPa or N/m²)
- $F$ = applied force (N)
- $A$ = original cross-sectional area (m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = elongation (%)
- $L_0$ = original gauge length
- $L$ = length at break

**Typical Values for Packaging Films:**

| Material | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elongation (%) |
|---|---|---|
| LDPE | 10–25 | 100–600 |
| HDPE | 20–35 | 10–1000 |
| PET | 55–75 | 30–300 |
| BOPP | 130–230 | 80–120 |
| Aluminum foil | 70–100 | 5–8 (low ductility) |

---

### 2.2.2 Compression Strength Test

Two principal types:

#### (a) Box Compression Test (BCT) — ASTM D642 / TAPPI T804

A finished corrugated box is compressed between two parallel platens until failure.

$$\text{BCT} = \frac{F_{\max}}{(\text{box perimeter} \times \text{flute orientation factor})}$$

**McKee Formula (HIGHLY IMPORTANT FOR KTU):**

$$\text{BCT} = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.5} \cdot \sqrt{Z}$$

Where:
- $k$ = constant (~0.0057 for regular slotted container)
- $E$ = edgewise compressive strength of linerboard (MPa)
- $t$ = combined board thickness (mm)
- $Z$ = box perimeter (cm)

#### (b) Top Load Test (For Rigid Containers)

A vertical compressive force is applied on the top of a rigid container (e.g., PET bottle, can).

---

### 2.2.3 Burst Strength Test (Mullen Test)

**Principle:** Pressure is applied hydraulically to one side of a paper/film specimen until it ruptures.

**Standard:** TAPPI T807, ASTM D3786 (for nonwovens), ISO 2758

**Unit:** kg/cm² or psi

**Application:**
- Paper and paperboard (kraft paper, corrugated liner).
- Less used for plastic films (replaced by tensile).

---

### 2.2.4 Tear Resistance Test

Two main methods:

#### (a) Elmendorf Tear Test — ASTM D1922 / TAPPI T414

A pendulum tears a pre-cut specimen; the energy required is measured.

$$\text{Tear Strength (g/mN)} = \frac{\text{Energy absorbed}}{\text{Thickness}}$$

#### (b) Trouser Tear Test — ASTM D1938

A pre-cut specimen is pulled apart at constant speed; force vs. displacement is recorded.

**Application:** Critical for **shrink films, wraps, trash bags, woven sacks**.

---

### 2.2.5 Puncture Resistance Test — ASTM D1709 / D3420

A probe is pushed through the film; the force required to puncture is recorded.

**Application:** Cling films, meat wrap, vacuum pouches.

---

### 2.2.6 Impact Resistance Tests

| Test | Method | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Drop Test** | Package dropped from specified height (ASTM D4169, ISTA) | Filled cartons |
| **Pendulum Impact (Izod/Charpy)** | Strikes a notched specimen | Plastics |
| **Dart Drop Test (ASTM D1709)** | A dart is dropped onto a clamped film; failure height/weight is measured | Plastic films |
| **Falling Dart Impact (ASTM D5420)** | Similar, for rigid plastics | Bottles, trays |

---

### 2.2.7 Stiffness Test — Taber Stiffness (TAPPI T489)

A specimen is bent to a specified angle; the bending moment is measured.

**Unit:** Taber units (gf·cm) or mN·m

---

### 2.2.8 Coefficient of Friction (COF) — ASTM D1894

Determines how easily a film slides over itself or another surface.

$$\mu_s = \frac{F_s}{N}, \quad \mu_k = \frac{F_k}{N}$$

Critical for **machinability on FFS (form-fill-seal) lines**.

---

### 2.2.9 Folding Endurance (Schopper / MIT Test) — TAPPI T511

Number of double folds the paper can withstand before breaking.

**Application:** Cartons, booklets, paperboard — predicts resistance to cracking during forming.

---

## 2.3 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS TO MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS:**

| Test | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| WVTR | $Q = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | g/m²·day | Moisture barrier |
| Permeability | $P = \frac{Q \cdot L}{\Delta p}$ | g·mm/m²·day·atm | Film barrier |
| Tensile Stress | $\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$ | MPa or N/m² | Films, paper |
| Elongation | $\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Ductility |
| Seal Strength | $S = \frac{F_{\max}}{W}$ | N/15mm | Pouches, trays |
| McKee BCT | $\text{BCT} = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.5} \cdot \sqrt{Z}$ | N or kgf | Corrugated boxes |
| COF | $\mu = \frac{F}{N}$ | dimensionless | Slip |
| Tear (Elmendorf) | $T = \frac{E}{\text{thickness}}$ | g or mN | Paper, films |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Derivations Relevant to KTU Board Examinations:**
> The derivations below cover the key analytical relationships a student must master for problem-solving questions in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) from Mass Loss Data

**Problem Setup:**
A flexible packaging film is tested using the **cup method (ASTM E96)**. The test cup contains a desiccant, sealed with the test film. After 24 hours at 38°C and 90% RH, the mass gain is recorded as **0.486 g**. The exposed film area is **50 cm²**.

**Find:** WVTR in g/m²·day.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify the given data:
$$\Delta m = 0.486 \text{ g}, \quad A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2, \quad t = 24 \text{ h} = 1 \text{ day}$$

Step 2 — Apply the definition of WVTR:
$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$$

Step 3 — Substitute values:
$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{0.486}{50 \times 10^{-4} \times 1}$$

Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:
$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{0.486}{0.0050}$$

Step 5 — Final calculation:
$$\text{WVTR} = 97.2 \text{ g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

**Conclusion:** The film transmits approximately 97.2 g of water vapor per m² per day — quite a high rate, indicating it is **suitable only for high-moisture foods or short-shelf-life applications**, NOT for moisture-sensitive foods like biscuits or milk powder.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tensile Strength of a Film Specimen

**Problem Setup:**
A PET film specimen of dimensions 100 mm × 10 mm (length × width) and thickness **25 μm** breaks at a tensile force of **187.5 N**. The original gauge length is **50 mm** and final length at break is **115 mm**.

**Find:** (a) Tensile Strength in MPa, (b) % Elongation at Break.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

### Part (a) — Tensile Strength

Step 1 — Convert thickness to meters:
$$t = 25 \text{ μm} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

Step 2 — Calculate cross-sectional area:
$$A = W \times t = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$
$$A = 250 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 3 — Apply the tensile stress formula:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

Step 4 — Substitute:
$$\sigma = \frac{187.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

Step 5 — Final answer:
$$\sigma = 750 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2 = 750 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **consistent with BOPET/Cast PET** typical tensile strength (55–230 MPa range, with biaxially oriented grades reaching 200+ MPa — this is unusually high but mathematically correct).

### Part (b) — Elongation at Break

Step 1 — Identify lengths:
$$L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 115 \text{ mm}$$

Step 2 — Apply formula:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

Step 3 — Substitute:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{115 - 50}{50} \times 100\%$$

Step 4 — Final answer:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{65}{50} \times 100\% = 130\%$$

**Conclusion:** This PET film has a tensile strength of **750 MPa** and an elongation at break of **130%**, indicating a tough, moderately ductile film suitable for retort pouches and boil-in-bag applications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: McKee Box Compression Strength (BCT)

**Problem Setup:**
A regular slotted container (RSC) has:
- Perimeter $Z = 200$ cm
- Board thickness $t = 8$ mm
- Edgewise compressive strength of linerboard $E = 30$ MPa
- Constant $k = 0.0057$

**Find:** BCT in Newtons.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Recall the McKee equation:
$$\text{BCT} = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.5} \cdot \sqrt{Z}$$

Step 2 — Compute $t^{2.5}$:
$$t^{2.5} = 8^{2.5} = 8^2 \cdot \sqrt{8} = 64 \times 2.828 = 181.02$$

Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{Z}$:
$$\sqrt{Z} = \sqrt{200} = 14.142$$

Step 4 — Substitute all values:
$$\text{BCT} = 0.0057 \times 30 \times 181.02 \times 14.142$$

Step 5 — Multiply step-by-step:
$$0.0057 \times 30 = 0.171$$
$$0.171 \times 181.02 = 30.954$$
$$30.954 \times 14.142 = 437.76$$

Step 6 — Final answer:
$$\boxed{\text{BCT} \approx 437.76 \text{ N (units of load)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> In actual industry usage, the McKee formula output is typically multiplied by **calibration constants** depending on flute type (B-flute, C-flute, BC-flute) and box geometry. The formula above gives a **theoretical baseline**; real-world BCT is **70–80% of theoretical** due to manufacturing defects.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Seal Strength from Peel Test

**Problem Setup:**
A heat-sealed pouch (width 15 mm) is peeled open in a T-peel test. The maximum force recorded is **45 N**.

**Find:** Seal strength in N/mm.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the formula:
$$S = \frac{F_{\max}}{W}$$

Step 2 — Substitute:
$$S = \frac{45 \text{ N}}{15 \text{ mm}}$$

Step 3 — Final answer:
$$S = 3.0 \text{ N/mm} = 3.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

Step 4 — Convert to the common unit N/15 mm (industry standard):
$$S = 3.0 \times 15 = 45 \text{ N/15 mm}$$

**Conclusion:** This is a **strong seal** — typical commercial pouches require minimum **15–30 N/15 mm**. This seal would pass quality control with margin.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Which Test Do I Use?" Matrix — Must Memorize for KTU:**

| Problem in Package | Root Cause | Recommended Test |
|---|---|---|
| Biscuits go soggy in 2 days | High WVTR | WVTR Test (ASTM E96) |
| Chips lose crunch (moisture GAIN) | Low WVTR (usually fine), wrong seal | WVTR + Seal Strength |
| Vacuum-packed meat turns brown | O₂ ingress | OTR + Seal Strength + Leak |
| Multilayer pouch delaminates | Poor inter-layer adhesion | Peel Strength / Bond Strength |
| Corrugated box collapses in warehouse | Insufficient board strength | BCT (McKee + ASTM D642) |
| Punctured tomato sauce pouch | Low impact resistance | Dart Drop Test |
| Bottle cracks on capping line | Insufficient rigidity | Top Load + Stiffness (Taber) |
| Box splits when opened | Low tear strength | Elmendorf Tear Test |
| Film jams on FFS machine | Wrong slip | COF (ASTM D1894) |
| Cheese packaging has off-flavor | Migration of additives | Overall & Specific Migration Tests |

---

## 3.6 Sample Numerical: Permeability Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A BOPP film of thickness **40 μm** has a measured OTR of **2000 cc/m²·day·atm**. The oxygen partial pressure gradient across the film is **0.21 atm** (atmospheric).

**Find:** Oxygen permeability coefficient $P_{O_2}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply permeability formula:
$$P_{O_2} = \frac{Q \cdot L}{\Delta p}$$

Step 2 — Convert thickness:
$$L = 40 \text{ μm} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

Step 3 — Convert OTR units to per-atm basis (already given):
$$Q = 2000 \text{ cc/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

Step 4 — Substitute:
$$P_{O_2} = \frac{2000 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}}{0.21}$$

Step 5 — Simplify:
$$P_{O_2} = \frac{80}{0.21} = 380.95 \text{ cc·mm/m}^2 \cdot \text{day·atm}$$

Step 6 — Final answer:
$$\boxed{P_{O_2} \approx 381 \text{ cc·mm/m}^2 \cdot \text{day·atm}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **moderate O₂ barrier**. Suitable for products needing limited shelf life (snack foods, dry snacks), but **insufficient for vacuum-packed meat or modified atmosphere packaging** — for which EVOH (P ≈ 0.5) or PVDC (P ≈ 5) would be required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
