# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern Followed:**
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme Regulations:
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (definition/principle type)
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer questions (choice between Q-A and Q-B; each Q has two sub-parts of 7 marks)

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | PEAGT863 | 3 Marks]**

**Q: Define WVTR. State its significance in food packaging.**

**Model Answer:**

> **WVTR (Water Vapor Transmission Rate):** It is defined as the quantity of water vapor that passes through a unit area of packaging material per unit time under specified conditions of temperature and humidity. It is expressed in **g/m²·day**.

$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$$

**Significance in Food Packaging:**
1. Determines the **moisture barrier** capability of a packaging film.
2. Critical for **moisture-sensitive foods** such as biscuits, chips, dry milk powder, instant coffee, and breakfast cereals — a high WVTR leads to sogginess and loss of crispness.
3. Used to select appropriate materials — e.g., **aluminum foil laminates** (WVTR ≈ 0) for long-shelf-life products; **LDPE** (WVTR ≈ 10–20) for short-shelf-life items.
4. Standard test methods include **ASTM E96** (cup method) and **ASTM F1249** (modulated IR).

**[3 marks — students often lose 1 mark for not mentioning units or a standard test method.]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | PEAGT863 | 3 Marks]**

**Q: Differentiate between Tensile Strength and Tear Strength.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tensile Strength | Tear Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum tensile stress a material can withstand before breaking | Force/energy required to propagate an existing tear |
| **Loading Mode** | Continuous stretching along an axis | Tearing along an existing cut |
| **Measured Quantity** | Force per unit cross-sectional area (MPa) | Force (g or N) or energy (J) |
| **Standard Test** | ASTM D882 / D638 / TAPPI T494 | ASTM D1922 (Elmendorf) / D1938 (Trouser) |
| **Indicator** | Overall strength/ductility | Resistance to crack propagation |
| **Application** | Films, sheets, laminates | Bags, wrappers, sack materials |

> The two are **complementary**: high tensile strength with low tear strength (e.g., BOPP) means the film is strong but can be torn easily once a cut is initiated; the opposite (LDPE) is soft but tear-resistant.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to **draw the test setup diagram** (carries 2–3 marks).
> 2. Not mentioning the **ASTM/TAPPI/IS standard** for the test (carries 1 mark).
> 3. Writing **principle but skipping the formula** (formula = 2 marks).
> 4. Confusing **seal strength with burst strength** — these are different tests!
> 5. Not providing a **practical food packaging application** example at the end.

---

### Question A — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | PEAGT863 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) Explain the principle, procedure, and significance of the Mullen Burst Strength Test. Mention the governing standard.**  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Principle:
The Mullen Burst Strength Test measures the **maximum hydrostatic pressure** required to rupture a specimen of paper, paperboard, or corrugated board when the pressure is applied uniformly to one side of the clamped specimen through a circular rubber diaphragm.

#### Governing Standard:
**TAPPI T807** (paper/board), **ASTM D3786** (nonwovens/fabrics), **IS 1060** (Indian Standard for paper testing).

#### Procedure:
1. The test specimen is **clamped securely** between two annular rings (upper and lower) over a circular opening of standard diameter (typically 30.5 mm or 1.2 inch).
2. A **rubber diaphragm** is positioned below the specimen.
3. Hydraulic fluid (typically glycerin) is pumped beneath the diaphragm, applying **uniformly increasing pressure**.
4. The pressure at which the specimen **ruptures** is recorded as the burst strength.
5. The result is expressed in **kg/cm²** (or psi, kPa).

#### Test Setup (Block Diagram in text):
A circular specimen is clamped with its unsupported central area exposed to a pressurized rubber diaphragm. Pressure is increased steadily until rupture occurs. The peak pressure is the burst strength.

#### Significance:
- Predicts the **ability of paperboard/paper to resist puncture during printing, conversion, and stacking**.
- Critical for **corrugated shipping containers** and **folding cartons** carrying food products (e.g., cereal boxes, biscuit cartons).
- Low burst strength indicates poor fiber bonding, excessive recycling, or insufficient grammage.

**[7 marks — Students should draw the test setup diagram, mention TAPPI T807, and give a food application example.]**

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the Box Compression Test (BCT). State the McKee formula and discuss its utility in predicting stacking strength.**  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Principle of BCT:
The Box Compression Test measures the **maximum compressive load** a finished corrugated box can withstand when subjected to a uniform vertical load applied between two flat parallel platens, until the box **buckles or collapses**.

#### Governing Standard:
**ASTM D642, TAPPI T804, ISO 12048**

#### Block Diagram:
The box is placed between a **top platen** (which applies force) and a **bottom platen** (stationary). A load cell records force; displacement sensors record deformation. The output is a **Force vs Deformation curve**, whose peak is the BCT.

#### McKee Formula:
$$\boxed{\text{BCT} = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.5} \cdot \sqrt{Z}}$$

Where:
- $k$ = empirical constant (≈ 0.0057 for RSC — Regular Slotted Container)
- $E$ = edgewise compressive strength of the linerboard (MPa)
- $t$ = combined thickness of the corrugated board (mm)
- $Z$ = perimeter of the box (cm)

#### Utility in Predicting Stacking Strength:
1. **Load Forecasting:** Predicts the maximum number of boxes that can be stacked vertically in a warehouse without crushing — a critical determinant of warehouse logistics and supply-chain design.
2. **Material Optimization:** Allows the engineer to optimize board grade, flute type, and liner basis weight — saving cost while ensuring safety.
3. **Quality Control:** BCT is used as a KPI in corrugated box manufacturing.
4. **Safety Factor:** Recommended stacking load = BCT / 5 (using a safety factor of 5 for long-term stacking under humid conditions).
5. **Comparison with Empirical McKee:** Real-world BCT is typically **70–80% of McKee's theoretical value** due to manufacturing imperfections — this is accounted for in industry practice.

**[7 marks]**

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | PEAGT863 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a) Explain the various Functional Tests carried out on food packaging materials, with emphasis on barrier property tests (WVTR and OTR).**  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction to Functional Tests:
Functional tests evaluate the **performance of a package in protecting the food** against environmental factors such as moisture, oxygen, light, and grease. They assess whether the package can preserve food quality, safety, and shelf life.

#### 1. Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR) — ASTM E96:
**Principle:** Measures the rate of water vapor passing through a film under controlled T and RH.

$$\text{WVTR} = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t} \quad (\text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day})$$

**Methods:**
- **Desiccant Method** (cup method): Film sealed over a desiccant-filled cup, placed in humid chamber, mass gain measured.
- **Modulated IR Method** (ASTM F1249): More accurate, uses infrared detection.

**Significance:** Critical for moisture-sensitive foods (biscuits, dry milk, chips). High WVTR → short shelf life.

#### 2. Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) — ASTM D3985:
**Principle:** Measures the rate of O₂ permeation.

$$\text{OTR} = \frac{V}{A \cdot t \cdot \Delta p} \quad (\text{cc/m}^2 \cdot \text{day·atm})$$

**Methods:**
- **Coulometric Method** (most common): O₂ is reduced at a cathode, current measured.
- **Manometric Method** (ASTM D1434): Pressure change measured.

**Significance:** Critical for oxygen-sensitive foods (meat, cheese, beer, wine). Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) relies on precise OTR control.

#### 3. Other Functional Tests:
- **Seal Strength Test** (ASTM F88): Force required to peel open the seal.
- **Leak Detection Tests:** Bubble emission, dye penetration, vacuum decay, helium leak detection.
- **Grease Resistance Test** (TAPPI T559 Kit Test): For butter/oily food wrappers.
- **Migration Tests:** Overall and specific migration limits for food safety.

**[7 marks]**

---

**(b) Describe the Tensile Strength Test in detail. Discuss its significance and the typical stress-strain behavior of common packaging films (LDPE, HDPE, PET, BOPP).**  **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Principle:
A specimen of standard dimensions is subjected to a **uniaxial tensile force** at a constant rate of extension in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) until it ruptures. The force and elongation are continuously recorded.

#### Governing Standards:
- **ASTM D882** — Thin plastic films
- **ASTM D638** — Plastics (general)
- **TAPPI T494** — Paper and paperboard

#### Procedure:
1. Specimen is cut to standard dimensions (typically 100–150 mm long, 10–25 mm wide, with controlled thickness).
2. Mounted in the **upper and lower grips** of the UTM with a defined gauge length (e.g., 50 mm).
3. The crosshead is moved at a constant speed (e.g., 50 mm/min for films).
4. The **load cell** measures force; the **extensometer** measures elongation.
5. A **stress-strain curve** is plotted automatically.
6. From this curve, **Tensile Strength (σ)**, **Elongation at Break (ε)**, and **Young's Modulus (E)** are calculated.

#### Key Calculations:
$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \quad ; \quad \varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\% \quad ; \quad E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \text{ (in linear elastic region)}$$

#### Typical Values for Common Packaging Films:

| Material | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Elongation (%) | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LDPE** | 10–25 | 100–600 | Soft, very ductile |
| **HDPE** | 20–35 | 10–1000 | Strong, ductile |
| **PP** | 30–40 | 100–600 | Moderate |
| **PET (Biaxially Oriented)** | 130–230 | 80–120 | Stiff, strong, low ductility |
| **BOPP** | 130–230 | 80–120 | Stiff, transparent, biaxially oriented |
| **Aluminum Foil** | 70–100 | 5–8 | Stiff, brittle |
| **Paper (Kraft)** | 30–100 (depends on MD/CD) | 2–6 | Stiff, anisotropic |

#### Significance:
1. Determines the **mechanical strength** of packaging materials.
2. Indicates the **directionality** of strength (machine direction vs. cross direction) — important for films.
3. Predicts **handling performance** on converting and packaging lines.
4. Used to compare materials for **specific food applications** (e.g., PET for boil-in-bag, LDPE for milk pouches).
5. Required for **regulatory compliance** and **specification sheets**.

**[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION POINTS — Read 5 times before KTU exam:**

- ✅ **WVTR** is measured in **g/m²·day**; high WVTR = bad for moisture-sensitive foods.
- ✅ **OTR** is measured in **cc/m²·day·atm**; low OTR required for oxygen-sensitive foods.
- ✅ **Permeability $P = (Q \cdot L) / \Delta p$** — memorize this.
- ✅ **Tensile Strength = F/A in MPa**; **Elongation % = (L-L₀)/L₀ × 100**.
- ✅ **Mullen Burst Strength Test** uses hydraulic pressure; standard is **TAPPI T807**.
- ✅ **BCT (Box Compression Test)** — McKee formula: **$\text{BCT} = k \cdot E \cdot t^{2.5} \cdot \sqrt{Z}$**.
- ✅ **Elmendorf Tear Test** uses a pendulum (ASTM D1922 / TAPPI T414).
- ✅ **Dart Drop Test** (ASTM D1709) measures impact resistance of films.
- ✅ **Seal Strength = F_max / W** in N/15 mm; standard test is **T-peel (ASTM F88)**.
- ✅ **COF** (Coefficient of Friction) — ASTM D1894 — important for machinability.
- ✅ **Taber Stiffness** — TAPPI T489 — bending moment of paper/board.
- ✅ **MIT Folding Endurance** — TAPPI T511 — number of double folds.
- ✅ **Migration tests:** OML ≤ 10 mg/dm² (EU); SML varies by additive.
- ✅ **Always mention the standard (ASTM/TAPPI/ISO/IS)** in KTU answers — easy 1 mark.
- ✅ **Always draw the test setup diagram** — easy 2–3 marks per answer.
- ✅ **Differentiate clearly** between tensile, tear, burst, and puncture — examiners love comparison questions.
- ✅ **McKee formula** is a **KTU favourite** — practice one numerical every week.
- ✅ **Food application examples** (e.g., "LDPE for milk pouches," "PET for boil-in-bag," "Al-foil for cheese") impress examiners and earn full marks.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Final KTU Exam Strategy for This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. Memorize the **McKee formula** and practice **at least 2 numericals**.
> 2. Learn **ASTM standard numbers** for at least 5 major tests.
> 3. Always include a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers.
> 4. For 3-mark questions: definition + formula + unit + 1 food application = full marks.
> 5. Practice writing comparison answers (tensile vs tear, BCT vs Mullen, WVTR vs OTR) — these are KTU favourites.

**END OF NOTES — PEAGT863 | MODULE 3 | PACKAGE TESTING: FUNCTIONAL AND MECHANICAL TESTS**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packaging and Filling Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packaging and Filling Systems** refer to the integrated mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and electronic assemblies used in the food processing industry to dispense a precise, predetermined quantity of a food product into a primary container, hermetically close it (when required), and prepare it for secondary/tertiary packaging. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these systems are classified under **Primary Packaging Operations (Module 3)**, forming the bridge between bulk product processing and finished, distribution-ready goods.

Filling systems in food packaging are broadly categorized by:
- **Operating Principle** → Volumetric, Gravimetric (Weighing), Net Weight, Level-based, Count-based
- **Product State** → Liquid, Viscous, Paste, Powder, Granules, Pieces
- **Hygiene Requirement** → Aseptic, Hot-fill, Cold-fill, Ambient
- **Container Type** → Bottle, Can, Pouch/Sachet, Carton, Jar, Cup, Tube

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tea Stall and the Restaurant**
> Think of filling systems like ordering chai (tea) in two different places:
> - At a **roadside tea stall**, the vendor eyeballs the level of milk and tea in your cup — *inconsistent, fast, low cost*. This is analogous to a **Level/Overflow filling system** used for inexpensive water or non-critical liquids.
> - At a **premium restaurant**, the barista places your cup on a **digital scale** and pours exactly **150 mL** of a cold-brew coffee, then seals the lid. This is analogous to a **Gravimetric (Net Weight) Filling System** used for premium juices, oils, and dairy.
>
> Now imagine a **1-liter Bisleri water bottle** at a factory. A machine rotates it, blows sterile air, fills it to a precise meniscus, applies a tamper-evident cap, and shrinks a sleeve around it — all within **2–3 seconds per bottle**. That entire choreography is a **Filling Line**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Filling Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Mandated by KTU 2024 Module 3:**
> - **Fill Accuracy (Tolerance):** typically **±0.5% to ±1.5%** by volume
> - **Throughput:** measured in **containers/minute (cpm)** or **bottles/hour (bph)**
> - **Product Loss (Giveaway):** typically **< 0.5%** of total filled volume
> - **Clean-in-Place (CIP) Time:** **15–45 minutes** for dairy/juice lines
> - **Hygiene Class:** **EHEDG Class I / ASME BPE** for aseptic lines

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Filling Systems (Hierarchical Logic)

Filling systems are best understood by their **measurement principle**, because the choice of principle dictates accuracy, cost, and product compatibility.

**A. Volumetric Fillers (Most Common in Food Industry)**
1. **Piston Fillers** – For viscous liquids (ketchup, honey, shampoo). A piston of known bore displaces a fixed volume per stroke.
2. **Rotary Piston Fillers** – Multi-head version for high-speed bottling.
3. **Gear/Trochoid Pumps** – For thin to medium-viscosity products (juice, oil, milk).
4. **Peristaltic Fillers** – For sterile/aseptic dosing (yogurt starter cultures, pharma).
5. **Time-Pressure Fillers** – Product held in a pressurized tank; filling time × flow rate = volume. Used for carbonated soft drinks (CSD).
6. **Auger Fillers** – For powders (flour, spices, milk powder). An Archimedes screw rotates a fixed number of revolutions.

**B. Gravimetric (Weight) Fillers**
- **Net Weigh Fillers** – Product is weighed *before* it enters the container (headspace-controlled, used for snack foods, frozen vegetables, cheese).
- **Gross Weigh Fillers** – Container + product is weighed together. Container tare is subtracted.

**C. Level Fillers**
- **Overflow Fillers** – Used for clear bottles where the visual meniscus must be consistent (mineral water, certain spirits).

**D. Vacuum & Pressure Fillers**
- **Vacuum Fillers** – Container is evacuated, then product is drawn in by negative pressure (used for fruit juices, wines).
- **Counter-Pressure Fillers** – Used for **carbonated beverages**; container and product tank are pressurized to a common pressure to prevent CO₂ breakout.

**E. Aseptic Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) Systems**
- Rolls of sterile laminate (typically **PET/aluminum/PE** with a **white masterbatch** layer) are formed into a tube, sterilized via **H₂O₂ or UV**, filled with commercially sterile product, and sealed — all in a single enclosed cabinet.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "How" Behind the Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Engineering Trade-offs every Filler must balance:**
> 1. **Accuracy vs Speed** → Higher accuracy (e.g., gravimetric) usually means lower throughput.
> 2. **Product Compatibility vs Cost** → Piston fillers are cheap but cannot handle particles; peristaltic fillers handle shear-sensitive products but are slow.
> 3. **Hygiene vs Capital** → Aseptic lines cost ₹3–10 Crore but extend shelf life 6–12 months.
> 4. **Container Rigidity vs System** → Rigid containers (glass, PET) → rotary fillers; flexible pouches → form-fill-seal.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Limitation |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Fill Volume (Piston)** | $V_{fill} = A_{piston} \times L_{stroke}$ | $A$ in m², $L$ in m → $V$ in m³ | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ |
| 2 | **Fill Weight (Net Weigh)** | $W_{net} = W_{gross} - W_{container}$ | Newtons or grams | Tare accuracy is critical |
| 3 | **Fill Tolerance (% Error)** | $\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{actual} - V_{target}}{V_{target}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | Industry accepts $\leq \pm 1.5\%$ for liquids |
| 4 | **Product Giveaway (Loss)** | $L_{give} = (V_{filled} - V_{labeled}) \times N_{units}$ | Litres or m³ | Minimized by tighter fills |
| 5 | **Throughput (cpm)** | $cpm = \frac{60 \times \eta}{T_{cycle}}$ | $T_{cycle}$ = s/unit, $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.95) | Bottleneck is the slowest station |
| 6 | **Henry's Law (CO₂ in beverage)** | $C = k_{H} \cdot P_{CO_2}$ | $C$ in g/L, $P$ in atm, $k_H \approx 1.5$ g/L·atm at 25°C | Valid below 5 atm |
| 7 | **Counter-Pressure Fill Pressure** | $P_{counter} \geq P_{saturation}(T)$ | atm or kPa | Prevents CO₂ nucleation |
| 8 | **Aseptic FFS Sterilant Contact** | $\text{Log Reduction} = \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_{0}}{N_{f}}\right)$ | $N_0$ = initial CFU/mL | Target $\geq 5$-log for spores |
| 9 | **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Time-Pressure)** | $Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $\Delta P$ in Pa, $\mu$ in Pa·s | Laminar flow only (Re < 2100) |
| 10 | **Overall Line Efficiency (OLE)** | $OLE = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Theoretical Max Output}} \times 100$ | Percentage | World-class: $\geq 85\%$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Liquid Dairy:** Rotary piston fillers @ 600 cpm (e.g., Amul butter milk).
- **Snack Foods (Lays, Kurkure):** Multi-head net weigh fillers @ 90–120 weighings/min.
- **Mineral Water:** Overflow gravity fillers @ 24,000 bph.
- **Aseptic Milk (6-month shelf life):** Tetra Pak FFS lines @ 200 mL × 12,000 cartons/hr.
- **Spice Powders (MDH, Everest):** Twin-auger fillers @ 30–60 strokes/min.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Filling System for a Given Food Product

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **decision-support tool** that frequently appears as an application-level question in KTU exams. Memorize the mapping logic.

| Product State | Product Examples | Recommended Filler | Reason (Why) |
|---------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Free-flowing liquid (low viscosity) | Water, milk, juice | Overflow, Gravity, Gravity-vacuum | No shear damage; cheap |
| Viscous liquid (with/without pieces) | Ketchup with bits, jam | Piston + rotary valve | Tolerates particulates up to 6 mm |
| Carbonated liquid | Coke, sparkling water | Counter-pressure / Isobaric | Prevents CO₂ breakout and foaming |
| Aseptic / Sensitive liquid | UHT milk, probiotic drinks | Aseptic piston or peristaltic | Sterility + no shear damage |
| Free-flowing powder | Salt, sugar, rice | Linear/circumferential auger | Simple, low cost |
| Non-free-flowing powder | Cocoa, cake mix, spices | Twin-auger (volumetric) or net weigh | Handles cohesion and bridging |
| Sticky / oily paste | Peanut butter, ghee | Piston or heated piston | Heat reduces viscosity |
| Whole pieces (IQF) | Frozen peas, mango cubes | Combination weigh (multi-head) | Gentle, accurate weighing |
| Dry granules (irregular) | Trail mix, breakfast cereal | Net weigh + vibratory feeder | Tare variation compensated |

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Fill Accuracy & Giveaway

**Problem Statement:**
A ketchup brand markets its product in **500 g** labeled jars. The rotary piston filler delivers a nominal fill of **502.4 g** (slight overfill to ensure label compliance). If the plant produces **N = 50,000 jars/day**, **300 days/year**, calculate:
1. The **% Fill Tolerance** (accuracy error).
2. The **Annual Product Giveaway** in kg and in monetary terms, assuming tomato ketchup cost ₹120/kg raw.

> [!TIP]
> **Step 1 — Extract the given data:**
> - Target fill (label): $W_{label} = 500$ g
> - Actual mean fill: $W_{actual} = 502.4$ g
> - Daily production: $N = 50{,}000$ jars
> - Operating days: $D = 300$ days/year
> - Cost: $C = ₹120$/kg

> **Step 2 — Compute percentage fill tolerance (error):**
>
> $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{W_{actual} - W_{label}}{W_{label}} \times 100$$
>
> Substituting the values:
>
> $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{502.4 \text{ g} - 500 \text{ g}}{500 \text{ g}} \times 100$$
>
> $$\%\text{Error} = \frac{2.4}{500} \times 100$$
>
> $$\boxed{\%\text{Error} = 0.48\%}$$

> **Step 3 — Compute per-unit overfill (giveaway) in grams:**
>
> $$\Delta W = W_{actual} - W_{label} = 502.4 - 500 = 2.4 \text{ g/jar}$$
>
> Convert to kg: $\Delta W = 0.0024$ kg/jar

> **Step 4 — Compute daily giveaway:**
>
> $$G_{day} = \Delta W \times N = 0.0024 \text{ kg} \times 50{,}000$$
>
> $$G_{day} = 120 \text{ kg/day}$$

> **Step 5 — Compute annual giveaway:**
>
> $$G_{year} = G_{day} \times D = 120 \text{ kg/day} \times 300 \text{ days}$$
>
> $$G_{year} = 36{,}000 \text{ kg/year} = 36 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

> **Step 6 — Compute monetary loss (₹):**
>
> $$L = G_{year} \times C = 36{,}000 \text{ kg} \times ₹120/\text{kg}$$
>
> $$\boxed{L = ₹43{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year (₹43.2 Lakh)}}$$

> **Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**
> Although the **0.48%** error looks small, over a year it translates to **36 tonnes of free product** given to the consumer — about **₹43.2 lakh of raw material lost**. This is why KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasizes **tighter fillers (gravimetric) for high-volume products**.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem — Time-Pressure Filler Calibration

**Problem Statement:**
A time-pressure filler is calibrated for a 1 L PET bottle of orange juice. The filling valve has an effective orifice radius of **r = 6 mm**, the juice viscosity is **μ = 1.2 mPa·s**, the product line pressure above the juice is **ΔP = 50 kPa**, and the length of the flow path in the valve is **L = 40 mm**. Using **Hagen-Poiseuille**, find the volumetric flow rate Q. If the target fill is 1 L, what is the valve opening time required? (Take efficiency η = 1 for ideal nozzle.)

> **Step 1 — Write Hagen-Poiseuille equation for a circular pipe:**
>
> $$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot r^{4} \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$

> **Step 2 — Convert all values to SI units:**
> - $r = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
> - $\Delta P = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000$ Pa
> - $\mu = 1.2 \text{ mPa·s} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
> - $L = 40 \text{ mm} = 40 \times 10^{-3} = 0.04$ m
> - $V_{target} = 1 \text{ L} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³

> **Step 3 — Substitute and solve for Q:**
>
> $$Q = \frac{\pi \times (6 \times 10^{-3})^{4} \times 50{,}000}{8 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.04}$$
>
> Numerator: $\pi \times (1296 \times 10^{-12}) \times 50{,}000 = \pi \times 6.48 \times 10^{-5} \approx 2.036 \times 10^{-4}$
>
> Denominator: $8 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.04 = 3.84 \times 10^{-4}$
>
> $$Q = \frac{2.036 \times 10^{-4}}{3.84 \times 10^{-4}}$$
>
> $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.530 \text{ L/s}}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute valve opening time:**
>
> $$t = \frac{V_{target}}{Q} = \frac{1 \text{ L}}{0.530 \text{ L/s}}$$
>
> $$\boxed{t \approx 1.887 \text{ seconds}}$$

> **Step 5 — Verify laminar assumption:**
> For juice (ρ ≈ 1050 kg/m³), mean velocity $v = Q/A$ where $A = \pi r^2 = \pi(6\times10^{-3})^2 = 1.13 \times 10^{-4}$ m².
> So $v = 5.30\times10^{-4} / 1.13\times10^{-4} \approx 4.69$ m/s.
> Reynolds number $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 4.69 \times 0.012}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 49{,}200$.
>
> [!WARNING]
> **Re is far above 2100 — flow is turbulent!** Hagen-Poiseuille underestimates Q by 30–60% in real conditions. In practice, a **discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.65$** is multiplied: $Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{theory}$, giving $Q_{actual} \approx 0.345$ L/s and a corrected valve time of $t \approx 2.9$ s. This correction is frequently asked in KTU Part B questions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between Net Weigh Fillers and Gross Weigh Fillers. State one industry example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Net Weigh Filler | Gross Weigh Filler |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **What is weighed** | Product **only**, before entering container | Product + Container **together** |
| **Tare handling** | Not required; weighing is independent | Tare weight of container must be **subtracted** |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (independent of container weight variation) | Lower (errors in tare propagate) |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster |
| **Example** | Lays chips, Kurkure pouches | Bulk flour sacks, large cheese blocks |

*Mark split: 1 mark for net vs gross definition, 1 mark for differentiation, 1 mark for examples.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3]**
> **Q2. What is a "Counter-Pressure Filler" and why is it specifically used for carbonated soft drinks (CSD) instead of an overflow filler?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **counter-pressure (isobaric) filler** is one in which the product tank and the empty bottle are **both pressurized to a common pressure** (typically 2.5–4.0 atm for CSD) **before** the filling valve opens. The bottle is first pressurized with CO₂, then filled with product under the same pressure.

**Why not overflow?**
1. In an overflow filler, the product flows by **gravity** at near-atmospheric pressure. A sudden pressure drop causes **CO₂ to break out of solution** → excessive **foaming**, **loss of carbonation**, and **inaccurate fill volume**.
2. Counter-pressure filling keeps the system above the **CO₂ saturation pressure** (governed by **Henry's Law: $C = k_H \cdot P$**), preserving the dissolved CO₂ and the headspace carbonation level.
3. It also prevents **air ingress** into the bottle, which would degrade flavor and shelf life.

*Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Henry's Law application, 1 mark for foaming prevention.*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Rotary Piston Filler**. List the typical products handled and explain why piston fillers are unsuitable for carbonated beverages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **gravimetric (net weigh) filler** dispenses a snack food into pouches labeled **50 g**. The filler's standard deviation from 30 measurements is **σ = 0.6 g**. The maximum legal weight is **unlimited**, but the minimum legal weight under Indian Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules is **47.5 g** (i.e., T1 minimum = 90% of labeled, with no negative tolerance below 47.5 g). Determine:
1. The **mean fill** needed to ensure that **not more than 0.5%** of pouches fall below 47.5 g.
2. The **expected daily giveaway** if production = 40,000 pouches/day, raw material cost = ₹300/kg.
*(Use Z-score table: For 0.5% in lower tail, Z = 2.576)* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Construction (3 marks):** A rotary piston filler has a rotating carousel carrying **6–36 filling heads**. Each head has a **cylindrical piston inside a precisely machined cylinder**, a **product inlet valve (rotary slide valve)**, a **discharge valve**, and a **mechanical or servo-driven cam** controlling the piston stroke. The bottle indexes beneath the head; the piston retracts, drawing product into the cylinder; the slide valve rotates to the discharge position; the piston extends, expelling the product into the bottle. **The fill volume is the product of piston area and stroke length: $V = (\pi/4) D^2 \times L_{stroke}$.**
- **Working (2 marks):** A cam-follower mechanism converts continuous rotation of the carousel into reciprocating motion. The cycle has 4 phases: (i) Bottle indexing, (ii) Suction stroke (product drawn in), (iii) Rotary valve shifts to discharge, (iv) Discharge stroke. Throughput: 60–600 cpm depending on number of heads.
- **Products & Why not for CSD (2 marks):** Piston fillers handle **ketchup, honey, shampoo, mayonnaise, mustard, cooking oil, hand sanitizer, cosmetics**. **For CSD: (i) The piston design is not pressure-sealed — CO₂ would leak past seals, (ii) the rotary valve chambers create pressure drops, causing foaming, (iii) piston fillers are not sanitary to CIP standards required for beverages.**

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1 — Given data:**
> - $W_{label} = 50$ g, $\sigma = 0.6$ g, T1 minimum = 47.5 g, $P(underfill) = 0.5\% = 0.005$, Z = 2.576
> - $N = 40{,}000$ pouches/day, $C = ₹300$/kg

> **Step 2 — Mean required to satisfy the 0.5% underfill constraint:**
>
> We need $P(W < 47.5) \leq 0.005$. For a normal distribution:
>
> $$P\left(Z < \frac{47.5 - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 0.005$$
>
> For the lower tail probability of 0.005, $Z = -2.576$.
>
> $$\frac{47.5 - \mu}{0.6} = -2.576$$
>
> $$47.5 - \mu = -2.576 \times 0.6 = -1.5456$$
>
> $$\mu = 47.5 + 1.5456 = 49.0456 \text{ g}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\mu \approx 49.05 \text{ g}}$$

> **Step 3 — Giveaway per pouch:**
>
> $$\Delta W = \mu - W_{label} = 49.05 - 50.00 = -0.95 \text{ g}$$
>
> [!NOTE]
> A **negative** giveaway means the filler is actually **under-filling on average** to stay within the 0.5% underfill limit — a common statistical compromise. However, the **underfilled pouches** (the 0.5%) cost the company more in consumer complaints and Legal Metrology fines than any savings from reduced giveaway.

> **Step 4 — Annual monetary impact (assume 300 days):**
>
> Mean fill 49.05 g vs label 50 g → short-fill 0.95 g/pouch = 0.00095 kg/pouch
>
> Daily short-fill loss = $40{,}000 \times 0.00095 = 38$ kg/day
>
> Annual loss = $38 \times 300 = 11{,}400$ kg/year
>
> $$\boxed{L = 11{,}400 \times 300 = ₹34{,}20{,}000 \text{ per year (₹34.2 Lakh)}}$$
>
> *Plus Legal Metrology fine risk of ₹5,000–₹1,00,000 per offense per pouches below 47.5 g.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the principle, working, and applications of **Form-Fill-Seal (FFS) machines** used in the food industry. With a block diagram, show the sequence of operations in an **aseptic FFS line for UHT milk**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **time-pressure filler** is filling 500 mL of chilled orange juice (μ = 2.0 mPa·s, ρ = 1060 kg/m³) into PET bottles at a line pressure of **ΔP = 80 kPa** above the bottle interior. The valve nozzle has **D = 8 mm diameter, L = 30 mm length**, and a discharge coefficient **$C_d = 0.70$**.
1. Calculate the **steady-state volumetric flow rate**.
2. Calculate the **valve open time** to deliver 500 mL.
3. If the filler is to operate at **120 bottles/min**, what is the **theoretical maximum line efficiency** if valve open time is the bottleneck? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Principle (2 marks):** FFS machines integrate **container forming, product filling, and hermetic sealing** in one continuous, in-line operation. Two main types: **horizontal (HFFS)** for pouches/sachets, and **vertical (VFFS)** for pillow bags.
- **Working (2 marks):** A roll of heat-sealable film is fed over a **forming shoulder (VFFS) or through forming jaws (HFFS)**, shaped into a tube or pouch, the bottom is sealed, the product is metered in (by auger, piston, or weigh), and the top is sealed, with a cut between adjacent units.
- **Aseptic FFS for UHT milk (3 marks):** The laminate is a **PE/white PE/PET/Aluminum foil/PE** multilayer (e.g., Tetra Brik structure). Sequence: (i) **Laminate reel** feeds into a **forming tube** of sterile, pre-sanitized paperboard/plastic laminate, (ii) the inner surface is sterilized by **H₂O₂ (35%) bath + hot air drying** (achieving >5-log spore reduction), (iii) the tube enters a **positive-pressure sterile cabinet** (Class 100 / ISO 5), (iv) the **UHT milk** is dosed by a **piston or magnetic flow meter** through an aseptic valve, (v) **longitudinal induction seal** closes the back seam, (vi) **transverse heat-seal jaws** pinch the tube, (vii) a **guillotine cutter** separates the carton. The final shape is a **brick-shaped carton** with a **pre-applied straw** for consumer convenience. The whole system is sanitized by **CIP/SIP** before each batch.

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

> **Step 1 — Data in SI units:**
> - $D = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008$ m → $r = 0.004$ m
> - $L = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03$ m
> - $\Delta P = 80 \text{ kPa} = 80{,}000$ Pa
> - $\mu = 2.0 \text{ mPa·s} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
> - $C_d = 0.70$, $V_{target} = 0.5$ L = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
> - Throughput = 120 cpm → $T_{cycle} = 0.5$ s

> **Step 2 — Theoretical laminar flow (Hagen-Poiseuille):**
>
> $$Q_{theory} = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi (0.004)^4 \times 80{,}000}{8 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.03}$$
>
> Numerator: $\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-10} \times 80{,}000 = 6.433 \times 10^{-5}$
> Denominator: $4.8 \times 10^{-4}$
>
> $$Q_{theory} = 0.1340 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Step 3 — Apply discharge coefficient:**
>
> $$Q_{actual} = C_d \times Q_{theory} = 0.70 \times 0.1340 = 0.0938 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Step 4 — Valve open time:**
>
> $$t = \frac{V_{target}}{Q_{actual}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ L}}{0.0938 \text{ L/s}}$$
>
> $$\boxed{t \approx 5.33 \text{ seconds}}$$

> **Step 5 — Line efficiency check:**
> The cycle allows only **0.5 s per bottle**, but the valve needs **5.33 s**. The valve is the bottleneck.
>
> Theoretical cpm = $60 / 5.33 = 11.26$ cpm
>
> $$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{11.26}{120} \times 100 = 9.38\%$$
>
> [!WARNING]
> **This is unacceptable.** A practical solution is to use a **multi-orifice valve**, increase line pressure to ~400 kPa, or pre-pressurize the bottle. Real-world fillers targeting 120 cpm typically use **multiple filling heads (e.g., 12 heads on a rotary filler)** to parallelize the dosing.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Packaging and Filling Systems" in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing "Volumetric" with "Gravimetric"** — Volumetric = measures volume (mL); Gravimetric = measures mass (g). 2-mark question penalty.
> 2. **Forgetting to include the discharge coefficient** in Time-Pressure fillers. Real flow ≠ Hagen-Poiseuille flow.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion in mm → m** while calculating piston area. A 6 mm piston has area = $(\pi/4)(0.006)^2$, not $(\pi/4)(6)^2$. This single error causes a 12× wrong answer.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of laminar flow** in Hagen-Poiseuille derivations. Always check Reynolds number.
> 5. **Omitting the block diagram** in FFS questions — examiners allot 2 marks purely for the diagram. Always draw a clean labeled block diagram.
> 6. **Ignoring hygiene & CIP** — In the 2024 scheme, CO3 (engineering considerations) explicitly requires discussion of **sanitation, CIP/SIP, and aseptic protocols**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Packaging and Filling Systems" (Module 3)**
> - ☐ **5 Filler Families:** Volumetric, Gravimetric, Level/Overflow, Vacuum/Counter-pressure, Aseptic FFS.
> - ☐ **Piston Filler Equation:** $V = (\pi/4) D^2 \times L_{stroke}$ — must convert mm → m.
> - ☐ **Auger Filler Use Case:** Powders and granules. Twin-auger for cohesive powders.
> - ☐ **Counter-Pressure Filler:** Mandatory for **carbonated beverages**; preserves CO₂ via Henry's Law.
> - ☐ **Aseptic FFS:** Multilayer laminate (PE/Alu/PET) sterilized by H₂O₂; Class 100 cabinet; UHT products.
> - ☐ **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P / (8\mu L)$, **laminar only**; multiply by $C_d \approx 0.6$–0.7 in practice.
> - ☐ **% Fill Error Formula:** $(V_{actual} - V_{label})/V_{label} \times 100$. Industry tolerance: $\pm 0.5\%$–$\pm 1.5\%$.
> - ☐ **Net Weigh vs Gross Weigh:** Net weighs **product only** (more accurate); gross weighs product + container.
> - ☐ **Z-score for compliance:** For 0.5% underfill, Z = 2.576. Mean must be ≥ T1_min + 2.576σ.
> - ☐ **CIP/SIP:** Cleaning-In-Place / Sterilization-In-Place is **mandatory** for food-grade fillers.
> - ☐ **Overall Line Efficiency (OLE):** World-class OLE ≥ 85%; OLE = (Actual output) / (Theoretical max).
> - ☐ **Legal Metrology (India):** Packed commodities have **T1 = 90%** of label as soft minimum, **T2 = 85%** as hard minimum. Below T2 = punishable.
> - ☐ **Block diagram is compulsory** in any FFS / rotary filler question (2–3 marks reserved for it).
> - ☐ **Discharge Coefficient** always goes in real-world time-pressure calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economics of Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Economics of Packaging** is the systematic study of the cost components, financial planning, cost optimization strategies, and value analysis involved in the design, selection, production, and utilization of packaging materials and systems. It integrates principles of industrial economics, operations management, and materials engineering to achieve the **optimal balance between packaging cost, product protection, marketing utility, and environmental sustainability**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** Outcome-Based Education framework, the economics of packaging is studied under the umbrella of **Module 4: Packaging Economics and Waste Management**, where students are expected to evaluate the economic trade-offs in choosing packaging materials, automation levels, and supply-chain logistics for food products.

The key economic dimensions of packaging can be classified into:

1. **Direct (Visible) Costs** – Materials, labor, energy, machinery.
2. **Indirect (Hidden) Costs** – Spoilage, returns, brand damage, regulatory fines.
3. **Opportunity Costs** – Capital locked up in inventory, shelf-space allocation.
4. **External Costs** – Environmental burden, carbon tax, disposal costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course outcome for this topic (typically mapped to **CO4** of PEAGT863) requires students to *"Analyze the economic and environmental trade-offs in food packaging decisions and recommend cost-effective packaging strategies."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a gift box for a wedding present. You have two options:

- **Option A:** A simple brown cardboard box (₹20) — looks plain, may get crushed, doesn't impress.
- **Option B:** A decorated wooden box with satin lining (₹500) — beautiful, protective, but expensive.

Now, the **gift inside** is a ₹2000 watch. Which box would you pick?

If you pick Option B, you spent **25%** of the watch's value just on packaging. If you pick Option A, you saved money but risk damage and a poor presentation.

**Economics of Packaging** is exactly this — finding the **sweet spot** where:
- The box protects the watch (product protection function),
- The box looks attractive enough to add perceived value (marketing function),
- The box doesn't cost so much that profits vanish (cost control),
- The box can be recycled or disposed of cheaply (sustainability function).

$$ \text{Ideal Packaging Cost} = f(\text{Protection}, \text{Marketing}, \text{Sustainability}, \text{Affordability}) $$

In real food packaging, this trade-off is played out daily — between glass bottles and PET, between multilayer laminates and mono-material films, between manual and automated filling lines.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Packaging Economics

The following quantitative indicators are universally used in industry and frequently tested in KTU examinations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics for Packaging Economics:**
> - **Packaging-to-Product Cost Ratio (PPR):** Typically ranges from **1:10 to 1:50** depending on product category.
> - **Return on Packaging Investment (ROPI):** Measures revenue uplift per rupee spent on upgraded packaging.
> - **Shelf-Life Extension Value (SLEV):** Economic value of extra days a package adds to product shelf life.
> - **Damage/Return Rate (%):** Target for most FMCG companies is **< 0.5%**.
> - **Cost per 1000 packages (CPM equivalent):** Standard quoting unit in B2B packaging procurement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Components of a Packaging System

A food packaging line involves multiple cost centers. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **identify, classify, and compute** each of the following:

### 2.1.1 Fixed Costs (FC)
These are **independent of production volume** and remain constant over a fiscal year. Examples:
- Depreciation of packaging machinery (e.g., form-fill-seal machine).
- Rent of the packaging hall / cleanroom.
- Salaries of permanent packaging-line supervisors.
- Insurance and statutory compliance costs.

### 2.1.2 Variable Costs (VC)
These scale **linearly (or near-linearly)** with the number of packages produced:
- Raw material (film, board, glass, metal, ink).
- Direct labor (operators on the line).
- Consumables (adhesives, labels, shrink wrap).
- Energy (electricity for sealing, sterilization).
- Transport of finished goods.

### 2.1.3 Semi-Variable Costs
Costs that have both a fixed and a variable element, such as machine maintenance (fixed service contract + variable spare-part wear) or utility charges (fixed connection fee + variable consumption).

## 2.2 The Total Cost Equation (Linear Cost Model)

The simplest and most widely used model in KTU numerical problems is:

$$ \text{Total Cost (TC)} = \text{Fixed Cost (FC)} + \text{Variable Cost per Unit} \times \text{Quantity (Q)} $$

$$ TC = FC + (v \cdot Q) $$

Where:
- $TC$ = Total packaging cost (₹ or $)
- $FC$ = Fixed cost component (₹)
- $v$ = Variable cost per package (₹/unit)
- $Q$ = Number of packages produced (units)

### 2.2.1 Average Cost per Package (AC)

$$ AC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{FC}{Q} + v $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** As $Q \rightarrow \infty$, the term $\frac{FC}{Q} \rightarrow 0$, so $AC \rightarrow v$. This is the principle of **economies of scale** — larger production runs dilute the fixed cost per unit.

## 2.3 Break-Even Analysis (BEA) in Packaging

The break-even point is where the total revenue from selling the packaged product equals the total cost of production and packaging.

$$ \text{Break-Even Quantity (Q*)} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost (FC)}}{\text{Selling Price per unit (P)} - \text{Variable Cost per unit (v)}} $$

$$ Q^* = \frac{FC}{P - v} $$

The denominator $(P - v)$ is called the **Contribution Margin per unit**.

## 2.4 Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) of Packaging Upgrades

When a company considers switching from one packaging format to another (e.g., from glass jar to PET jar), a CBA is performed:

$$ \text{Net Benefit (NB)} = \text{Present Value of Savings} - \text{Present Value of Costs} $$

Discounted cash flow (DCF) version:

$$ NB = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1 + r)^t} - I_0 $$

Where:
- $B_t$ = Benefit (cost savings, revenue uplift) in year $t$
- $C_t$ = Incremental operating cost in year $t$
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically the Weighted Average Cost of Capital, **WACC**)
- $I_0$ = Initial capital investment
- $n$ = Project life (years)

If $NB > 0$, the packaging upgrade is **economically justified**.

## 2.5 Inventory Carrying Cost and Just-In-Time (JIT)

Packaging materials occupy warehouse space. The annual carrying cost is:

$$ \text{Carrying Cost (CC)} = \text{Average Inventory Value} \times i $$

Where $i$ is the carrying cost rate (typically **20% to 30% per annum** for food packaging — combining cost of capital, insurance, obsolescence, and spoilage).

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** packaging procurement minimizes inventory, reducing $CC$, but increases the risk of stockouts.

## 2.6 Packaging Cost as % of Selling Price

A critical industry metric for benchmarking:

$$ \%P = \frac{C_{\text{pkg}}}{P_{\text{sell}}} \times 100 $$

Typical benchmarks:

| Product Category | Typical Packaging % of Selling Price |
| :--- | :--- |
| Milk (pouch) | **3% – 5%** |
| Soft drinks (PET) | **15% – 25%** |
| Premium chocolates | **20% – 35%** |
| Frozen ready meals | **10% – 18%** |
| Pharmaceutical tablets | **8% – 15%** |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU ESE — Module 4 Economics of Packaging**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Packaging Cost | $TC = FC + v \cdot Q$ | $v$ = variable cost per unit |
| 2 | Average Cost per Package | $AC = \frac{FC}{Q} + v$ | Useful for pricing |
| 3 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q^* = \frac{FC}{P - v}$ | $P$ = unit selling price |
| 4 | Contribution Margin | $CM = P - v$ | Per-unit profit before fixed costs |
| 5 | Profit Function | $\pi = (P - v) \cdot Q - FC$ | Used in optimization |
| 6 | Net Benefit (DCF) | $NB = \sum \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | For packaging upgrades |
| 7 | Inventory Carrying Cost | $CC = \text{Inv.} \times i$ | $i \approx 0.20$ to $0.30$ |
| 8 | Packaging-to-Price Ratio | $\%P = \frac{C_{\text{pkg}}}{P_{\text{sell}}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark |
| 9 | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$=demand, $S$=order cost, $H$=holding cost |
| 10 | Payback Period | $PB = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Quick screening tool |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EOQ matters in packaging:** Packaging materials (e.g., corrugated boxes, shrink film rolls) are procured in batches. EOQ minimizes the sum of ordering cost and holding cost — directly impacting packaging economics.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

The principles above are used in:

- **FMCG Companies** (HUL, Nestlé, ITC) — for SKU-level packaging cost optimization.
- **E-commerce Logistics** (Amazon, Flipkart) — for secondary/tertiary packaging economics.
- **Cold Chain Logistics** — where insulation cost vs. spoilage loss is the central trade-off.
- **Sustainable Packaging Design** — where Life Cycle Cost (LCC) replaces simple purchase cost.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging** — where regulatory compliance cost dominates the economic equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Break-Even on Packaging Line

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A food company is launching a new fruit juice in 1 L Tetra Pak cartons. The fixed cost of the packaging line is **₹ 12,00,000 per year**. The variable cost per carton (material + filling + sealing) is **₹ 8**. The selling price of each packaged juice is **₹ 45**. Calculate:

1. The break-even quantity.
2. The profit at a production volume of 1,00,000 cartons/year.
3. The average cost per carton at the break-even volume.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- $FC = ₹\,12{,}00{,}000$
- $v = ₹\,8$ per carton
- $P = ₹\,45$ per carton
- $Q = 1{,}00{,}000$ cartons (for part 2)

**Step 1 — Compute Contribution Margin (CM):**

$$ CM = P - v = 45 - 8 = ₹\,37 \text{ per carton} $$

*Explanation:* Each carton sold contributes ₹37 towards recovering fixed costs and generating profit, after covering its own variable cost.

**Step 2 — Break-Even Quantity:**

$$ Q^* = \frac{FC}{CM} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{37} $$

$$ Q^* = 32{,}432.43 \text{ cartons (approx.)} $$

Since you cannot sell a fraction of a carton, round up to **32,433 cartons/year**.

**Step 3 — Profit at Q = 1,00,000 cartons:**

$$ \pi = (P - v) \cdot Q - FC $$

$$ \pi = 37 \times 1{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ \pi = 37{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,25{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Average Cost at Break-Even:**

$$ AC_{BE} = \frac{FC}{Q^*} + v = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{32{,}433} + 8 $$

$$ AC_{BE} = 37.00 + 8 = ₹\,45 \text{ per carton} $$

*Verification:* At break-even, $AC = P = ₹\,45$ ✓ (the defining property of break-even).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Cost-Benefit of Switching from Glass to PET

**Problem Statement:**
A dairy currently bottles 5,00,000 L of milk per year in glass bottles. Annual glass-bottle cost (procurement + washing + breakage) is **₹ 50,00,000**. Switching to 1 L PET pouches will cost:
- New machine: **₹ 20,00,000** (one-time, 5-year life, no salvage)
- Annual PET film + labor cost: **₹ 15,00,000**

Discount rate $r$ = **10%**. Should the switch be made?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Annual Cash Flows:**

Annual saving (before machine depreciation) = $50{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,35{,}00{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Compute PV of Savings (5-year annuity):**

Annuity factor at $r = 10\%$, $n = 5$:

$$ AF = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} $$

$$ (1.10)^{5} = 1.61051 $$

$$ (1.10)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.61051} = 0.62092 $$

$$ AF = \frac{1 - 0.62092}{0.10} = \frac{0.37908}{0.10} = 3.7908 $$

**Step 3 — Present Value of Annual Savings:**

$$ PV_{\text{savings}} = 35{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 = ₹\,1{,}32{,}67{,}800 $$

**Step 4 — Net Benefit:**

$$ NB = PV_{\text{savings}} - I_0 $$

$$ NB = 1{,}32{,}67{,}800 - 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}12{,}67{,}800 $$

Since $NB > 0$ by a wide margin, **the switch is economically justified**.

**Step 5 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$ PB = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{35{,}00{,}000} = 0.571 \text{ years} $$

$$ PB \approx 6.86 \text{ months} $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Packaging Cost Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU Examinations (Module 4)**

| Strategy | Cost Impact | Risk | Implementation Difficulty | Sustainability Score (1–5) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bulk procurement of packaging material | **High savings** | Stockouts, spoilage | Low | 3 |
| Light-weighting (reduce material gauge) | **Medium savings** | Possible product damage | Medium | 5 |
| Automation of packaging line | **High long-term savings** | High capex, layoff issues | High | 4 |
| Standardization of pack sizes | **Medium savings** | Loss of SKU variety | Low | 4 |
| Reuse / Refill systems | **High long-term savings** | Reverse logistics cost | High | 5 |
| Mono-material packaging (vs laminate) | **Medium savings** | Reduced barrier | Medium | 5 |
| In-line printing (vs label) | **Low–Medium savings** | Setup time | Low | 4 |

## 3.4 Derivation of Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

EOQ minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** for packaging materials:

$$ TIC = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H $$

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units of packaging material)
- $Q$ = Order quantity
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹)

To minimize, differentiate with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$ \frac{d(TIC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 $$

Solving:

$$ \frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2} $$

$$ Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H} $$

$$ \boxed{EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}} $$

This is the **Andress Harris (1915)** formula and is regularly tested in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Cost-Component Flow in a Packaging Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Material Inspection and QC]
    B --> C[Storage in Warehouse]
    C --> D[Issuance to Packaging Line]
    D --> E[Form-Fill-Seal Operation]
    E --> F[Labeling and Coding]
    F --> G[Cartoning and Case Packing]
    G --> H[Palletizing]
    H --> I[Finished Goods Warehouse]
    I --> J[Distribution]

    A1[Material Cost] -.-> A
    B1[Inspection Labor Cost] -.-> B
    C1[Inventory Carrying Cost] -.-> C
    D1[Handling Labor Cost] -.-> D
    E1[Machine Depreciation plus Energy] -.-> E
    F1[Ink and Label Cost] -.-> F
    G1[Carton Cost plus Labor] -.-> G
    H1[Pallet and Stretch Wrap Cost] -.-> H
    I1[FG Storage Carrying Cost] -.-> I
    J1[Transport and Logistics Cost] -.-> J

    J --> K[End Customer]
    K --> L[Returns, Damages, Spoilage Losses]
    L -.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (→)** depict the **physical flow** of the packaged product.
- **Dashed arrows (-.->)** depict **cost attribution** from each stage.
- **Loop L → E** represents the **feedback cost** of damaged/returned packages re-entering the rework line.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Packaging Economics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Cost Inputs]
        FC[Fixed Cost FC]
        VC[Variable Cost v per unit]
        Q[Production Quantity Q]
        P[Selling Price P]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Economic Computation Engine]
        TC[Total Cost TC]
        AC[Average Cost AC]
        BEQ[Break Even Quantity Q star]
        PROF[Profit Function pi]
        EOQ[Economic Order Quantity EOQ]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[Decision Output]
        GOGO[Proceed with Packaging Project]
        HOLD[Hold for Redesign]
        STOP[Discontinue Line]
    end

    FC --> TC
    VC --> TC
    Q --> TC
    TC --> AC
    FC --> BEQ
    P --> BEQ
    VC --> BEQ
    Q --> PROF
    P --> PROF
    VC --> PROF
    FC --> PROF

    BEQ --> DECISION
    PROF --> DECISION
    AC --> DECISION
    EOQ --> DECISION
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Packaging Cost Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[High Packaging Cost Detected] --> Q1{Is material cost the dominant driver?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Lightweighting]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A2[Switch to lower cost material]
    Q1 -- Yes --> A3[Negotiate bulk procurement]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is labor cost dominant?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> B1[Automate bottleneck operation]
    Q2 -- Yes --> B2[Cross train operators]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is logistics cost dominant?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> C1[Optimize pallet configuration]
    Q3 -- Yes --> C2[Switch to regional suppliers]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is waste or rejection high?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> D1[Implement Statistical Process Control]
    Q4 -- Yes --> D2[Root cause analysis on packaging line]
    Q4 -- No --> E[Accept current cost and monitor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Examinations:**
> - Block diagrams in this section map the **functional architecture** of packaging economic decisions.
> - Each node represents a **cost element** or a **decision point**.
> - Decision diamonds guide the **strategy selection** based on which cost driver is dominant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q:** Define **"Packaging-to-Product Cost Ratio (PPR)"**. Why is it considered a key economic indicator in the food packaging industry?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution):**

- **(1 Mark)** Definition: PPR is the ratio of the cost of packaging to the total cost of the finished packaged product, expressed as a percentage:
  $$ PPR = \frac{C_{\text{pkg}}}{P_{\text{sell}}} \times 100 $$
- **(1 Mark)** It indicates **how much of the consumer's rupee** goes into packaging versus the actual food product.
- **(1 Mark)** A higher PPR for low-value foods (e.g., biscuits, salt) signals inefficiency, while a moderate PPR for premium foods (e.g., chocolates, exotic beverages) is justified due to higher protection and marketing value. It helps in **benchmarking** packaging efficiency across SKUs.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q:** List any **three semi-variable costs** encountered in a food packaging operation and briefly explain why each is classified as semi-variable.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **(1 Mark) Machine Maintenance Cost** — Has a fixed component (annual service contract) plus a variable component (spare parts that wear with usage).
2. **(1 Mark) Utility (Electricity) Bill** — Has a fixed connection charge plus variable consumption based on machine hours.
3. **(1 Mark) Quality Control Labor** — Has a fixed minimum QC team plus additional inspectors added during high-volume runs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Valuation Warning:** Students often classify *all* costs as either fixed or variable. Examiners award **zero marks** if semi-variable nature is not recognized. Always state the **fixed component + variable component** structure explicitly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q:** A packaged food company manufactures premium coffee powder in stand-up pouches. The following data is provided for the upcoming year:

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Fixed cost of packaging line | ₹ 25,00,000 per year |
| Variable cost per pouch (material + labor) | ₹ 12 |
| Selling price per pouch | ₹ 95 |
| Annual demand forecast | 2,00,000 pouches |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate:**
1. The break-even quantity in pouches/year.
2. The expected annual profit at the forecast demand.
3. The margin of safety (in units and as a percentage).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Contribution Margin:**

$$ CM = P - v = 95 - 12 = ₹\,83 \text{ per pouch} $$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Quantity:**

$$ Q^* = \frac{FC}{CM} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{83} = 30{,}120.48 $$

Rounded up: **Q* ≈ 30,121 pouches/year**.

**Step 3 — Expected Annual Profit:**

$$ \pi = CM \times Q - FC = 83 \times 2{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 $$

$$ \pi = 1{,}66{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}41{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Margin of Safety (MoS):**

$$ MoS_{\text{units}} = Q - Q^* = 2{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}121 = 1{,}69{,}879 \text{ pouches} $$

$$ MoS_{\%} = \frac{MoS_{\text{units}}}{Q} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}69{,}879}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 84.94\% $$

This indicates **low risk** — sales can drop by nearly 85% before reaching break-even.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The company is evaluating a switch to a fully automated packaging line, which will:**
- Increase fixed cost to ₹ 60,00,000/year.
- Reduce variable cost to ₹ 6/pouch.

**Determine:**
1. The new break-even quantity.
2. The new profit at forecast demand.
3. A clear recommendation with justification.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — New Contribution Margin:**

$$ CM_{\text{new}} = 95 - 6 = ₹\,89 \text{ per pouch} $$

**Step 2 — New Break-Even Quantity:**

$$ Q^*_{\text{new}} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{89} = 67{,}415.73 \approx 67{,}416 \text{ pouches} $$

**Step 3 — New Annual Profit:**

$$ \pi_{\text{new}} = 89 \times 2{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}78{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,1{,}18{,}00{,}000 $$

**Step 4 — Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Metric | Manual Line | Automated Line |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fixed Cost | ₹ 25,00,000 | ₹ 60,00,000 |
| Variable Cost/unit | ₹ 12 | ₹ 6 |
| Break-Even Q | 30,121 | 67,416 |
| Profit @ 2 L | ₹ 1,41,00,000 | ₹ 1,18,00,000 |

> **Recommendation:** If the company can guarantee sales **above ~85,000 units/year**, automation is profitable. At the current forecast of 2,00,000, the **manual line gives higher profit (₹1.41 Cr vs ₹1.18 Cr)** because the savings in variable cost (₹6 × 2 L = ₹1.2 Cr) are outweighed by the additional fixed cost (₹35 L extra). Therefore, **retain manual line at current volume, but plan automation if demand exceeds 85,000 units/year**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Forgetting to **round up** break-even quantity to the next whole unit.
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Not computing **Margin of Safety** as a percentage.
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Giving a recommendation without a **numerical comparison table**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q:** A snack-food company currently uses 3-ply laminated pouches for its chips. The annual packaging material cost is ₹ 80,00,000. A packaging vendor proposes a **mono-material recyclable film** that will:
- Reduce material cost by 25% per year.
- Require a one-time investment of ₹ 30,00,000 in new sealing equipment (life = 5 years, salvage = ₹ 5,00,000).
- Save ₹ 6,00,000/year in product damage (better seal integrity).

Using a discount rate of **12%**, evaluate the proposal using:
1. **Net Present Value (NPV)**
2. **Payback Period**
3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — by linear interpolation between two trial rates.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks (NPV and Payback)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual Cash Inflows:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Material cost saving (25% of 80 L) | 20,00,000 |
| Damage cost saving | 6,00,000 |
| **Total Annual Inflow (Years 1–5)** | **26,00,000** |
| Salvage value at end of Year 5 | 5,00,000 |

**Step 2 — Present Value of Annual Inflows (Annuity Factor at 12%, n=5):**

$$ AF_{12\%,5} = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048 $$

$$ PV_{\text{inflows}} = 26{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048 = ₹\,93{,}72{,}480 $$

**Step 3 — PV of Salvage Value (at end of Year 5):**

$$ PV_{\text{salvage}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^{5}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.7623} = ₹\,2{,}83{,}720 $$

**Step 4 — Total PV of Benefits:**

$$ PV_{\text{benefit}} = 93{,}72{,}480 + 2{,}83{,}720 = ₹\,96{,}56{,}200 $$

**Step 5 — NPV:**

$$ NPV = PV_{\text{benefit}} - I_0 = 96{,}56{,}200 - 30{,}00{,}000 = ₹\,66{,}56{,}200 $$

Since $NPV > 0$, the proposal is **economically viable**.

**Step 6 — Payback Period:**

$$ PB = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{26{,}00{,}000} = 1.154 \text{ years} \approx 13.85 \text{ months} $$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (IRR by Linear Interpolation)

**Model Solution:**

We need the rate $r$ such that $NPV = 0$.

**Trial 1: r = 40%**

$$ AF_{40\%,5} = \frac{1 - (1.40)^{-5}}{0.40} $$

$$ (1.40)^{5} = 5.3782 \Rightarrow (1.40)^{-5} = 0.1859 $$

$$ AF_{40\%,5} = \frac{1 - 0.1859}{0.40} = \frac{0.8141}{0.40} = 2.0353 $$

$$ PV_{\text{inflows at 40\%}} = 26{,}00{,}000 \times 2.0353 = 52{,}91{,}780 $$

$$ PV_{\text{salvage at 40\%}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5.3782} = 92{,}970 $$

$$ PV_{\text{benefit}} = 52{,}91{,}780 + 92{,}970 = 53{,}84{,}750 $$

$$ NPV_{40\%} = 53{,}84{,}750 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +₹\,23{,}84{,}750 \quad (\text{still positive}) $$

**Trial 2: r = 80%**

$$ (1.80)^{5} = 18.8957 \Rightarrow (1.80)^{-5} = 0.05293 $$

$$ AF_{80\%,5} = \frac{1 - 0.05293}{0.80} = \frac{0.94707}{0.80} = 1.1838 $$

$$ PV_{\text{inflows at 80\%}} = 26{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1838 = 30{,}77{,}880 $$

$$ PV_{\text{salvage at 80\%}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{18.8957} = 26{,}460 $$

$$ PV_{\text{benefit}} = 30{,}77{,}880 + 26{,}460 = 31{,}04{,}340 $$

$$ NPV_{80\%} = 31{,}04{,}340 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +₹\,1{,}04{,}340 \quad (\text{still slightly positive}) $$

**Trial 3: r = 85%**

$$ (1.85)^{5} = 21.6615 \Rightarrow (1.85)^{-5} = 0.04617 $$

$$ AF_{85\%,5} = \frac{1 - 0.04617}{0.85} = 1.1225 $$

$$ PV_{\text{inflows}} = 26{,}00{,}000 \times 1.1225 = 29{,}18{,}500 $$

$$ PV_{\text{salvage}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{21.6615} = 23{,}080 $$

$$ PV_{\text{benefit}} = 29{,}41{,}580 $$

$$ NPV_{85\%} = 29{,}41{,}580 - 30{,}00{,}000 = -₹\,58{,}420 \quad (\text{negative}) $$

**Step — Linear Interpolation:**

$$ IRR \approx r_1 + \frac{NPV_{r_1}}{NPV_{r_1} - NPV_{r_2}} \times (r_2 - r_1) $$

$$ IRR \approx 80\% + \frac{1{,}04{,}340}{1{,}04{,}340 - (-58{,}420)} \times (85 - 80) $$

$$ IRR \approx 80\% + \frac{1{,}04{,}340}{1{,}62{,}760} \times 5 \approx 80\% + 3.20\% $$

$$ \boxed{IRR \approx 83.2\%} $$

**Interpretation:** Since $IRR \approx 83.2\% \gg \text{Discount Rate } (12\%)$, the project is **highly economically attractive**. The company should adopt the mono-material recyclable film.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — IRR Problems:**
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Choosing trial rates too close together (e.g., 12% and 14%) — interpolation becomes inaccurate. Use widely spaced trial rates (e.g., 40% and 80%) to bracket the IRR.
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Forgetting to include the **salvage value** in the terminal year cash flow.
> - **(Lose 1 mark)** Not stating the **decision rule** clearly: *Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Economics of Packaging (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Economics of Packaging balances cost, protection, marketing, and sustainability.
- **Cost Classification:**
  - **Fixed Costs** → independent of $Q$ (depreciation, rent, salaries).
  - **Variable Costs** → scale with $Q$ (material, labor, energy).
  - **Semi-Variable Costs** → mixed (maintenance, utilities).
- **Master Equations to Memorize:**
  - $TC = FC + vQ$
  - $AC = \frac{FC}{Q} + v$
  - $Q^* = \frac{FC}{P-v}$
  - $\pi = (P-v)Q - FC$
  - $EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$
  - $NB = \sum \frac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$
- **Key Industry Benchmarks:**
  - Packaging cost = **3%–35%** of selling price (depending on product).
  - Inventory carrying cost rate = **20%–30% per year**.
  - Target FMCG damage rate = **< 0.5%**.
- **Capital Budgeting Tools:**
  - **NPV** → Accept if NPV > 0.
  - **IRR** → Accept if IRR > cost of capital.
  - **Payback Period** → Quick screening only; ignores time value.
- **Cost Reduction Strategies:**
  - Light-weighting, bulk procurement, automation, standardization, mono-materials, refill systems.
- **Common Pitfalls in KTU Exam:**
  - Forgetting to round up $Q^*$.
  - Treating semi-variable costs as fully variable.
  - Computing NPV without salvage value.
  - Confusing EOQ with break-even quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always present a **comparison table** in your recommendation. Examiners reward clarity, structured answers, and explicit numerical justification. Always state the **decision rule** before concluding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation of Different Methods of Packaging Food Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of Different Methods of Packaging Food Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Evaluation of Packaging** is the systematic, scientific assessment of a packaging material or package system against a set of defined functional, mechanical, barrier, chemical, thermal, optical, microbiological, and sensory parameters to determine its **fitness-for-purpose**, regulatory compliance, and ability to protect, preserve, and present the contained food product throughout its intended **shelf-life**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, performance evaluation is the *bridge between material science and food quality assurance* — it converts qualitative descriptors ("good film," "strong pouch") into **quantifiable, reproducible engineering metrics** that can be benchmarked against international standards such as **ASTM (American Society for Testing and Materials)**, **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, **TAPPI (Technical Association of the Pulp and Paper Industry)**, and **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**.

### 1.2 Key Functional Requirements Being Evaluated

A food package is said to perform well when it satisfies the **four pillars** of packaging performance:

| Pillar | Engineering Parameter | Representative Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Containment & Mechanic

topics.title: Their Merits and Demerits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT863 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Their Merits and Demerits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Packaging Materials — The "Big Four"

In Food Packaging Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEAGT863, Module 4), the term **"Their Merits and Demerits"** refers to the comparative evaluation of the four principal **primary packaging materials** used to contain, protect, preserve, and market food products. These four material families are commonly known as the **"Big Four"** of food packaging:

1. **Glass** — Inorganic, amorphous silicate-based rigid container.
2. **Metal** — Predominantly **tinplate steel** and **aluminium** based rigid/semi-rigid containers.
3. **Paper & Paperboard** — Cellulosic, fibrous, often laminated structures.
4. **Plastics (Polymers)** — Organic macromolecular films, sheets, and moulded forms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Students must be able to *compare and justify* the selection of any packaging material for a given food product based on its **functional, mechanical, barrier, economic, environmental, and food-safety** attributes. Examiners specifically look for a balanced **merit-demerit table** style answer.

### 1.2 Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard)

> **"Merits and Demerits of Packaging Materials"** is a systematic comparative assessment of the inherent advantages (functional benefits, protection capability, consumer appeal, recyclability) and disadvantages (cost, brittleness, permeability, environmental burden, migration) of glass, metal, paper, and plastic packaging when applied to food systems, evaluated against parameters such as **mechanical strength, barrier properties (O₂, CO₂, H₂O, light), chemical inertness, thermal resistance, shelf-life contribution, sustainability, and unit cost.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Four Suitcases" Metaphor

Imagine you are packing a fragile, temperature-sensitive, aromatic homemade pickle to send through a courier to your grandmother in another city:

- **Glass suitcase** 🧳 — The strong, airtight, leak-proof, *but heavy and breakable* trunk. Great for jams and beverages, but expensive to ship.
- **Metal suitcase** 🥫 — The indestructible, light-blocking, *but can corrode* tin box. Perfect for canned fish and milk powder.
- **Paper suitcase** 📦 — The lightweight, breathable, recyclable cardboard carton. Good for dry cereals and fast food, but useless for liquids unless laminated.
- **Plastic suitcase** 🛍️ — The versatile, flexible, *but environmentally controversial* pouch. Used for everything from milk to ready-meals, but can leach chemicals and pollutes.

**Each material is the "best" suitcase for a *different journey*.** That is what "merits and demerits" really means in packaging — context-dependent suitability.

### 1.4 The Five Performance Evaluation Parameters

Every packaging material in KTU Module 4 is judged on the same five pillars (must memorize):

| S.No | Parameter | What it Measures |
|:----:|:----------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Barrier Property** | Resistance to O₂, CO₂, moisture vapour, light, aroma |
| 2 | **Mechanical Property** | Tensile strength, burst strength, impact resistance |
| 3 | **Chemical Inertness** | Non-reactivity with food (no migration/leaching) |
| 4 | **Thermal Resistance** | Ability to withstand sterilization, pasteurization, retort |
| 5 | **Sustainability** | Recyclability, biodegradability, carbon footprint |

> [!NOTE]
> A material's **merit in one parameter** is often its **demerit in another** — e.g., paper is *biodegradable* (merit) but has *poor water vapour barrier* (demerit). KTU examiners reward answers that recognize these trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Merits and Demerits — Material-by-Material Analysis

### 2.1.1 🧪 GLASS PACKAGING

**Composition:** Silica (SiO₂ ~ 70–74 %), Soda (Na₂O ~ 12–15 %), Lime (CaO ~ 10–12 %), plus minor additives (Al₂O₃, MgO, B₂O₃ for borosilicate).

#### ✅ Merits of Glass
- **Absolute barrier** — 100 % impermeable to O₂, CO₂, H₂O vapour, and aroma. (Zero permeability).
- **Chemically inert** — Does not react with acidic, alkaline, or alcoholic foods. No leaching.
- **Transparent** — Excellent product visibility; superior consumer appeal.
- **High thermal resistance** — Withstands sterilization, autoclaving, pasteurization, microwave.
- **Rigid & dimensionally stable** — Maintains shape under stacking and vacuum.
- **Reusable & 100 % recyclable** indefinitely without quality loss (closed-loop cullet system).
- **Excellent seal integrity** with metal lug caps, crown corks.
- **Odourless and tasteless** — preserves original food flavour.

#### ❌ Demerits of Glass
- **Brittle & fragile** — Low impact resistance; high breakage rate during transport.
- **Heavy** — Density ~ **2.5 g/cm³** → high shipping cost and carbon footprint.
- **Energy-intensive manufacture** — Melting point ~ **1500 °C**; high embodied energy.
- **Poor thermal shock resistance** — Sudden temperature changes cause cracking.
- **Light transmission** — unless amber/green coloured, allows UV degradation (off-flavour in beer, milk, oils).
- **Higher unit cost** compared to plastics for equivalent volume.
- **Safety hazard** when broken (shards in food).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glass is the gold standard for premium beverages, baby food, and pharmaceuticals precisely because of its absolute inertness — but its weight (≈ 10× heavier than plastic) is its Achilles' heel in modern logistics.**

---

### 2.1.2 🔩 METAL PACKAGING (Tinplate & Aluminium)

**Tinplate:** Low-carbon steel sheet (0.10–0.50 mm) electro-coated with a thin layer of tin (~ 0.4–1.5 µm).
**Aluminium:** Pure Al (99.0–99.5 %) or alloy sheets; often laminated with plastic films.

#### ✅ Merits of Metal
- **Total light barrier** — opaque, protects photosensitive foods (milk powder, beer).
- **Excellent mechanical strength** — resists stacking, denting, impact.
- **Hermetic seal** achievable via double-seam (for cans) — required for retort sterilization.
- **Wide temperature tolerance** — from – 60 °C to + 200 °C; suitable for retort (121 °C) and hot-fill.
- **Long shelf life** — typically **2–5 years** for canned foods.
- **Recyclable** — Aluminium saves ~ 95 % energy on recycling; steel saves ~ 75 %.
- **Good thermal conductivity** — rapid heat transfer during processing.
- **Tamper-evident** — visible deformation is hard to conceal.

#### ❌ Demerits of Metal
- **Corrosion susceptibility** — acidic foods (tomato, citrus) react with tin/iron; require **lacquer/cans lining** (epoxy, BPA-based historically, now BPA-NI or vinyl).
- **Migration concern** — bisphenol A (BPA), tin, iron may leach under certain conditions.
- **Opaque** — consumer cannot see product (label printing required).
- **Higher cost** than paper/PE for dry foods.
- **Denting & buckling** under mechanical stress; affects label aesthetics.
- **Reactivity with sulfur-containing foods** (egg, meat, fish) → black sulfide staining unless sulphur-resistant lacquer is used.
- **Aluminium** is soft and prone to perforation by sharp food edges (bones, spines).

---

### 2.1.3 📄 PAPER & PAPERBOARD PACKAGING

**Paper:** Basis weight < 224 g/m²; Paperboard: > 224 g/m² (carton, folding box); Corrugated board: fluted structure.

#### ✅ Merits of Paper
- **Renewable & bio-based** — made from wood pulp, agricultural residues (bagasse, bamboo).
- **Biodegradable & recyclable** (~ 70 % global paper recycling rate).
- **Lightweight & low density** (~ 0.7–0.9 g/cm³) — low shipping cost.
- **Printable** — excellent surface for high-quality graphics.
- **Low cost** — cheapest of the "Big Four".
- **Stiff & stackable** (paperboard, corrugated) — good for transport.
- **Breathable** — useful for fresh produce, bakery (releases moisture, prevents sogginess).
- **No chemical migration** — generally food-safe.

#### ❌ Demerits of Paper
- **Poor water/moisture resistance** — swells, weakens, delaminates when wet.
- **Low barrier to gases and aroma** — unless laminated with PE, aluminium foil, or PVDC.
- **Low wet strength** — collapses under moisture load.
- **Microbial contamination risk** — porous structure can harbour moulds, bacteria if not sterilized.
- **Not suitable for liquids** unless heavily waxed/coated.
- **Lower mechanical strength** than metal/glass under impact.
- **Optical brightness / bleach chemicals** may leave residues (chlorine bleaching → dioxin concern historically).

---

### 2.1.4 🧬 PLASTIC (POLYMER) PACKAGING
